success_verification.final_conclusion: The ultrahigh-throughput screening-assisted in vivo directed evolution system successfully evolved α-amylase (N473Y, 48.3% improvement) and resveratrol pathway (1.7-fold improvement via plasmid backbone mutations), but limitations include low mutation rate, off-target genomic mutations, and non-coding mutations masking protein improvements.
limitations: Low mutation rate (6.77×10⁻⁸ per base per generation) limits sequence space exploration.
Off-target genomic mutations require plasmid retransformation between rounds.
Beneficial mutations may occur in non-coding regions rather than protein-coding sequences.
metrics: Percentage of cells with >20% activity increase

codon degeneracy can be clearly visible in the DNA sequencing electropherogram (Fig 3), confirming the expected distribution of nucleotides instead of the original codon.
The quality of the randomly mutated library was assessed by sequencing 20 clones (Table 2).

evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen 735 clones from Cal-A libraries using liquid handler robot for halo-based assays.

evidence.quote_or_span: The resulting Cal-A libraries expressed in E. coli BL21 (DE3) were screened to evaluate selectivity of hydrolase activity toward long versus short-chain triglycerides.
For the olive/oil rhodamine plates, the tributyrin was replaced with 2.5 g olive oil, and rhodamine was added at a concentration of 0.001% w/v.
A script was designed to pick up 20 μL of culture, column by column, from the inoculum plates and spot 8 μL of inoculum by lightly touching the agar surface.
A total of 735 clones were screened (Table 1).

evidence.quote_or_span: When Tyr93 was mutated, 89% of the clones retained activity and discriminated preferentially towards long-chain fatty acids.

Phe431 tolerated mutation without little effect, as activity was maintained in 90% of the variants and no increased discrimination was found.

evidence.quote_or_span: DE can be inefficient when mutations exhibit non-additive, or epistatic, behavior.
DE is limited because screening for performance can only explore a small, local region of sequence space.
taking one mutational step at a time can cause the experiment to become stuck at a local optimum, especially on rugged protein fitness landscapes where mutation effects exhibit epistasis
active learning has the potential to unlock improved engineering outcomes

an acquisition function is applied to the trained model to rank all sequences in the design space
the cycle is repeated until fitness is sufficiently optimized

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design workflow with SSM and epPCR libraries, robotic cultivation, and MALDI-ToF MS analysis; choose AlbC as proof-of-concept target.

evidence.quote_or_span: Here we report an automated workflow for directed evolution of new enzymatic activities via high-throughput library creation and label-free MS screening.
For a proof of concept, we chose to engineer a cyclodipeptide synthase (CDPS) that synthesizes diketopiperazine (DKP) compounds with therapeutic potential.
site-saturation mutagenesis (SSM) and error-prone PCR (epPCR) libraries expressing CDPS mutants were automatically created and cultivated on an integrated work cell.
Culture supernatants were then robotically processed for matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight (MALDI-ToF) MS analysis at a rate of 5 s per sample.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design a workflow that can create libraries, cultivate mutants, and perform label-free MS screening at high throughput.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An integrated robotic workcell can automate library creation, cultivation, and MALDI-ToF MS analysis at 5 s per sample, enabling screening of thousands of clones.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Implement the workflow and validate with wild-type AlbC.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Workflow designed to automatically create and cultivate SSM and epPCR libraries, robotically process culture supernatants for MALDI-ToF MS at 5 s per sample, and analyze data with custom algorithms.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform multivariant analysis of 108 theoretical m/z values of 190 possible DKP molecules (mass window 115–373 Da) using one-way ANOVA with Tukey's test.

evidence.quote_or_span: The resulting mass spectral data were processed via custom computational algorithms, which performed a multivariant analysis of 108 theoretical mass-to-charge (m/z) values of 190 possible DKP molecules within a mass window of 115–373 Da.
The peak intensities at the theoretical m/z values of predicted cyclodipeptide ions in each MALDI mass spectrum were analyzed by the one-way ANOVA with Tukey's multiple comparison test.

Notably, by creating and screening $\sim$ 4500 epPCR library members within a week using unlabeled MS on an integrated, robotic workcell, an AlbC mutant producing a new cyclopeptide product was identified, revealing a previously unknown residue (F186) that exerts a substantial impact on substrate specificity.

initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution of enzymes is limited by the lack of high-throughput screening (HTS) assays for new catalytic activities. Mass spectrometry (MS) is label-free and suitable for untargeted discovery but traditionally slow. The goal is to develop an automated, label-free MS screening workflow for directed evolution of new enzymatic activities, demonstrated by engineering a cyclodipeptide synthase (CDPS) to produce new diketopiperazine (DKP) products.

source.title: directed evolution of a cyclodipeptide synthase with new activities via label fr

success_verification.final_conclusion: An automated, label-free MS screening workflow successfully identified an F186L AlbC mutant producing a new DKP product (cFV), demonstrating the feasibility of high-throughput directed evolution for new enzymatic activities.
limitations: Only one mutant (F186L) reported with new activity; other variants not detailed.
Untargeted MS screening requires careful optimization to detect weak signals.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop SelFi, a four-step computational framework.

evidence.quote_or_span: The biggest challenge in directed enzyme evolution is identifying high-throughput screens or selections to isolate the variant(s) with the desired property.
most directed evolution of enzymes are still largely limited by the inability to identify and implement selections or screens.

We present in this paper a computational metabolic engineering framework, Selection Finder (SelFi), to construct a selection pathway from a desired enzymatic product to a cellular host and to couple the pathway with cell survival.

SelFi identifies a selection pathway in four steps. In the first step, SelFi constructs traversal pathways to consume the desired enzymatic reaction product and convert it to a native metabolite within the cellular host.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No computational framework exists to systematically construct selection pathways linking any enzymatic product to host survival.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A computational metabolic engineering framework can construct selection pathways from a desired enzymatic product to a cellular host and couple the pathway with cell survival.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to detail the four steps of SelFi.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SelFi is designed to identify selection pathways in four steps: construct consumption pathways, eliminate alternate carbon sources, identify knockout targets, rank pathways.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Directed enzyme evolution lacks generic high-throughput selections.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement Step 1: use retroProPath to construct consumption pathways.

evidence.quote_or_span: We reverse ProPath's search direction to identify a pathway starting from a compound of interest to an endogenous metabolite within the host. We refer to the use of the algorithm in this reversed manner as retroProPath.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to construct consumption pathways from product to host metabolite.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Reversing ProPath algorithm (retroProPath) can identify pathways from desired product to endogenous metabolite.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to couple pathway with survival by eliminating alternate carbon sources.
evolution_trajectory.observation: retroProPath constructs traversal pathways from desired product to native metabolite.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: SelFi framework outlined with four steps.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement Step 2: eliminate alternate carbon sources.

evidence.quote_or_span: SelFi therefore eliminates all organic carbon uptakes except for the uptake provided through the consumption pathway, and inorganic $CO_{2}$ , an essential waste product.
In some cases, the reactant of the specified enzymatic reaction is native to the host, and limiting carbon uptake to be only through the consumption pathway is not possible.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Pathway not yet coupled to survival; host can use other carbon sources.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Eliminating all organic carbon uptakes except the consumption pathway and CO2 will couple pathway to survival.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Limitation: when reactant is native, eliminating carbon sources does not couple pathway to survival; need knockouts.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Carbon sources eliminated except consumption pathway and CO2.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Consumption pathways constructed.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement Step 3: sequential greedy knockout strategy with max 3 knockouts.

evidence.quote_or_span: In the second and third steps, SelFi identifies the minimal set of carbon sources and knockout targets required to link cell survival with the consumption pathway and to guarantee a minimum flux.
To identify possible knockout targets, SelFi utilizes a sequential greedy strategy.
SelFi explores knocking out one reaction at a time using FBA to calculate the minimum flux through the consumption pathway.

SelFi continues to find an additional knockout target that improves the flux, repeating this process until the maximum allowed number of knockouts, as specified by the user, is reached. In this work, we set the number of knockouts to three to limit the computational cost associated with evaluating each consumption pathway.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to guarantee minimum flux through consumption pathway via knockouts.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A sequential greedy knockout strategy can identify minimal set of knockouts to guarantee minimum flux.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to rank resulting pathways.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Knockout targets identified to guarantee minimum flux.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Carbon source elimination insufficient when reactant is native.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement Step 4: rank pathways based on flux, length, and knockouts.

evidence.quote_or_span: In the last step, SelFi ranks the resulting pathways based on flux, pathway length, and number of required knockouts.

Ideally, candidate pathways are chosen based on the guaranteed minimum consumption flux, pathway length, and number of required knockouts. Shorter, higher-consumption flux pathways with the smallest number of knockouts are preferable over others.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Multiple pathways possible; need to select best ones.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ranking pathways by minimum consumption flux, pathway length, and number of knockouts will identify optimal selection pathways.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply SelFi to test cases.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Pathways ranked; shorter, higher-flux pathways with fewer knockouts preferred.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Knockout targets identified.

Comparative analysis against a control resin identified 1043 compounds that exhibited significant enrichment (enrichment ratio > 1).

success_verification.final_conclusion: Flubendazole identified as most potent dual CD38 inhibitor (IC50 14.78 µM hydrolase, 26.31 µM cyclase); SAR highlights rigid aromatic R1 and methoxycarbamide R2 as critical; limitations include micromolar potency and lack of in vivo data.
limitations: Only extracellular CD38 targeted
Micromolar potency
No cellular or in vivo data
Oxibendazole cyclase data compromised by fluorescence interference

initial_request.known_context: Heme is a valuable compound with applications in food, medicine, and research. Its biosynthesis in E. coli requires balanced expression of hemBCDEFY genes and efficient conversion of PPIX to heme by FECH. Previous attempts have been hindered by pathway imbalance and low FECH activity.
quantifiable_goals: Increase PPIX titer
Increase heme titer
Achieve high heme yield in fed-batch fermentation
initial_request.research_problem: Heme biosynthesis in E. coli is limited by unbalanced expression of multiple pathway genes, complex regulatory mechanisms, and low enzymatic activity of protoporphyrin ferrochelatase (FECH).
initial_request.target_object: Heme biosynthetic pathway and FECH enzyme

source.title: Heme biosensor-guided in vivo pathway optimization and directed evolution for efficient biosynthesis of heme

success_verification.final_conclusion: The biosensor-guided approach achieved 160.8 mg/L PPIX and 127.6 mg/L heme in fed-batch, demonstrating effective pathway optimization and FECH evolution.
limitations: Heme yield of 127.6 mg/L is still far from commercialization; further optimization needed.
validation_methods: Shake-flask fermentation for PPIX and heme titers
Fed-batch fermentation for final heme titer

Low number of mutations per variant

evidence.quote_or_span: we show that our approach reduces the wet-lab burden to identify optimal GB1 designs by 67%, relative to the results presented in [4] on the same data.
a Bayesian approach only required 191 fitness value acquisitions (20 initial observations plus 9 batches of 19) to identify designs with high fitness values

quantifiable_goals: Improve target property over traditional DE

initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution optimizes a target property but may degrade unmeasured properties (e.g., solubility, thermostability). ML-assisted DE models are myopic, exacerbating this trade-off.
initial_request.target_object: Protein variants with improved target property while maintaining unmeasured native-like properties

source.title: Bayesian optimization with evolutionary and structure-based regularization for directed protein evolution

success_verification.final_conclusion: Structure-based regularization usually improves designs and almost never hurts; evolutionary-based regularization via TPLM is unreliable. Bayesian approach reduces wet-lab burden by 67% on GB1.
limitations: BRCA1 and Spike datasets only include single-site mutations, limiting traditional DE simulation to GB1.
FoldX predictions may be less accurate without complete structural context (e.g., missing binding partner for GB1).
metrics: Reduction in wet-lab burden (67% fewer sequences tested)
validation_methods: Comparison of acquisition counts with prior non-Bayesian ML-assisted DE on GB1

evidence.quote_or_span: an sgRNA targeting the gene MAP2K1 (which encodes the protein MEK1) at the site encoding K59 generated the strongest drug-resistance phenotype for both vemurafenib and selumetinib
We hypothesized that this unexpected result was the consequence of NHEJ-mediated repair of the sgRNA cut site, which led to the creation of drug resistant variants of MEK1.

To validate the activity of the variants produced by the K59 sgRNA, we synthesized ORFs with these deletions, introduced them into A375 cells via lentivirus, and treated with selumetinib and vemurafenib.

evidence.quote_or_span: The sgRNA MAP2K1/BRAF mini-library was screened in three biological replicates in each of two cell lines.
Vemurafenib (PLX-4032, Selleckchem S1267) was screened at 2 $\mu$ M, and selumetinib (AZD-6244, Selleckchem S1008) was screened at 1.5 $\mu$ M.

Cells were selected for 5 days with puromycin (1 $\mu$ g/mL for A375 and 2 $\mu$ g/mL for MEL-JUSO) to remove uninfected cells, then each population of transduced cells were split to vemurafenib (2 $\mu$ M), selumetinib (1.5 $\mu$ M), and unselected arms.

Due to the strong positive selection that occurs in the presence of the small molecules, however, some populations fell below this bottleneck; in this case all surviving cells were reseeded at each passage.
Throughout the screen, cells were split at a density to maintain a representation of $\sim$ 500 cells per sgRNA.

However, these distinct contexts facilitated the identification of MAP2K1 variants in A375 cells and BRAF variants in MEL-JUSO cells.
These results emphasize the impact of cellular context on variant characterization; both A375 and MEL-JUSO cells harbor activating mutations in the RAS/RAF/MEK signaling cascade.

We speculate that A375 cells, with the BRAF V600E mutation, are more reliant on MAPK signaling and are thus a more sensitive model system in which to find MAP2K1 mutations, whereas the NRAS Q61L mutation activates additional survival pathways in MEL-JUSO cells.

initial_request.known_context: Previous genome-wide loss-of-function screens in A375 melanoma cells identified sgRNAs that sensitize to MAPK inhibitors. Unexpectedly, an sgRNA targeting MAP2K1 K59 conferred strong drug resistance.
quantifiable_goals: Identify drug-resistant variants
Characterize allelic diversity

initial_request.research_problem: Deciphering functional consequences of DNA variation by creating novel protein variants using CRISPR/Cas9-mediated mutagenesis, turning a screening by-product into a discovery tool.

source.title: Creation of Novel Protein Variants with CRISPR/Cas9-Mediated Mutagenesis: Turning a Screening By-Product into a Discovery Tool

success_verification.final_conclusion: CRISPR/Cas9-mediated NHEJ mutagenesis can generate diverse protein variants, enabling discovery of drug-resistant alleles. Cellular context significantly influences which variants are identified.
limitations: Cellular context impacts variant identification (A375 vs. MEL-JUSO)
Bottlenecking due to strong positive selection
Modest enrichment of some variants (e.g., BRAF Q494 deletion 3.2-fold)

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a method that uses ensemble zero-shot fitness prediction and Pareto optimization to balance fitness and diversity.

evidence.quote_or_span: Due to the scarcity of fitness data for new-to-nature enzyme functions, training of supervised ML models to guide such directed evolution efforts is challenging.
central to the successful engineering of new-to-nature enzymes is the design of effective starting libraries without relying on experimentally determined enzyme fitness
an effective starting library should achieve two desiderata–high fitness and rich diversity.

a diverse starting library enriched with distinct functional variants will allow ML models to more efficiently map out the fitness landscape, ultimately enhancing the efficiency of downstream ML-guided directed evolution (MLDE).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to design a starting library that balances high fitness and rich diversity to enable discovery of functional variants and efficient downstream MLDE.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An effective starting library should achieve high fitness and rich diversity; co-optimizing both using zero-shot fitness predictions and diversity metrics will yield superior libraries.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design an algorithm that implements this hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Prior work shows that diverse libraries enriched with functional variants improve MLDE efficiency, but no method jointly optimizes fitness and diversity for cold-start scenarios.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: No effective method to design starting libraries for new-to-nature enzyme functions without experimental fitness data.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design MODIFY: ensemble predictor (ESM-1v, ESM-2, EVmutation, EVE, MSA Transformer) with z-score normalization and averaging; library design as maximizing expected fitness plus weighted diversity with residue-level control via α_i hyperparameters.

evidence.quote_or_span: MODIFY applies a novel ensemble ML model that leverages protein language models (PLMs) and sequence density models to make zero-shot fitness predictions and employs a Pareto optimization scheme to design libraries with both high expected fitness and high diversity.
we formulate the library design problem as an optimization problem of maximizing the expected fitness of sequences sampled by the library and the library's sequence diversity.
we delineate the sequence-level diversity into residue-level resolution.
the library diversity diversity(p) in our framework is defined as the sum of site-wise entropy of the distribution over AAs

The ensemble ML model of MODIFY was compared with five state-of-the-art unsupervised protein fitness predictors (ESM-1v, ESM-2, EVmutation, EVE, and MSA Transformer) for zero-shot protein fitness predictions.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to computationally model fitness and diversity and optimize them jointly.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An ensemble of unsupervised models can provide robust zero-shot fitness predictions, and Pareto optimization can balance expected fitness and site-wise entropy-based diversity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate ensemble predictor on benchmark datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Formulated optimization objective: maximize expected fitness + weighted sum of site-wise entropies. Residue-level diversity control allows tuning per site.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated but no concrete algorithm.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on 87 ProteinGym DMS datasets using Spearman correlation.

evidence.quote_or_span: MODIFY's ensemble predictor stood out by delivering accurate and robust predictions (Fig. 2a) and achieving the best Spearman correlation for the largest number of times (34/87; Fig. 2b).
Across ProteinGym, MODIFY consistently outperformed at least one of the baselines in all 87 DMS datasets
we found that, while none of these existing models consistently prevails, our ensemble model is a robust predictor and outperforms others most of the time
including all individual models led to the best prediction performance

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the ensemble outperform individual models on zero-shot fitness prediction?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The ensemble will be more robust and accurate than any single model across diverse proteins.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Further validate on high-order mutants and across MSA depths.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MODIFY achieved best Spearman correlation in 34/87 datasets and consistently outperformed at least one baseline in all datasets. Ensemble including all models performed best.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: MODIFY ensemble predictor designed but not validated.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze performance on GB1, ParD3, CreiLOV high-order landscapes and stratify ProteinGym by MSA depth.

evidence.quote_or_span: stratifying results based on the MSA depth of parent sequences in ProteinGym indicated that MODIFY outperformed all baselines for proteins across low, medium, and high MSA depths (Fig. 2c; Supplementary Information A.1).
MODIFY achieved notable performance improvements over other baselines, suggesting its generalizability in predicting the fitness of high-order mutants (Supplementary Fig. 3).

evidence.quote_or_span: the MODIFY library exhibited higher mean experimental fitness (Fig. 3c) and preserved library diversity as indicated by average entropy (Fig. 3d)
Exploitation, a variation of MODIFY that only prioritized variants by zero-shot fitness prediction with no consideration of diversity, resulted in a less diverse library (Fig. 3c,d)
MODIFY's improvements were consistently observed across varying library sizes (Fig. 3e)

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop MODIFY-informed setting: use experimental single-mutant data to adjust α_i at D40, allowing beneficial mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: MODIFY predicted all 19 possible substitutions at this site to be disadvantageous, while experiment data indicated the opposite for most mutations (Fig. 3f, g)
we explored MODIFY's informed setting (denoted as MODIFY-informed; Fig. 1b) and showed how prior knowledge of a protein's fitness can be incorporated through MODIFY's residue-level diversity control

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: At GB1 position D40, MODIFY predicted all mutations deleterious, but experiments showed many beneficial. How to correct this?

evolution_trajectory.observation: MODIFY-informed setting corrected the misprediction at D40, improving fitness at that site.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: MODIFY library outperforms on GB1 overall, but local mispredictions exist.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Simulate MLDE on GB1: train ML models on each library and evaluate mean/max fitness and recall of top 100 variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: the MODIFY library contained variants enriched for multiple fitness peaks (Fig. 4f), suggesting a Pareto optimal library with higher mean fitness than the NNK library (Fig. 4b) and higher diversity than the FoldX, FuncLib, and Exploitation libraries (Fig. 4c–e).
The ML model trained on the MODIFY library outperformed all others, exhibiting the highest mean/max fitness and recall for high-fitness variants (Fig. 4h–j).

although the FoldX library and the FuncLib library contained a higher fraction of better-than-wildtype variants (Fig. 4g), the ML model trained on the libraries performed consistently the worst with regard to mean/max fitness and recall (Fig. 4h–j).
Moreover, functional double-proline mutants with a proline at residues 99, 100, 101, and 102, including MLYPPT, MPQPNQ, VQFPPQ, KPWPNY, and SPIPAM, were uncovered from this MODIFY library.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the MODIFY library enable better MLDE than other library design methods?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: MODIFY library, with balanced fitness and diversity, will yield ML models with higher mean/max fitness and recall of top variants compared to NNK, FoldX, FuncLib, and Exploitation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate experimentally on a real enzyme system.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MODIFY library yielded ML models with highest mean/max fitness and recall. FoldX and FuncLib had high fraction of better-than-wildtype variants but low diversity, leading to poor MLDE performance.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: MODIFY and MODIFY-informed libraries designed, but downstream MLDE performance unknown.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a 1000-variant MODIFY library for Rma cyt c, synthesize via oligo-pool, and screen for C-B and C-Si activity.

A DNA oligo pool (141 bp) containing 1000 sequences designed by MODIFY was ordered from Twist Bioscience

In C-Si bond formation, MODIFY library provided a 1.9-fold higher averaged yield and a 1.3-fold higher averaged enantiomeric ratio (Fig. 5j).

evolution_trajectory.observation: Library constructed and screened. For C-B, MODIFY library gave 2.2-fold higher averaged yield and 4-fold higher e.r. vs NNK. For C-Si, 1.9-fold higher yield and 1.3-fold higher e.r.

evidence.quote_or_span: the MGAANQ variant displayed a TTN of 2880 and an e.r. of 95:5 (Fig. 5g, entry 2) outperforming the experimentally evolved RDT variant (Fig. 5g, entry 1).
the SFLTNQ variant displayed a TTN of 3,320 and an e.r. of 98:2 (Fig. 5h, entry 2), outperforming the experimentally evolved TDE variant (Fig. 5h, entry 1).

we identified a generalist Rma cyt c variant MLYPPT, which is highly active and enantioselective for both the borylation (2880 TTN, 94:6 e.r. (Fig. 5g, entry 3)) and the silylation (2,740 TTN, 97:3 e.r. (Fig. 5h, entry 6)) reactions

Enable MLDE to achieve high mean/max fitness and recall of top variants

source.title: Machine learning-guided co-optimization of fitness and diversity facilitates combinatorial library design in enzyme engineering

success_verification.final_conclusion: MODIFY successfully designs starting libraries that balance fitness and diversity, enabling discovery of highly active and enantioselective variants for new-to-nature reactions, including a generalist variant MLYPPT.
limitations: Cold-start setting relies solely on zero-shot predictions, which can mispredict at certain sites (e.g., GB1 D40).
Single-round screening may not capture full potential; iterative MLDE could further improve.

Structure-based filtering (pLDDT, ΔΔG) adds computational cost and may discard functional variants.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a protocol for assaying combinatorial effects of several mutations at once.

evidence.quote_or_span: introducing single mutations one at a time and individually testing for changes in function is a tedious and time-intensive process

The protocol described herein eliminates the bottleneck of individually testing variants of genes and promoters (generated by site-directed mutagenesis) by assaying the combinatorial effect of several mutations at once, as part of a large multi-variant library.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to rapidly test many mutations simultaneously?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combinatorial testing of multiple mutations in a large library can eliminate the bottleneck.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design a method to create such combinatorial libraries.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The protocol eliminates the bottleneck by assaying combinatorial effects in a large multi-variant library.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Individual testing of single mutations is a bottleneck.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use a combination of overlap extension PCR and saturation/partial saturation mutagenesis with degenerate primers.

evidence.quote_or_span: The method described here uses a combination of overlap extension Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) (Ho et al., 1989; Horton et al., 1989) and saturation or partial saturation mutagenesis with degenerate primers (Kretz et al., 2004) to produce a library of gene and promoter variants
These oligos can then be used in a two-step PCR (fragment generation followed by assembly of fragments) to rapidly generate libraries with diversity on the order of $10^{4}-10^{7}$ variants.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate a library of 10^4-10^7 variants with mutations at specific sites?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Overlap extension PCR with degenerate primers can create combinatorial libraries of desired size.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need a high-throughput screening method to isolate desired variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The method can produce a library of gene and promoter variants.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: No method to generate large combinatorial libraries with targeted mutations.

tool_or_method.name: Overlap extension PCR with degenerate primers

evolution_trajectory.decision: Couple the library to a fluorescent reporter and use FACS for screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: The resulting libraries lend themselves to high-throughput screening via fluorescence activated cell sorting (FACS), when coupled to a relevant fluorescent reporter, allowing for the rapid identification and isolation of variants of interest

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to rapidly screen millions of variants for gain-of-function?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: FACS with a fluorescent reporter can enable high-throughput screening.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to decide which regions to target for mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Libraries lend themselves to high-throughput screening via FACS, allowing rapid identification and isolation of variants of interest.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library generation method exists, but screening is needed.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt a semi-rational design targeting specific promoter regions.

evidence.quote_or_span: semi-rational refers to the approach of targeting specific regions of the promoter, known to be involved with various mechanisms of transcription or translation, rather than just randomly mutating the entire promoter region.

For transcription, the regions approximately 35 and 10 bases upstream of the transcriptional initiation site (referred to as -35/ -10 promoter sites) are particularly important for transcription initiation and therefore a single nucleotide mutation here can have dramatic effects (Einav and Phillips, 2019).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which specific regions of promoters should be targeted for effective gain-of-function mutations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Targeting known functional regions (-35/-10, operator, RBS) will yield more useful variants than random mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: For proteins, structural data can guide targeting; need to incorporate that.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Semi-rational approach targets regions known to be involved in transcription or translation.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: General library generation and screening approach defined.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use available structural information and computational tools to identify key residues.

evidence.quote_or_span: When targeting which sequences to randomize, one may take either a random, semi-rational or fully rational approach. This is frequently determined by the amount of information available for the protein of interest
Tertiary structures can also be predicted for proteins with a known sequence using computational approaches such as Rosetta (Rohl et al., 2004) or AlphaFold (Jumper et al., 2021).
In silico docking consisting of protein-protein or protein-ligand interactions (Lyskov and Gray, 2008; Lyskov et al., 2013) permits guided selection of amino acids involved in binding.

Targeting only very relevant residues with computationally informed mutations, or conservative mutations such as neutral drift mutations (Lynch and Hill, 1986), can simplify library generation, reduce library size, and decrease noise and workload.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to select residues for mutagenesis in proteins?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Structural data (crystal structures, AlphaFold) and in silico docking can guide targeted mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design the actual mutation library construction process.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Targeting only very relevant residues with computationally informed mutations can simplify library generation and reduce noise.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Semi-rational design for promoters established; need analogous strategy for proteins.

tool_or_method.name: Structural analysis and in silico docking

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design primers with degenerate bases at mutation sites, separate sequence into fragments, and assemble via overlap PCR.

evidence.quote_or_span: A number of residues are targeted for mutagenesis around the area of interest
The protein sequence is then separated into several fragments with the mutation sites located at one end of each fragment.

Each fragment will be generated by PCR reactions consisting of the template to be mutated and a pair of primers. One of those primers (the mutation primer) includes degenerate bases in the middle to effect mutation.

The method described here introduces a variety of mutations at each mutation site by performing PCR-based, site directed mutagenesis, with primers containing degenerate bases at the desired mutation site.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to introduce mutations at multiple sites and assemble full-length variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fragmenting the sequence and using degenerate primers with overlap extension PCR will allow combinatorial assembly.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to incorporate a substitution matrix to guide conservative mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: This approach introduces a variety of mutations at each site and enhances diversity by combinatorial assembly.

parameters.fragments: separated with mutation sites at ends
parameters.primer_design: degenerate bases at mutation sites, 20 bp overlaps

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Target residues identified; need to design primers and assembly strategy.

tool_or_method.name: PCR-based site-directed mutagenesis with degenerate primers and overlap extension PCR

evolution_trajectory.decision: Refer to BLOSUM62 matrix when designing degenerate primers.

evidence.quote_or_span: When designing the primers, it is helpful to refer to an amino acid substitution matrix, such as the Block Substitution Matrix 62 (BLOSUM62) table

In the case of the BLOSUM62 matrix, the scores shown were determined based on sequences with an identity of 62% or less – making it useful for generating variants that are dissimilar to the starting sequence, but not entirely divergent

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to avoid entirely divergent mutations that are likely deleterious?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using BLOSUM62 substitution matrix will guide conservative mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to validate that in silico docking can successfully guide library design.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BLOSUM62 is useful for generating variants that are dissimilar but not entirely divergent.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Cite successful examples from literature.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Design principles established; need evidence that the approach works.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use Gibson assembly to clone the assembled library into a vector.

evidence.quote_or_span: The resulting gene or promoter library is then cloned into a vector by Gibson assembly
their combination is rare within the mutation library field, despite offering superior flexibility, control of mutation bias, and ease of use

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to combine PCR mutagenesis, overlap PCR, and cloning efficiently?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining PCR-based site-directed mutagenesis, overlap extension PCR, and Gibson assembly offers superior flexibility and control.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to acknowledge limitations of alternative methods and the method itself.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The combination is rare but offers superior flexibility, control of mutation bias, and ease of use.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Individual components validated; need to integrate into a complete workflow.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Highlight advantages: reduced deleterious mutations and larger library sizes.

evidence.quote_or_span: Without designing a bias towards potentially useful mutations, it is expected that a much larger percentage of the mutations will be deleterious, which requires screening or selecting from a larger sampling of the population.

Targeted mutagenesis approaches, such as site directed mutagenesis (SDM) and site-saturation mutagenesis (SSM) have proven to be extremely useful, however, the relatively low library size that can be achieved through these methods has restricted their application in directed evolution approaches

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does this method compare to random mutagenesis and existing targeted methods?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Random mutagenesis produces more deleterious mutations; existing targeted methods have low library sizes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to address a specific limitation: degenerate primer bias.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Random mutagenesis requires screening larger populations; SDM/SSM have restricted library sizes.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Core method defined; need to compare with existing methods.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Include a verification step: sequence random clones or whole library.

evidence.quote_or_span: the different sequences may show differences in annealing during PCR resulting in a degree of bias

this protocol includes a verification step of either sequencing a random selection of clones isolated from the library (see Figure 6A), or sequencing DNA extracted from the whole library (see Figure 6B), for quality assurance

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to ensure library quality and diversity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sequencing verification can detect bias and confirm desired mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to acknowledge another limitation: requirement of prior knowledge.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sequencing ensures quality assurance by verifying mutations and diversity.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Degenerate primers may introduce bias due to differential annealing.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge these limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: This method is additionally limited by availability of knowledge about the targeted protein or promoter
Still, the high price, compared to other techniques, makes this technique appropriate for only some specific applications.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the inherent limitations of this targeted approach?

evolution_trajectory.observation: Method is limited by availability of knowledge about the targeted protein or promoter; DNA synthesis is still expensive for some applications.

initial_request.research_problem: Systematically identify key residues in full-length SARS-CoV-2 spike protein that alter ACE2-binding affinity and monoclonal antibody evasion using a mammalian cell surface-display platform.

source.title: High-throughput screening of spike variants uncovers the key residues that alter the affinity and antigenicity of SARS-CoV-2

success_verification.final_conclusion: Identified ten key RBD residues for ACE2 affinity; N-glycan mutants increase affinity but reduce infectivity; eight mutations increase infectivity 3–12-fold; Q493A/M, Y453F, Q498Y/H confer mAb resistance; Q498Y evades serum antibodies.
limitations: Recombinational effect of multiple sites is complex; next variant hard to predict
Further screening of Omicron sequence needed
In vitro findings may not fully recapitulate in vivo infection
validation_methods: Individual ACE2 binding assays for top candidates

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop ACIDES, a method that combines statistical inference and in-silico simulations.

evidence.quote_or_span: the vast and noisy nature of the sequencing data impedes the estimation of the performance of individual variants

in order to rank the variants and to identify the best performing ones, the score should come with a precise estimation of its statistical error. As a consequence of the noise in the counts, some irrelevant variants might appear to be highly enriched (winner's curse)

when running DE over multiple rounds, it is hard to know when to end the experiment: performing too few rounds could lead to selection of weak variants, not representative of their true ranking. On the other hand, performing too many rounds is costly, time-consuming and even ethically questionable

we propose ACIDES that combines statistical inference and in-silico simulations to improve performance estimation in the library selection process by attributing accurate statistical scores to individual variants

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Noisy NGS count data hinders accurate estimation of variant performance and convergence detection.

evidence.quote_or_span: ACIDES does not account for changes in the selection pressure over rounds.
ACIDES uses a negative binomial model to describe the dispersion of count data by assuming that the count variance depends only on the frequency of the variant.
it is possible that dispersions induced by a sequence-dependent procedure, such as error-prone PCR
statistical errors due to the replicates that do not share the same initial library cannot be described by ACIDES, provided that the model is only trained on a single series of screening rounds.

initial_request.known_context: NGS data from forward genetic screens are vast and noisy, impeding estimation of variant performance. Scores need precise statistical error estimation to avoid winner's curse. It is hard to know when to end the experiment: too few rounds may select weak variants, too many rounds are costly.
initial_request.research_problem: Noisy NGS count data in forward genetic screens hinders accurate estimation of variant performance and convergence detection, necessitating a method for robust scoring, error estimation, and on-line monitoring.

source.title: Directed Evolution of Pseudomonas fluorescens Lipase Variants With Improved Thermostability Using Error-Prone PCR

success_verification.final_conclusion: Error-prone PCR yielded PFL mutants L218P and P184C/M243C with Tm increases of 2.5°C and 6°C. Combined triple mutant P184C/M243C/L218P showed additive effect with Tm 68.0°C (+8°C) and 2.2-fold half-life increase. Structural analysis revealed disulfide bond and proline substitution reduce flexibility. Mutants also showed increased methanol tolerance. However, Tm increase was modest and structural analysis relied on a homology model.
limitations: Modest Tm increase (8°C) may not be sufficient for all industrial applications.
Structural analysis based on homology model.

success_verification.final_conclusion: The FACS-based screening using the pSenSox biosensor successfully identified the LbADH^{K71E} variant with improved catalytic properties for 2,5-hexanedione reduction and broader substrate scope. evidence.quote_or_span: We reasoned that if this electrostatic-based mechanism for 'fine tuning' of the emission wavelength is indeed operative in mTFP1, variants with alternative chromophore structures, should also be blue-shifted relative to their avGFP analogs.

So is His163 solely responsible for the blue-shift of mTFP1 or does His197 also play a role? That is, does the emission maximum of 503 nm for mTFP1-H163M represent an appropriate 'reference' state for this particular chromophore structure when located in this particular chromophore cavity and in the complete absence of any blue-shifting effects? Previous work suggests that the answer to the latter question is probably no, and the emission maximum of the reference state is more likely to be approximately 515 nm.

initial_request.known_context: mTFP1 is a monomeric cyan fluorescent protein with blue-shifted emission relative to avGFP-derived CFPs. The molecular basis of this blue-shift is hypothesized to involve electrostatic interactions from His163 and His197.
quantifiable_goals: Create hue-shifted variants with distinct emission wavelengths
Achieve brightness and photostability comparable to or better than existing FPs like EGFP
initial_request.research_problem: Engineering hue-shifted monomeric variants of Clavularia cyan fluorescent protein (mTFP1) to expand the FP toolkit for live-cell imaging, and identifying the molecular determinants of its blue-shifted emission.

source.title: Hue-shifted monomeric variants of Clavulariacyan fluorescent protein: identification of the molecular determinants of color and applications in fluorescence imaging

success_verification.final_conclusion: Successfully engineered mWasabi, a bright green FP with narrow spectra suitable for multicolor imaging, and validated mTFP1 and mWasabi as fusion tags. Identified His163 and His197 as key determinants of blue-shift in mTFP1.
limitations: mWasabi photostability (93 s) is lower than EGFP (174 s)
mTFP1-Y67H is non-fluorescent, limiting analysis of blue-shift with histidine-derived chromophore
validation_methods: Two-color imaging with Sapphire to assess bleed-through
Fusion protein localization in live cells

evolution_trajectory.decision: Express, purify, and characterize 88 unique designs.

evidence.quote_or_span: we expressed, purified, and characterized a total of 88 unique designs
17 of which (71%) were active
we selected six designs with the maximal number of mutations (12–14), but none of these was functional

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to experimentally validate functionality of designs.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Purified designs will show high activity, but maximal mutation designs may fail.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use random forest to recover false negatives from deep sequencing.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 71% of cluster representatives active; designs with 12–14 mutations non-functional.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Epistatic interactions identified.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test 19 designs predicted functional but not observed in deep sequencing.

evidence.quote_or_span: we selected 19 designs that were predicted to be functional by the random forest analysis but were not observed among the positive hits in the deep-sequencing analysis. Surprisingly, 15 (79%) were active, confirming that a random forest analysis based on deep-sequencing data of htFuncLib designs can be used to recover false negatives

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Deep sequencing may miss functional designs (false negatives).
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Random forest can recover false negatives.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze structural basis for stability and quantum yield differences.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 15 of 19 (79%) were active, confirming random forest recovers false negatives.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Purification results show high activity but failure of maximal mutants.

tool_or_method.name: random forest prediction and experimental validation

evolution_trajectory.decision: Examine design models for molecular explanations.

evidence.quote_or_span: We examined the design models for a molecular explanation of the large observed differences in stability and quantum yield.
Ser205Thr is enriched in designs with high thermostability and quantum yield though we are unaware of previous studies that pointed to its significance.
Many of the active designs exhibit radical mutations, including Thr203His (13%), Gln69Met (9%), Ser205Asp (9%), Gln94Leu (8%), and Tyr145Met (8%) (Supplementary Dataset 6).
For instance, Tyr145Phe, seen previously to enhance stability and quantum yield $^{47}$ , appeared in all five high stability/brightness designs but only in one of the bottom 26 designs.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to explain differences in stability and quantum yield.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Key mutations like Tyr145Phe, Thr203His, and Ser205Thr contribute to stability and quantum yield.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess photostability variability among designs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Ser205Thr enriched in high thermostability and quantum yield designs; Tyr145Phe and Thr203His also key.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: False negatives recovered.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure photostability of selected designs.

evidence.quote_or_span: We also observed large variability in photostability, which is the resistance of the chromophore to bleaching by bright light.
We isolated two designs that exhibited higher photostability than GFP (photostable.1 & photostable.2, with seven mutations each from PROSS-eGFP) and many significantly less photostable designs
At the extremes, design fast.4 (6 mutations) photobleaches tenfold faster than GFP, while the design photostable.1 requires 122% of that time.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to assess photostability variability among designs.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Photostability varies widely among designs, with some more photostable than GFP and others less.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude on functional diversity and epistasis verification.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Large variability in photostability observed; isolated photostable.1 and photostable.2 (more photostable than GFP) and fast.4 (tenfold faster bleaching), with photostable.1 requiring 122% of GFP photostability time.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Stability and quantum yield differences analyzed.

constraints: Must overcome epistatic constraints
Must enrich for stable, folded, and potentially active designs
initial_request.known_context: Epistatic interactions between mutations can severely restrict the chances of finding functional multipoint mutants.
quantifiable_goals: Generate thousands of functional active-site mutants
Achieve high enrichment of low-energy mutants
initial_request.research_problem: Active sites are sensitive to mutations due to high density of molecular interactions, making functional multipoint mutants exceptionally rare. Epistasis severely restricts the chances of finding functional multipoint mutants.

source.title: Designed active-site library reveals thousands of functional GFP variants

success_verification.final_conclusion: htFuncLib successfully generated thousands of functional GFP active-site mutants, overcoming epistatic constraints. The approach verified that energetically incompatible mutations are a significant source of epistasis.
limitations: Designs with 12–14 mutations were non-functional
Deep sequencing missed some functional designs (false negatives)
Photostability varied widely among designs

Random forest prediction and experimental validation

evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate four E. coli deletion strains with unbranched ETS variants pumping 1, 2, 3, or 4 protons per electron.

evidence.quote_or_span: a holistic examination of the system and its plasticity is lacking
we generated four Escherichia coli deletion strains (named ETS-1H, 2H, 3H, and 4H), each with one of the four unbranched ETS variants that pump 1, 2, 3, or 4 proton(s) per electron, respectively

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: A holistic examination of the system and its plasticity is lacking.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Constructing unbranched ETS variants with defined proton pumping stoichiometries will enable a systems-level analysis of ETS plasticity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The variants need to be evolved to observe adaptive responses.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Four strains were successfully generated.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: The plasticity and modularity of the E. coli ETS are not holistically understood.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ALE on all four variants in oxic conditions until growth rate plateaus.

evidence.quote_or_span: we performed ALE with four independent replicates of each variant in an oxic environment
We evolved all variants until their growth rate plateaued. ETS-1H, 2H & 3H required evolution for approximately 400 generations, while ETS-4H required approximately 700 generations.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The adaptive potential of these synthetic ETS variants is unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Adaptive laboratory evolution will improve growth rates and reveal mutational targets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify genetic changes underlying adaptation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ETS-1H, 2H, 3H plateaued after ~400 generations; ETS-4H required ~700 generations.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Four unbranched ETS variants have been constructed.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence genomes of evolved strains and identify mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: All four evolved replicates of uETS-3H acquired point mutations in sdhA
All four replicates of uETS-4H acquired mutations in an inadequately characterized gene, yjjX

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The genetic basis of adaptation is unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Whole-genome sequencing will reveal convergent mutations in key genes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Predict functional impact of mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Few mutations overall; all ETS-3H replicates had sdhA mutations; all ETS-4H replicates had yjjX mutations.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolved strains obtained from ALE.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze proteome allocation using ME-model and flux balance analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: The ETS-3H and ETS-4H strains forced to respire using larger NqRED (NDH-I) increased the flux through the proteome conservative ED pathway.
With an approximately 3.5-fold higher protein cost, the Embden–Meyerhoff–Parnass (EMP) pathway consumes a larger proportion of proteome as compared to the Entner–Doudoroff (ED) pathway $^{29}$ .

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do ETS variants rewire metabolism to support growth?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: High proton-pumping variants shift glycolytic flux to the proteome-efficient ED pathway.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Relate proteome changes to aero-type classification.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ETS-3H and ETS-4H increased flux through ED pathway; EMP pathway has ~3.5-fold higher protein cost.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutations predicted to affect metabolism.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Classify aero-types based on biomass yield and proteome allocation.

evidence.quote_or_span: We observed that ETS-1H, ETS-2H, and ETS-3H did not show a major shift in their biomass yield during evolution and thus preserved their respective aero-types (Fig. 3a).
ETS-4H jumped from a lower to a higher aero-type after evolution, suggesting an increase in oxic metabolism.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do these shifts alter the overall respiratory strategy (aero-type)?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Aero-types will remain conserved except for ETS-4H, which may shift to a higher aero-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Define the broader gene set involved in respiratory adaptation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ETS-1H, 2H, 3H preserved aero-types; ETS-4H shifted to a higher aero-type.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Proteome allocation shifts observed.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Curate ATS gene set and compute total proteome allocation and ATP production.

evidence.quote_or_span: The total proteome allocated to the ATS was very similar in each eETS variant, and the total ATP output of each proteome expressed was almost constant (Fig. 3b).
The ATS is composed of 209 genes (Supplementary Data 3). The ATS represents about 38% proteome allocation in all evolved variants.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the full set of genes and proteome allocation underlying respiratory adaptation?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A defined Aero-Type System (ATS) of 209 genes accounts for ~38% proteome allocation and constant ATP output.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Explore transcriptomic tradeoffs within the ATS.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ATS proteome allocation is similar across evolved variants; total ATP output is almost constant.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Aero-type shifts suggest a larger system is involved.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform independent component analysis (ICA) on ATS transcriptome data.

evidence.quote_or_span: Thus, an oxic-anoxic transcriptomic tradeoff enabled the four ETS variants to maintain similar ATP production capacity (Fig. 3d).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do transcriptomic changes enable similar ATP production despite different ETS compositions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An oxic-anoxic transcriptomic tradeoff balances the ATS.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Synthesize findings into a model of metabolic plasticity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Oxic-anoxic tradeoff identified; enables similar ATP production capacity across variants.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: ATS defined with constant proteome allocation and ATP output.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Integrate metabolic flux and proteome data to assess plasticity.

evidence.quote_or_span: Thus, metabolic plasticity (reflected in metabolic rewiring and associated proteome allocation) allows for redundancy in the eETS variants while supporting the same growth rate.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does metabolic plasticity allow redundancy in ETS variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Metabolic rewiring and proteome allocation plasticity support the same growth rate across variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Confirm that proteome allocation and ATP output are conserved.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Metabolic plasticity allows redundancy in eETS variants while supporting the same growth rate.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Transcriptomic tradeoff identified.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Quantify proteome allocation and ATP output across all evolved variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: the overall proteome allocation to the ATS is similar in the evolved variants and generates the same amount of ATP, enabling them to achieve the same growth rate

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the conserved proteome allocation and ATP output a general principle?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The overall proteome allocation to the ATS is similar in evolved variants and generates the same amount of ATP, enabling the same growth rate.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Interpret as alternate optimal states.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Similar proteome allocation and ATP output confirmed.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Metabolic plasticity demonstrated.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize the different combinations of proton pumping efficiency, metabolic rewiring, and proteome allocation.

evidence.quote_or_span: The different ways in which the ATS is balanced underlies its plasticity and represents a demonstration of the key systems biology concept of alternate optimal states.

These alternate states have a different combination of proton pumping efficiency, complementary metabolic rewiring achieved through tradeoffs in the composition of the transcriptome, and concomitant efficiency of proteome allocation, but enable the same overall cellular function.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How can different ETS compositions achieve the same function?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The ATS can be balanced in different ways, representing alternate optimal states.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Estimate proton-to-ATP ratio via computational modeling.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Alternate states have different proton pumping efficiency, transcriptome tradeoffs, and proteome allocation but enable the same cellular function.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Conserved proteome allocation and ATP output observed.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Sample proton pumping ratios from 2.5 to 4.5 in ME-model and optimize to match experimental growth rates.

evidence.quote_or_span: Proton pumping ratios from 2.5 to 4.5 were sampled by changing the stoichiometry of the ATPS4rpp reaction in the ME-model, and then the proton pumping ratio was optimized so that the model produced a biomass dilution rate that matched the experimentally determined growth rate.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the proton-to-ATP ratio consistent with the observed growth rates?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A proton-to-ATP ratio of 10:3 is optimal for all variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude on the deep plasticity of the ATS.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A ratio of 10 protons per 3 ATP was supported.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Alternate optimal states characterized.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Interpret the ATS as a nexus that balances energetic systems across environmental niches.

evidence.quote_or_span: The cytoplasmic-periplasmic adaptive nexus that the ATS represents thus illustrates the deep plasticity inherent in achieving balanced energetic systems to match metabolic needs in different environmental niches.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What broader principle does the ATS illustrate?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The ATS represents a cytoplasmic-periplasmic adaptive nexus illustrating deep plasticity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Finalize conclusions on ETS plasticity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The ATS illustrates deep plasticity in achieving balanced energetic systems to match metabolic needs in different environmental niches.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Computational modeling supports a consistent proton-to-ATP ratio.

constraints: Unbranched ETS variants with defined proton pumping stoichiometries
Oxic growth conditions
Glucose minimal medium
input_data: E. coli K-12 MG1655 wild-type strain
Gene deletion strains for ETS components
initial_request.known_context: E. coli has a highly flexible ETS with multiple dehydrogenases and reductases, but a holistic examination of the system and its plasticity is lacking.
quantifiable_goals: Achieve plateaued growth rate for all ETS variants
Characterize proteome allocation and ATP output
Determine proton-to-ATP ratio
initial_request.research_problem: Lack of a holistic, systems-level understanding of the plasticity and modularity of the bacterial respiratory electron transport system (ETS).

source.title: Laboratory evolution of synthetic electron transport system variants reveals a larger metabolic respiratory system and its plasticity

success_verification.final_conclusion: The ATS represents a cytoplasmic-periplasmic adaptive nexus illustrating deep plasticity, enabling alternate optimal states that achieve the same growth rate through different combinations of proton pumping efficiency, metabolic rewiring, and proteome allocation.
limitations: Only oxic conditions tested
Limited to glucose minimal medium
Mutations in sdhA and yjjX not fully characterized functionally

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a combinatorial library with tunable orthogonal promoters and enzyme variants, screen with a biosensor, and build predictive models.

evidence.quote_or_span: The optimization of the heterologous biosynthesis of flavonoids is in no way straightforward and despite these previous engineering efforts, its industrial potential still remains largely unexploited

it is required to attune every enzymatic reaction in the pathway to each other and to the cell's available resources, thereby avoiding flux imbalances leading to accumulation of metabolites and associated toxicity or a potential detrimental metabolic burden on the cell

a combinatorial engineering approach using the tools for tunable and orthogonal expression, created in Bervoets et al. (2018) [8], is combined with a biosensor-driven screening to collect high-quality data to feed three different predictive models with increasing complexity to optimize microbial naringenin biosynthesis

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to balance pathway modules to avoid metabolite accumulation and toxicity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A combinatorial engineering approach using orthogonal expression tools and biosensor-driven screening can collect high-quality data for predictive modeling to optimize the pathway.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assemble the combinatorial pathway library.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The approach is expected to generate a dataset covering a broad design space.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Heterologous naringenin biosynthesis in E. coli suffers from flux imbalances and metabolic burden.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Assemble the naringenin pathway module combinatorially with variability at promoter and enzyme levels.

evidence.quote_or_span: The described module is assembled in a combinatorial manner, with variability introduced at the promoter and enzymatic levels.
the theoretical search space comprises 160.000 (see Eq. 1) possible pathway configurations

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to create a diverse library covering the design space.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using 10 sigmaB-specific promoters and 2 isozymes per step will generate 160,000 possible pathway configurations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen the library using the naringenin biosensor.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Library assembled with broad coverage.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Combinatorial approach designed.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen 190 colonies in MTP using the biosensor and select top 35 based on fluorescence.

evidence.quote_or_span: the selection was restricted to 35 strains
The acquired fluorescence data from the 190 different strains was normalized for optical density (OD $_{600}$ ) and sorted in descending order.

To determine the genotype of the 35 fluorescence-based selected library strains, first, cells from the MTP cultures were streaked on agar plates lacking the antibiotic required to sustain replication of the biosensor carrying plasmid. Next, single colonies were cultured for plasmid isolation and subsequent Sanger sequencing

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which strains produce high naringenin titers?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Biosensor fluorescence correlates with naringenin production, enabling high-throughput screening.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Measure naringenin titers and genotype selected strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 35 strains selected for further characterization.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Combinatorial library constructed.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform UPLC to measure naringenin titers and sequence the genotypes.

evidence.quote_or_span: the naringenin titer in the MTP-scale cultures was determined through UPLC analysis and ranged from 1.52 to 27.03 mg/L
DNA sequencing was performed to determine the corresponding genotypes
Of the selected strains, the naringenin titer in the MTP-scale cultures was determined through UPLC analysis and ranged from 1.52 to 27.03 mg/L
DNA sequencing was performed to determine the corresponding genotypes.
the selection was restricted to 35 strains

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Actual naringenin titers and genotypes of selected strains unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: UPLC analysis and DNA sequencing will provide accurate titer and genotype data.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze data to identify key enzyme variants and build models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Titers ranged from 1.52 to 27.03 mg/L; genotypes determined.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: 35 strains selected from biosensor screening.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ANOVA and barplot analysis; remove poorly represented variants (At4CL, PhCHS) to reduce features.

evidence.quote_or_span: For one out of the 35 observations, sequencing was unsuccessful.
pathway configurations containing the TAL CDS from Flavobacterium johnsoniae (FjTAL) perform significantly better on average than those containing the TAL CDS from Rhodotorula glutinis (RgTAL)
the presence of only a single 4CL CDS from Arabidopsis thaliana (At4CL) and three appearances of the CHS CDS from Petunia hybrida (PhCHS) in the dataset
It was decided accordingly to remove the total of four entries in the dataset containing either of these two enzyme variants to reduce the number of model features

For the followed pathway construction workflow, all enzyme variants in the pathway (listed in Table 2) were cloned in separate carrier plasmids, in which all operons catalyzing the same enzymatic reaction are flanked by the same pair of Golden Gate (GG) restriction sites, sequentially matching with the GG restriction sites for the different steps in the pathway $[34]$ (see Fig.

By using a unique linker-based Golden Gate assembly workflow for the construction of the pathway variants, the presence of all promoters and coding sequences is evenly distributed in the final pathway library $[34, 39]$ .

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: One strain failed sequencing, reducing dataset to 34; need to identify influential enzyme variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Data exploration will reveal which enzyme variants significantly affect titer.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Build OLS regression model with reduced dataset.
evolution_trajectory.observation: FjTAL significantly outperforms RgTAL; At4CL and PhCHS underrepresented, removed from dataset.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Titer and genotype data collected for 35 strains.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Build OLS model with 6 features, quadratic and interaction terms; reduce by backward elimination.

evidence.quote_or_span: first an ordinary least squares (OLS) regression analysis was performed
This initial model was reduced to only maintain the terms contributing to product formation by sequentially removing the least significant term
The predicted top six naringenin producers show a consensus of high FjTAL, Petroselinum crispum 4CL (Pc4CL) and PhCHI expression and low Gerbera hybrida CHS (GhCHS) expression
This is in contradiction with what could be expected from the performed data exploration for the preferred expression level of 4CL and CHS.
The regression model contains 18 regressors and was trained with the 30 observations.

(2018) [30] developed a machine-learning workflow, especially designed to deal with the optimization of heterologous biosynthetic pathways, trained with a relatively small dataset generated from a prescreened combinatorially engineered library, similar to this work.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a predictive model to relate promoter TIF and enzyme variants to titer.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: OLS regression with quadratic and interaction terms can capture nonlinear effects.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: OLS model overfits and is sensitive to outliers; try PLS regression.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Model achieved R²=0.93 but predictions contradicted data exploration.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Dataset reduced to 30 observations with key enzyme variants identified.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Build PLS model with 2 latent variables using 30 observations.

evidence.quote_or_span: Partial least squares (PLS) regression model
The regression model contains 18 regressors and was trained with the 30 observations.

The regression coefficients explaining promoter TIF all align toward an increased production capacity for high expression of TAL, and in sheer contrast with the OLS model, high expression of CHS and low expression of 4CL and CHI.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a more robust model that aligns with data exploration.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PLS regression with latent variables will handle multicollinearity and small sample size better.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use PLS predictions to guide further optimization; also explore ANN ensemble.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Model achieved R²=0.79; predictions consistent with data exploration.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: OLS model failed due to overfitting and outlier sensitivity.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt ANN ensemble workflow: 1000 iterations, random weights, store top 10 producers, selection threshold 0.5*f_max.

evidence.quote_or_span: This ANN ensemble workflow (as opposed to the training of a single model) was adopted to avoid overfitting due to the use of a relatively small dataset.
The genetic parts predicted to contribute to a superior naringenin production are very similar to the PLS model predictions with minor differences for TAL and CHS promoter TIF
The supplementation of glycerol to the medium increases the titer further by 116.8 ± 5.6% to 53.3 mg/L for strain Top10.5 and 111.2 ± 48.9% to 61.4 mg/L for Top10.4

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Risk of overfitting with small dataset when using ANN.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An ANN ensemble with multiple random initializations and selection of top performers will avoid overfitting.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate model predictions by constructing and testing optimized strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ANN ensemble predictions similar to PLS, with minor differences in TAL and CHS promoter TIF.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: PLS model provides robust predictions.

evidence.quote_or_span: both optimized strains show, on average, an almost 15 times increase in production titer compared to NarRef

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the predicted strains actually produce higher titers?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Strains built according to model predictions will outperform the unbalanced reference pathway.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test glycerol supplementation to further boost titer.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Optimized strains showed ~15-fold titer increase compared to NarRef.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model predictions suggest optimal pathway configurations.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Supplement medium with glycerol and measure titers for Top10.4 and Top10.5.

evidence.quote_or_span: The supplementation of glycerol to the medium increases the titer further by 116.8 ± 5.6% to 53.3 mg/L for strain Top10.5 and 111.2 ± 48.9% to 61.4 mg/L for Top10.4

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can glycerol supplementation further increase titer by boosting malonyl-CoA?
