initial_request.known_context: Flux imbalances and metabolic burden hinder heterologous flavonoid production.
quantifiable_goals: Improve naringenin titer
initial_request.research_problem: Optimizing the heterologous naringenin biosynthesis pathway in E. coli using orthogonal expression tools and a biosensor-driven, model-based workflow to balance pathway modules and improve production titer without precursor supplementation or strain engineering.
initial_request.target_object: naringenin biosynthesis pathway in E. coli

source.title: Biosensor-driven, model-based optimization of the orthogonally expressed naringenin biosynthesis pathway

success_verification.final_conclusion: Model-based optimization achieved ~15-fold titer increase; glycerol further boosted titers to 61.4 mg/L, but malonyl-CoA limitation and plasmid instability remain challenges.
limitations: Malonyl-CoA limitation in stationary phase
Plasmid instability and cell stress in bioreactor

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a combinatorial library by coupling rational site selection with shuffling of active mutants.

evidence.quote_or_span: we failed to identify DNA polymerases with increased mismatch discrimination from unfocused libraries while we also failed to obtain DNA polymerases with reverse transcription activity from focused libraries
we attempted to design a combinatorial library that coupled structure-based rational design and molecular shuffling of functional mutants

We reasoned that shuffling only active mutants for combinatorial library design would reduce the load of non-functional mutations in enzyme variants and thereby enrich the sequence diversity of the library with improved functional scope.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to simultaneously engineer both increased mismatch discrimination and reverse transcriptase activity in KlenTaq DNA polymerase.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining structure-based rational design and molecular shuffling of functional mutants will reduce non-functional mutations and enrich functional diversity, enabling discovery of both properties.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to select target residues for mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: We reasoned that shuffling only active mutants would reduce non-functional mutations and improve functional scope.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Select 19 residues in finger, palm, and thumb domains for saturation mutagenesis.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform RACHITT: anneal mutagenic oligonucleotides to transient ssDNA template, extend, ligate, UDG treat, PCR amplify, clone, and transform.

evidence.quote_or_span: The mutagenic oligonucleotides of 173 active mutants were annealed with a transient single stranded template DNA of KlenTaq DNA polymerase
After chimeric strand synthesis, the nicks were ligated with Taq DNA ligase and the transient single stranded template DNA was treated with uracil-DNA glycosylase
The shuffling of active mutants involved four steps: annealing, gap filling/ligation, uracil-DNA glycosylase treatment and PCR of chimeric DNA.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop an automated tool for post-docking minimization of protein-ligand complexes.

evidence.quote_or_span: available computer programs do not easily address problems such as: structural optimization of compounds in a screening library, receptor flexibility/induced-fit, and accurate prediction of protein-ligand interactions

It has been shown that structural optimization of chemical compounds and that post-docking optimization in multi-step structure-based virtual screening approaches help to further improve the overall efficiency of the methods.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether post-docking optimization can improve enrichment in structure-based virtual screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Post-docking optimization and structural refinement of compounds can improve the efficiency of structure-based virtual screening.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Previous studies suggest that post-docking energy minimization can significantly improve enrichment for some protein targets.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop AMMOS with AMMP for fast energy minimization, supporting partial to full flexibility.

evidence.quote_or_span: we developed the program AMMOS for refining both, the 3D structures of the small molecules present in chemical libraries and the predicted receptor-ligand complexes through allowing partial to full atom flexibility through molecular mechanics optimization.
AMMOS proposes relatively fast energy minimization by making use of the full-featured molecular mechanics program AMMP

AMMP has several advantages that make it relevant for the present applications such as: a fast multipole method for including all atoms in the calculation of long-range potentials and robust structural optimizers.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design Hotspot Hunter with ANN and SVM as predictive engines, using soft computing integration for robust prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: The high cost of peptide synthesis, limited access to human peripheral blood samples, and time-consuming experimental assays further add to the challenge.

Nearly all existing publicly available promiscuous T-cell epitope prediction servers perform prediction for a single protein sequence per submission, while those that accept multiple sequences are not tailor-made to predict hotspots; they rely on manual visualization techniques.

Given the large size of pathogen proteomes, it is painstaking to integrate the individual prediction results, and therefore impractical to use these tools for large-scale systematic study of promiscuous epitopes
Hotspot Hunter uses Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM) methods as predictive engines.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No computational system exists for large-scale, automated prediction of immunological hotspots across multiple HLA supertypes and pathogen proteomes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A computational system integrating ANN and SVM predictive engines can enable large-scale screening and selection of candidate immunological hotspots.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to compile training datasets for the predictive models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ANN and SVM are chosen as complementary methods; soft computing integration is planned to combine their predictions.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental T-cell epitope mapping is costly and low-throughput; existing computational tools are limited to single proteins and manual integration.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Compile training datasets: A2 (3050 peptides), A3 (2216), B7 (4102), DR (2396).

evidence.quote_or_span: These data were mainly from three sources, the MHCPEP database [24], IEDB [25], and published literature, and a set of HLA non-binding peptides (Brusic V., unpublished data).

Since the data originated from a single set of experiments, with relatively consistent experimental conditions, it is reasonable to expect that the DR training dataset is of higher quality relative to datasets aggregated from multiple sources.

The HLA-A2 training dataset had a total of 3050 (675 binders and 2375 non-binders) 9-mer peptides related to 15 variants of the HLA-A2 supertype (0201, 0202, 0203, 0204, 0205, 0206, 0207, 0208, 0209, 0210, 0211, 0214, 0217, 6802 and 6901).
The HLA-A3 training dataset had a total of 2216 (680 binders and 1536 non-binders) 9-mer peptides related to eight variants (0301, 0302, 1101, 1102, 3101, 3301, 3303 and 6801).
The HLA-DR training dataset had 2396 (448 binders and 1948 non-binders) 9-mer peptides related to six HLA-DRB1 variants (0101, 0301, 0701, 0801, 1101 and 1501).

The HLA-B7 training dataset had a total of 4102 (1258 binders and 2844 non-binders) 9-mer peptides related to 13 variants (0702, 1508, 3501, 3502, 3503, 5101, 5102, 5103, 5301, 5401, 5501, 5502 and 5601).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need high-quality training datasets for four HLA supertypes (A2, A3, B7, DR).
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Compiling data from MHCPEP, IEDB, literature, and unpublished sources will provide sufficient training data; DR dataset from consistent experiments may be higher quality.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Build ANN models using the training data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Datasets compiled; DR dataset expected higher quality due to consistent experimental conditions.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Predictive engines selected; no training data available.

Ten-fold cross-validation results showed that the area under the receiver operating curve (Aroc) for A2, A3, B7 and DR ANN models were 0.83, 0.83, 0.88 and 0.85, respectively.

evidence.quote_or_span: The "binding strength" represents the sum of binding scores predicted by ANN and SVM models for each hotspot.
Individual peptides with prediction scores greater than or equal to the threshold 65 for DR supertype are considered as positive binders to multiple DR alleles

initial_request.research_problem: Experimental identification of T-cell epitopes is laborious, costly, and impractical for large-scale screening across multiple HLA alleles and pathogen proteomes. source.title: Hotspot Hunter: a computational system for large-scale screening and selection of candidate immunological hotspots in pathogen proteomes

success_verification.final_conclusion: Hotspot Hunter successfully predicts immunological hotspots across four HLA supertypes, with Aroc values up to 0.95 for SVM models. Experimental validation showed correct prediction of most known hotspots, with some misses. The system enables large-scale screening, consensus hotspot identification, and target selection for epitope-based vaccines.
limitations: Some hotspots missed (e.g., 1/2 HLA-B7 hotspots missed)

we propose that such predictive tasks should be defined within the context of individual proteins or protein families that share similar functions.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design PreMode architecture.

evidence.quote_or_span: PreMode applies SE(3)-equivariant mechanisms on edge features and node features first through a star-graph that connects variant site with all other residues to capture the direct impacts of amino acid alterations on other residues, then through the k-nearest neighbor (KNN)-graph that connects each residue with its closest neighbor residues to capture the second order impacts
PreMode is an SE(3)-equivariant graph attention neural network with 4 layers.
First layer is a feature embedding layer that encodes the input features into latent dimensions.
Second and third layers are equivariant graph attention layers designed to model interactions between the connected nodes

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing methods do not capture direct and second-order impacts of amino acid alterations.

parameters.architecture: SE(3)-equivariant graph attention neural network with 4 layers

evolution_trajectory.decision: Pretrain PreMode on 148,324 pathogenic/benign variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: We collected a total of 148,324 missense variants for training, including 51,494 benign variants from PrimateAI $^{18}$ and 32,350 non-overlapping benign variants from ClinVar $^{59}$ with at least one-star non-conflict submits that labeled as “benign” or “likely-benign”; 64,480 “pathogenic” and “likely-pathogenic” variants from ClinVar database with at least one-star non-conflict submits $^{8}$ and non-overlapping variants from HGMD $^{41}$ (Table 2).

We collected 83,844 benign variants from ClinVar $^{59}$ and PrimateAI $^{18}$ in 13,748 genes, and 64,480 pathogenic variants from ClinVar $^{59}$ and HGMD $^{41}$ in 3703 genes (“Methods: Training and testing datasets”).

Instead, we used independent testing data for which the pathogenicity label was entirely based on statistical evidence, that is, 533 pathogenic missense variants in cancer hotspots from cBioportal $^{50,52,60}$ , and same number of benign variants in the same genes randomly selected from common variants in primates $^{18}$ .

For testing, we included 1066 missense variants, including 533 pathogenic missense variants from somatic missense hotspots in 153 cancer driver genes that not annotated in the training data; and 533 benign variants from the same genes, randomly selected from ClinVar and PrimateAI that not overlapping with training dataset (Table 2).

evidence.quote_or_span: DNA assembly scenarios encountered in metabolic engineering for the construction of amino acid and related compound producers, such as design-build-test-learn cycles, construction of precise genetic circuits and repetitive DNA molecules, usually require for iterative, scarless and repetitive sequence assembly methods, respectively.

Here, we developed a Type IIP and IIS RE-assisted method named PS-Brick that comprehensively takes advantage of the properties of PCR fragments and REs for iterative, seamless and repetitive sequence assembly.

source.title: A seamless and iterative DNA assembly method named PS-Brick and its assisted metabolic engineering for threonine and 1-propanol production

success_verification.final_conclusion: PS-Brick is the first restriction-ligation assembly method using both Type IIP and IIS REs, enabling iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly. It was successfully applied to threonine overproduction (45.71 g/L) through four DBTL cycles and to 1-propanol pathway assembly (1.35 g/L), though low enzyme activity limited 1-propanol yield.
limitations: Low 1-propanol titer due to insufficient KivD and ADH2 activity
PS-Brick unfit for combinatorial pathway engineering due to limitations in multi-part assembly
Need to eliminate internal restriction sites remains a general limitation for RE-based strategies

amino acid replacements exhibiting high energy contributions to binary complex stabilization and low values of betweenness centrality measure were selected as promising positional targets for mutagenesis

evolution_trajectory.decision: Build homology model of IDI using E. coli IDI (2ICJ) as template and perform molecular docking with IPP.

evidence.quote_or_span: in silico structural modelling and molecular docking were performed by using the 3D structure of IPP isomerase from E. coli (PDB ID: 2ICJ)
The molecular docking results revealed that the binding energy between the substrate and the mutant enzyme was increased to 8.87 kcal/mol from the original 6.87 kcal/mol
L141H substitution showed His-141 participated in the construction of the active pocket, which enlarged the IDI catalytic domain
Due to hydrophobicity of Phe-195 and Cys-256, Ala-106, Phe-107 and Cys-139 could fixed substrate better

This suggested that enhanced enzymatic activity was primarily a result of two main factors: (1) improved affinity between the substrate and enzyme caused by mutations (L141H, Y195F and W256C) and (2) increased enzyme stability due to mutations within the hydrogen bond and hydrophobic interaction

initial_request.known_context: IDIs are limited by low enzyme activity, short half-life and weak substrate affinity
initial_request.research_problem: IDI is a rate-limiting enzyme in the lycopene biosynthetic pathway with low activity, short half-life, and weak substrate affinity. The goal is to enhance IDI catalytic activity through protein engineering to improve lycopene production in E. coli.
initial_request.target_object: Isopentenyl diphosphate isomerase (IDI) from Saccharomyces cerevisiae

source.title: Enhancement of the catalytic activity of Isopentenyl diphosphate isomerase (IDI) from Saccharomyces cerevisiae through random and site-directed mutagenesis

success_verification.final_conclusion: Triple mutant L141H/Y195F/W256C significantly enhances IDI catalytic activity, leading to 3.13-fold lycopene production improvement in vivo, with improved kinetic parameters, pH and temperature stability, and sustained fermentation performance. Mutations act additively through enhanced substrate affinity and enzyme stability.
limitations: Error-prone PCR inherent deficiency limits substantial further increase in IDI activity; other techniques needed for further optimization.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct sensor strain S3 by deleting hupAB and nifH in R. capsulatus.

evidence.quote_or_span: The deletion of hupAB genes in the RC25 strain completely eliminated its in vivo uptake hydrogenase activity (nil compared to $2504 \pm 450$ nmol $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{h}^{-1}\mathrm{OD}_{600}^{-1}$ in the wild-type strain).

First, the response to 10% exogenous $H_{2}$ improved 5-fold in the $\Delta$ hupAB strain S2 compared to in S1; second, the constitutive activation of PhupA was observed in the $\Delta$ hupT strain RC24-S1; and third, no response to $H_{2}$ was observed in the $\Delta$ hupR strain RC54-S1.
Figure 1C shows that the $\beta$ -galactosidase activity in S2 was 15-fold higher under diazotrophic conditions compared to non-diazotrophic conditions, validating its use as a biosensor.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate 3-D models of wild-type and V7 NifH by homology modeling.

evidence.quote_or_span: 3-D model of R. capsulatus NifH and the V7 variant were generated by homology modeling (http://swissmodel.expasy.org/) using the A. vinelandii NifH structures as template (1NIP for wild-type NifH and 1G5P for NifH-V7, respectively).
Both models yielded NifH homodimer with capability to ligate $[4Fe-4S]$ clusters.
The overall protein overlap (4–171 amino acid residues) and an RMSD value of 0.900 Angstroms for the protein backbone atoms indicate very similar model architectures.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a multiplex CRISPR-Cas9 system (CRISPRm) for markerless integration of DNA libraries into the yeast genome.

evidence.quote_or_span: present directed evolution systems require the use of extrachromosomal gene libraries encoded in bacteriophage or plasmids, which suffer from high levels of copy number variation
common yeast centromere containing (CEN) plasmids vary widely in copy number per cell resulting in highly variable expression levels from cell to cell
The presence of the Cas9-produced DSB in genomic DNA can increase the rate of homology-directed repair (HDR) with linear DNA at the DSB locus by several 1000-fold

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to achieve efficient, markerless chromosomal integration of DNA libraries to eliminate copy number variation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: CRISPR-Cas9 can increase homology-directed repair efficiency by several 1000-fold, enabling high-throughput chromosomal integration.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design a new sgRNA architecture to improve expression and stability for multiplexing.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The presence of the Cas9-produced DSB in genomic DNA can increase the rate of homology-directed repair (HDR) with linear DNA at the DSB locus by several 1000-fold.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Extrachromosomal gene libraries in yeast suffer from high copy number variation, leading to variable expression and confounding selection. Standard homologous recombination is too inefficient for markerless integration of DNA libraries.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a new sgRNA architecture with HDV ribozyme fused to the 5' end.

evidence.quote_or_span: We reasoned that the structured ribozyme would protect the 5' end of the sgRNA from 5' exonucleases

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: sgRNA 5' end is susceptible to exonucleases, limiting abundance and multiplexing efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fusing the sgRNA to the 3' end of the self-cleaving HDV ribozyme will protect the 5' end and allow flexible promoter use.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test various RNA polymerase III promoters for sgRNA expression with the new architecture.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The structured ribozyme would protect the 5' end of the sgRNA from 5' exonucleases.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that CRISPR-Cas9 can enhance HDR for chromosomal integration, but sgRNA stability and expression need optimization.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test various RNA polymerase III promoters for sgRNA expression in diploid yeast at the URA3 locus.

evidence.quote_or_span: With a single sgRNA, we found tRNA sequences used as RNA polymerase III promoters resulted in nearly 100% efficient barcode insertion in diploid yeast at the URA3 locus, resulting in 5-fluororotic acid resistance (Boeke et al., 1984) whereas the non-tRNA promoters were mostly ineffective (Figure 3A; Supplementary file 1A).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which RNA polymerase III promoter yields highest sgRNA expression and targeting efficiency?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: tRNA promoters will provide efficient sgRNA expression due to their natural processing.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess target sequence bias across multiple genomic loci using the best promoter.
evolution_trajectory.observation: tRNA promoters resulted in nearly 100% efficient barcode insertion at the URA3 locus, while non-tRNA promoters were mostly ineffective.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: HDV ribozyme-sgRNA fusion designed, but optimal promoter for sgRNA expression unknown.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Target 11 unlinked yeast genes in diploid S288C cells using tRNA^Tyr promoter.

evidence.quote_or_span: We also assessed target sequence bias by targeting 11 unlinked yeast genes in diploid S288C yeast cells using tRNA $^{Tyr}$ as the RNA polymerase III promoter.

We found that targeting was highly efficient for every genomic target except LEU2, which at first was weak but was restored to 100% by selecting a different guide sequence (LEU2-2) as the sgRNA (Figure 3B).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does targeting efficiency vary across different genomic loci and guide sequences?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Targeting efficiency will be high for most loci, but some may require guide sequence optimization.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Extend to multiplex targeting of multiple loci simultaneously.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Targeting was highly efficient for every genomic target except LEU2, which was restored to 100% by selecting a different guide sequence (LEU2-2).
evolution_trajectory.state_before: tRNA promoters identified as highly efficient for single-locus targeting, but target sequence bias unknown.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test trans-multiplexed CRISPR-Cas9 by targeting two and three unlinked loci in diploid cells.

evidence.quote_or_span: We simultaneously targeted two and three unlinked genomic loci in diploid cells for loss-of-function mutagenesis by introducing a single plasmid expressing Cas9 and two or three sgRNAs with two or three gene-specific barcoded DNA molecules, respectively. The efficiency of duplex and triplex targeting (requiring four and six chromosome cuts, respectively) with two sgRNAs (URA3 and LYP1) or three (URA3, LYP1 and COX10) was 43% and 19%, respectively (Figure 3D; Supplementary file 1A).

This multiplexed efficiency was dependent on the presence of the HDV ribozyme 5' of the guide sequence as duplex targeting dropped to 3.5% in cells with sgRNA lacking the 5' HDV ribozyme (Figure 3D; Supplementary file 1A).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can CRISPRm simultaneously target multiple unlinked genomic loci with high efficiency?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multiplex targeting efficiency will be lower than single-locus but sufficient for library integration, and dependent on HDV ribozyme.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that HDV ribozyme increases sgRNA abundance, explaining improved multiplexing.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Duplex targeting efficiency was 43%, triplex 19%, and without HDV ribozyme duplex dropped to 3.5%.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Single-locus targeting efficiency established, but multiplex targeting capability unknown.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure relative cellular abundance of sgRNAs with and without HDV ribozyme by RT-qPCR.

evidence.quote_or_span: We measured the relative cellular abundance of sgRNAs expressed by $P_{SNR52}$ , with and without the 5' HDV ribozyme, by reverse transcription quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) and found that the presence of the ribozyme increases the intracellular abundance of the sgRNAs by sixfold (Figure 3E), consistent with the model that sgRNA abundance is rate limiting for CRISPR/Cas targeting (Hsu et al., 2013).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the HDV ribozyme increase intracellular sgRNA abundance?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: HDV ribozyme increases sgRNA abundance by protecting from degradation, and sgRNA abundance is rate-limiting for CRISPR targeting.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply cis-multiplexing for in vivo assembly of functional genes from multiple fragments.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Presence of the ribozyme increased intracellular sgRNA abundance by sixfold.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Multiplex targeting efficiency shown to depend on HDV ribozyme, but mechanism unclear.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test in vivo assembly of a nourseothricin-resistance gene from three overlapping PCR products at the URA3 locus.

evidence.quote_or_span: We tested the efficiency of in vivo assembly of a functional nourseothricin-resistance (Nat $^{R}$ ) gene from three overlapping PCR products encoding a transcription promoter, protein open reading frame (ORF) and transcription terminator at a single locus using cis-multiplexing in the diploid S288C strain.

We found 85% efficiency of targeting and assembly in both copies of the URA3 locus in diploid yeast S288C cells, and 70% in polyploid ATCC4124 by using the tRNA $^{Pro}$ sequence as the sgRNA promoter (Figure 3C; Supplementary file 1A)

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can CRISPRm assemble a functional gene from multiple overlapping PCR products in vivo?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cis-multiplexing (targeting all copies of a locus) will allow efficient assembly of a selectable marker from three fragments.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use CRISPRm for directed evolution of cdt-1 transporter to improve cellobiose utilization.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 85% efficiency in diploid S288C and 70% in polyploid ATCC4124.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: sgRNA abundance validated as rate-limiting, enabling complex multi-fragment assembly.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform in vivo selection for improved cellobiose utilization by integrating an error-prone PCR library of cdt-1 into the URA3 locus.

evidence.quote_or_span: To select for improved cellobiose utilizing strains we used error-prone PCR to amplify the cdt-1 gene. We co-transformed the library of mutated cdt-1 alleles, along with overlapping dsDNA fragments for the promoter and terminator, for assembly and integration into the URA3 locus (ura3:: $P_{PGK1}$ -cdt-1- $T_{ADH1}$ library) of a diploid strain with a previously integrated $\beta$ -glucosidase gh1-1 gene (lyp1:: $P_{TDH3}$ -gh1-1- $T_{CYC1}$ ) using CRISPRm.

We then grew the transformants in medium containing cellobiose as the sole carbon source to select for functional cdt-1 alleles and identified one strain with 2.6-fold (homozygous cdt-1 N209S/I354N/S360P/T406S/W531L allele) and one strain with 1.7-fold (homozygous cdt-1 Q45H/F262Y/F533Y allele) increased cellobiose utilization capacity over wild type cdt-1 (Figure 4A).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can CRISPRm be used for directed evolution of a transporter to improve cellobiose utilization?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR library of cdt-1 alleles integrated into the chromosome will allow selection of improved variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Combine beneficial mutations to test for synergistic effects.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified strains with 2.6-fold and 1.7-fold increased cellobiose utilization over wild type.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: CRISPRm enables efficient multi-fragment assembly, suitable for library integration.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use CRISPRm to introduce the N209S mutation into the cdt-1 Q45H/F262Y/F533Y background.

evidence.quote_or_span: The quadruple mutant cdt-1 allele resulted in synergistic cellobiose utilization with 3.9-fold increased growth over strains expressing wild-type cdt-1 (Figure 4A).

We then grew the transformants in medium containing cellobiose as the sole carbon source to select for functional cdt-1 alleles and identified one strain with 2.6-fold (homozygous cdt-1 N209S/I354N/S360P/T406S/W531L allele) and one strain with 1.7-fold (homozygous cdt-1 Q45H/F262Y/F533Y allele) increased cellobiose utilization capacity over wild type cdt-1 (Figure 4A).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can combining mutations from different alleles yield synergistic improvements?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Introducing the N209S mutation into the Q45H/F262Y/F533Y background will create a quadruple mutant with enhanced activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate improved mutants in anaerobic fermentations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The quadruple mutant resulted in synergistic cellobiose utilization with 3.9-fold increased growth over wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Two cdt-1 mutant alleles identified with improved cellobiose utilization, but potential for synergy unknown.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test selected cdt-1 alleles in anaerobic fermentations using cellobiose as the sole carbon source.

evidence.quote_or_span: We next tested the selected cdt-1 alleles in anaerobic fermentations using cellobiose as the sole carbon source.
We found that wild-type cdt-1 integrated into the chromosome was barely able to support cellobiose fermentation (Figure 4C).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the selected cdt-1 alleles enable efficient cellobiose fermentation under anaerobic conditions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Chromosomally integrated mutant cdt-1 alleles will support complete cellobiose fermentation, unlike wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Discuss limitations of the selection method.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Wild-type cdt-1 integrated into the chromosome barely supported fermentation, while mutants enabled complete fermentation.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Improved cdt-1 mutants show enhanced growth on cellobiose plates, but performance in industrially relevant conditions unknown.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation that selection may have enriched for sequences conferring improved mRNA stability or translation efficiency.

evidence.quote_or_span: it is also possible that selection enriched for sequences that confer improved mRNA stability or translation efficiency, due to the fact that the selection was carried out in vivo.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Could the selection have enriched for sequences that improve mRNA stability or translation efficiency rather than protein function?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: In vivo selection may enrich for non-coding mutations affecting expression, not just protein activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note strain-dependent promoter efficiency as another limitation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: It is possible that selection enriched for sequences that confer improved mRNA stability or translation efficiency, due to the in vivo nature of the selection.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Successful directed evolution and validation, but potential confounding factors in selection.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Report that tRNA^Tyr was inefficient in ATCC4124 though effective in S288C diploid, indicating strain-dependent promoter activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: Notably, tRNA $^{Tyr}$ was inefficient at targeting in ATCC4124 though it was effective in S288C diploid (Supplementary file 1A), indicating that the choice of tRNA used for the expression of the HDV-sgRNA impacts multiplexing in a strain-dependent manner.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the choice of tRNA promoter universally effective across different yeast strains?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: tRNA promoter efficiency is strain-dependent, limiting generalizability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude the trajectory.
evolution_trajectory.observation: tRNA^Tyr was inefficient at targeting in ATCC4124 though effective in S288C diploid, indicating strain-dependent promoter choice impacts multiplexing.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: CRISPRm efficiency shown to vary with tRNA promoter and strain background.

the position of missing residues from Asp87 to Asn90 was modelled with Modeller software

evidence.quote_or_span: Parameter 8 (maximum allowed overlap volume between protein and ligand surface: 100 Å³) gave the best results
This is due to clashes between the protein and ligand atom surfaces.

Re-docking against minimized structures with the same parameter sets gave best results. All the parameter sets reproduced the actual binding mode of the ligand, further made interactions to key amino acids and RMS deviation were less than 2 Å.
Parameter 8 (maximum allowed overlap volume between protein and ligand surface: 100 Å³) gave the best results in terms of docking score, docking conformation and interactions to key amino acids.

The major difference between our previous deployments (WISDOM-I and the data challenge on avian flu) is the automatic resubmission of the docking jobs on the grid.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose a rational design strategy (MSC) combining MD, SMD, and conservation analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: The current thermostability modification strategies for nattokinase do not involve modification of the catalytic centre.

Modifying the active centre of an enzyme is a more challenging process than modifying other regions. This is due to the fact that the amino acid residues in the active centre of an enzyme are highly conserved and interact extensively with the substrate. This makes it more likely that the enzyme will become inactivated following a mutation in this region.

Thermostability modification studies of nattokinase have not addressed its active centre region, while active centre modifications using directed evolution tend to result in a higher workload and a high percentage of negative results.

It is therefore evident that a rational design strategy to enhance the thermostability of the enzyme by modifying its active centre represents an effective means of solving the aforementioned problems.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No rational design strategy exists for active center thermostability modification that overcomes activity-stability trade-off.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A rational design strategy targeting the active center can overcome the activity-stability trade-off and reduce screening workload.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the MSC strategy steps.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Rational design strategy is hypothesized to effectively solve the problems.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Nattokinase thermostability is poor, and active center modification is challenging due to high conservation and risk of inactivation.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design MSC strategy: 1) MD to find flexible sites, 2) SMD to eliminate substrate-interacting residues, 3) conservation analysis to exclude strictly conserved residues.

evidence.quote_or_span: a new strategy based on the combination of molecular dynamics simulation at different temperatures, steered molecular dynamics simulation, and sequence conservative analysis (MSC) was designed in this study.

The initial identification of candidate sites with high flexibility changes was achieved through a comparative analysis of RMSF alterations in the active centre of nattokinase under varying temperature conditions. This was followed by the utilisation of steered molecular dynamics simulation to eliminate sites involved in substrate interactions upon substrate entry into the enzyme's active centre. Subsequently, the highly conserved sites were further excluded through a sequence conservative analysis.

it has become imperative to devise a rational design scheme for the stability modification of the active centre of nattokinase that reduces the intensity of screening work while improving the prediction accuracy.

initial_request.known_context: Heme overproduction is limited by enzyme activity and feedback inhibition. Non-iron metalloporphyrins (MPs) like ZnPPIX can be detoxified by heme, potentially coupling growth to heme production.
quantifiable_goals: Improve growth under ZnPPIX stress
Increase specific activity and catalytic efficiency of heme biosynthetic enzymes
initial_request.research_problem: Intrinsic low activity and feedback inhibition of heme biosynthetic enzymes limit heme overproduction, and existing directed evolution methods have limitations.
initial_request.target_object: Heme biosynthetic enzymes (PbgS, HmbS, UroS, UroD, CgoX, CpfC, ChdC) in Corynebacterium glutamicum

source.title: Development of a growth-coupled selection platform for directed evolution of heme biosynthetic enzymes in Corynebacterium glutamicum

success_verification.final_conclusion: The growth-coupled selection platform based on ZnPPIX detoxification effectively evolved four out of seven heme biosynthetic enzymes, yielding mutants with significantly improved growth and catalytic activity. Three enzymes (HmbS, UroS, ChdC) were not evolvable under current conditions, highlighting a need for platform optimization.
limitations: Three enzymes (HmbS, UroS, ChdC) did not show improved growth, indicating the selection platform needs optimization for universality.
validation_methods: Reconstruction of CpfC mutants to rule out background mutations
Kinetic characterization of mutants using fluorescence assay

evolution_trajectory.decision: Summarize findings: downregulation of midstream module enhances flux, but excessive flux harms accumulation. Combination optimization achieved yields from 20.3 to 219.7 mg/g DCW, improving lycopene accumulation by 120-250% over reference strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: coordinating the expression of all the genes of the midstream module was an effective strategy for optimizing the lycopene synthesis pathway

The four genes of the midstream module were fine-tuned based on these foundations by the construction of the RBS library, which further optimized the lycopene metabolic pathway and improved lycopene accumulation by 120%\~250% over the reference strains.

initial_request.known_context: Simple overexpression of enzymes in multienzyme pathways can cause imbalance, growth inhibition, or poor yield due to intermediate cytotoxicity. Previous in vitro optimization of MVA enzyme ratios improved conversion but is not directly applicable in vivo due to cellular complexity.
quantifiable_goals: Increase lycopene yield over reference strains
Achieve balanced expression of MVK, PMK, MVD, IDI
initial_request.research_problem: Low lycopene yield in E. coli due to metabolic imbalance in the downstream MVA pathway (MVK, PMK, MVD, IDI). Previous in vitro optimization improved conversion but not directly applicable in vivo. Need for in vivo fine-tuning to balance upstream and downstream modules and reduce intermediate accumulation.
initial_request.target_object: Lycopene synthesis pathway in E. coli

risk_notes: All coverage requirements met with direct evidence quotes.

source.title: Optimizing the downstream MVA pathway using a combination optimization strategy to increase lycopene yield in Escherichia coli

success_verification.final_conclusion: Combinatorial RBS library optimization of the downstream MVA module across three plasmid backbones successfully improved lycopene yield. The best strain LM22 achieved 219.7 mg/g DCW, representing a 120% increase over the reference strain. Coordinating expression of all midstream module genes was effective, improving lycopene accumulation by 120-250% over reference strains. However, no clear proportional relationship among the four genes was found, and 20-30% of selected strains underperformed in shake flasks, indicating screening/fermentation inconsistency.
limitations: No clear proportion relationship between MVK, PMK, MVD, IDI and lycopene flux
Small sample size (500 out of 10,000) limited detection of associations
20-30% of selected strains performed worse than controls in shake flasks
Yield lower than previously reported 448 mg/g DCW

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a deep generative model based on autoregressive factorization of sequence probability.

evidence.quote_or_span: State-of-art computational methods rely on models that leverage evolutionary information but are inadequate for important applications where multiple sequence alignments are not robust.
We introduce a deep generative model adapted from natural language processing for prediction and design of diverse functional sequences without the need for alignments.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No alignment-free method exists that can predict mutation effects and design functional sequences for these challenging cases.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An autoregressive generative model adapted from NLP can predict and design functional sequences without alignments.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design the model architecture.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model can learn from natural sequences to capture higher-order constraints.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Alignment-based methods fail for antibodies, disordered proteins, and indels.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use dilated CNNs instead of LSTM/GRU.

evidence.quote_or_span: Instead, we parameterize this process with dilated convolutional neural networks (Supplementary Fig. 1), which are feed-forward deep neural networks that aggregate long-range dependencies in sequences over an exponentially large receptive field

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to parameterize the autoregressive likelihood to capture long-range dependencies in protein sequences.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Dilated convolutional neural networks can aggregate long-range dependencies without gradient issues.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate model on mutation effect prediction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Dilated CNNs avoid exploding gradients and capture long-range dependencies.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to implement model.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on 40 deep mutational scan datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: We first test our method on the problem of prediction of mutation effects, which are typically analyzed using alignment-based statistical methods.
Without using alignments, the autoregressive model matches alignment-dependent state-of-art missense mutation effect prediction (DeepSequence) for 40 different deep mutational scan experiments.
we validated our model first on deep mutational scan data, with on-par performance with the best currently available model

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the alignment-free model can match alignment-based methods on mutation effect prediction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can predict mutation effects as accurately as DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Further validate on nanobody thermostability and indel fitness.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model matches state-of-the-art alignment-based method DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model architecture chosen; need to test predictive performance.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Train on ~1.2M llama nanobody sequences and test on thermostability data.

evidence.quote_or_span: The autoregressive model (blue) can learn from natural sequence repertoires of llama nanobodies to predict the thermostability of llama nanobody sequences with variation in the framework and complementarity determining regions with greater accuracy than HMMs (orange).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the model predict nanobody thermostability better than HMMs?

evolution_trajectory.observation: The model predicts thermostability with greater accuracy than HMMs.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated on missense mutations; need to test on antibody-related tasks.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on IGP dehydratase dataset with indels and missense mutations.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the model predict fitness effects of insertions and deletions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can handle variable-length mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to sequence generation for nanobody library design.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AUC = 0.90, outperforming HMM (AUC 0.88).
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model works for missense and stability; need to test on indels.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop DockThor-VS web server.

evidence.quote_or_span: the success of virtual screening experiments depends on factors that are often not trivial to be addressed by non-specialist researchers
possible genomic variations in the active/binding site region of molecular targets can drastically affect the binding mode and affinity of ligands

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Virtual screening success depends on factors like target structure preparation, compound library preparation, receptor flexibility, and genomic variations, which are challenging for non-specialists.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A web server providing curated structures of SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets (wild type and non-synonymous variants) and drug libraries will facilitate repurposing virtual screening and help assess the impact of mutations on drug binding.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop DockThor-VS web server with curated structures for six SARS-CoV-2 targets and their non-synonymous variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: we present the developments implemented in the DockThor-VS web server to provide a virtual screening (VS) platform with curated structures of potential therapeutic targets from SARS-CoV-2 incorporating genetic information regarding relevant non-synonymous variations
DockThor-VS provides ready-for-docking 3D structures for wild type and selected mutations for Nsp3 (papain-like, PLpro domain), Nsp5 (Mpro, 3CLpro), Nsp12 (RdRp), Nsp15 (NendoU), N protein, and Spike

evolution_trajectory.decision: For each protein target, select a set of structures representing different conformations (e.g., with varying ligand types, side chain conformations).

evidence.quote_or_span: For each protein target, we chose a set of structures representing different relevant conformations complexed with varying types of ligand (e.g., ligands covalently bound or not, side chains conformations closing or opening the binding site), thus providing an ensemble of representative structures of the flexibility of the receptor.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Receptor flexibility can significantly affect ligand binding; using a single conformation may lead to false negatives.

evolution_trajectory.observation: we found that the majority of the predicted binding affinities are moderate in the range of high to low micromolar affinity units. This result is expected since many drugs under clinical trials exhibited no activity or only modest inhibitory effects against SARS-CoV-2 in some experimental studies reported in the literature.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Docking scores available.

constraints: Use only FDA-approved drugs from e-Drug3D library
Docking results must not be used for clinical guidance
input_data: Curated 3D structures of SARS-CoV-2 targets (wild type and selected mutations)
initial_request.known_context: Virtual screening success depends on target structure preparation, compound library preparation, receptor flexibility, and genomic variations. Non-specialist researchers often struggle with these factors.
quantifiable_goals: Identify promising drugs under clinical trials against COVID-19
Assess impact of non-synonymous variations on drug binding
initial_request.research_problem: The COVID-19 pandemic created an urgent need for effective treatments. Virtual screening success depends on accurate target structures, receptor flexibility, and genetic variations. The study aims to develop DockThor-VS, a web server providing curated 3D structures of SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets (wild type and non-synonymous variants) and drug libraries, to facilitate repurposing virtual screening and assess the impact of mutations on drug binding.
initial_request.target_object: SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets (Nsp3 PLpro, Nsp5 Mpro, Nsp12 RdRp, Nsp15 NendoU, N protein, Spike) and their non-synonymous variants

risk_notes: Added step 12 to cover K17 hypothesis on multi-target inhibition.
Corrected residue numbering in step 7 from Arg92 to Arg102.
Ensured step 10 observation includes pH range 6.6 to 7.4.
Reframed step 12 (now step 13) as comparative analysis decision.

source.title: Drug design and repurposing with DockThor-VS web server focusing on SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets and their non-synonym variants

success_verification.final_conclusion: DockThor-VS provides a platform for virtual screening against SARS-CoV-2 targets, incorporating receptor flexibility and genetic variations. Ledipasvir, imatinib, lopinavir, and daclatasvir were identified as promising multi-target drugs. Multiple conformations reduce false negatives; mutations may confer resistance. Docking results are not for clinical guidance.
limitations: Docking results must not be used for clinical guidance; top hits need experimental validation.
Protein dynamic effects caused by mutations are not taken into account by the docking protocol.
Platform currently covers only six targets; continuous updates planned.
validation_methods: Comparison with experimental studies from literature

Therefore, the $GltB^{E686Q}$ mutation was introduced either into the plasmid-less precursor strain GSLA2, which overproduces L-lysine, or its derivative GSLA2G that carries the gdh deletion.

Preparations of TmTrpBs WT-003-1-A, Q90\*-003-1-A, and Tri-100-2-A, the 60 new variants, and four top-performing TrpB benchmark variants from past classical directed evolution campaigns (including TmTriple) were all tested for product formation with indole by UV absorption and nine indole analogs by high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) to detect substrate promiscuity (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Attribute diversity to depth, scale, and cryptic variation.

evidence.quote_or_span: A partial explanation may come from the high primary activities of OrthoRep-evolved TmTrpBs, as validated by kinetic measurements showing that variants tested have $k_{cat}/K_{M}$ values for indole well in the $10^{5} M^{-1} s^{-1}$ range.

Since OrthoRep drove the evolution of TmTrpB in a continuous format for >100 generations, each resulting TmTrpB is the outcome of many rounds of evolutionary improvement and change (evolutionary depth).
We attribute this to the fact that we ran our evolution experiments in multiple independent replicates (evolutionary scale).

A third explanation for the promiscuous profile diversity of these TmTrpB variants is that each replicate evolution experiment had, embedded within it, mechanisms to generate cryptic genetic variation without strong selection on primary activity.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What factors contributed to the observed diversity and promiscuity?

initial_request.known_context: Classical directed evolution lacks the depth and scale of natural evolution. OrthoRep is a continuous evolution system that may overcome this limitation.
quantifiable_goals: Generate diverse TmTrpB variants with expanded promiscuous activities
Achieve robust growth without tryptophan in indole-supplemented media
initial_request.research_problem: Recapitulating the depth and scale of natural ortholog evolution in the laboratory to generate diverse enzyme variants with expanded promiscuous activities.

source.title: Scalable continuous evolution for the generation of diverse enzyme variants encompassing promiscuous activities

success_verification.final_conclusion: OrthoRep continuous evolution successfully generated diverse TmTrpB variants with expanded promiscuous activities, including a variant uniquely accepting 5-CF₃, by selecting only on primary activity. The diversity is attributed to evolutionary depth, scale, and cryptic genetic variation.
limitations: Evolved diversity is lower than natural orthologs (median 4.3% vs. 11% for human-mouse orthologs)
Only one unique promiscuous activity (5-CF₃) was identified among 60 variants
The approach has only been demonstrated on TmTrpB
metrics: Number of consensus and clonal mutations
validation_methods: Growth assays in indole-supplemented media lacking tryptophan

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a method using inducible ATFs to control pathway gene expression.

evidence.quote_or_span: Balanced expression of multiple genes is central for establishing new biosynthetic pathways or multiprotein cellular complexes.
high-level expression of pathway genes often increases metabolic burden
The most significant limitation of RE-based assembly methods is their sequence dependency, as RE IIs recognition sites must be eliminated from all DNA sequences to be assembled (“DNA domestication”).

GG-based methods are entirely based on construction steps performed in E. coli, which often is not straightforward, in particular for assemblies of genes of long pathways; amplification of large plasmids is not easily done in E. coli

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to achieve balanced expression of multiple pathway genes while minimizing metabolic burden and overcoming limitations of current assembly methods.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Inducible artificial transcription factors (ATFs) can allow conditional expression of genes, decoupling growth and production phases and reducing metabolic burden.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design a cloning system based on this hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Inducible ATFs allow a conditional expression of genes.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Balanced expression of multiple genes is difficult due to unknown optimal levels, and high-level expression often increases metabolic burden. Existing Golden Gate cloning methods are limited by sequence dependency and E. coli assembly difficulties.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design COMPASS with three-level cloning (Entry, Acceptor, Destination vectors) and positive selection.

evidence.quote_or_span: COMPASS allows the rapid combinatorial assembly of up to ten pathway genes, each transcriptionally controlled by nine inducible ATF/binding site (BS) units
COMPASS makes use of positive selection protocols, which severely reduces the need for checking individual constructs
COMPASS is equipped with multi-locus CRISPR/Cas9-mediated modification capacity

An important aspect of COMPASS is that it only requires 11 core vectors (one Entry, eight Acceptor, and two Destination vectors) to establish combinatorial libraries of thousands to millions of constructs for ten-gene pathways.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a cloning system that enables rapid combinatorial assembly of pathways with ATF control.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A three-level cloning system with positive selection and CRISPR/Cas9 integration can efficiently generate combinatorial libraries.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test ATF activity in genomic context to validate the design.
evolution_trajectory.observation: COMPASS requires only 11 core vectors to generate libraries of millions of constructs for ten-gene pathways.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that inducible ATFs can decouple growth and production.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Integrate nine ATF/BS regulators into a second locus (XII-5) and quantify transcriptional output.

evidence.quote_or_span: we tested whether the genomic locus into which the nine regulators are integrated affects their transcriptional activity. To this end, we placed them into a second locus, XII-5, and quantified the transcriptional output and noise level.

the genomic position affected the transcriptional output of the plant regulators. However, the expressional effect exerted by the different regulators was well preserved. The strongest regulators always led to the highest expression mean of the reporter protein, regardless of where the regulator was placed in the genome

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether genomic position affects ATF transcriptional output.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Genomic position may affect ATF activity but relative strengths should be preserved.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to combinatorial assembly of pathway modules.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Genomic position affected transcriptional output, but relative strengths of regulators were preserved.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: COMPASS design established; need to validate ATF activity in genomic context.

evidence.quote_or_span: Epistasis phenomena can impair the predictions in protein engineering and screening.
In this sense, machine learning has not been applied in order to evolve enzyme mutants with enhanced or inverted stereoselectivity.

We propose a new approach based only on the amino acids sequence and using digital signal processing (FFT) and protein spectrum to modelling and predict the biological activity/fitness and for identifying combination of single points amino acid substitutions with improved fitness.

Among the 28 multiple point mutants, the model DSB\_FFT generates more accurate predictions for 22 mutants, better results than the one based only on the addition of mutations (see Fig.

We created a validation dataset with the 28 multiple point mutants (Table S3), including the mutant LW202, that were previously reported (among a total of 512) $^{50}$ , in order to check the accuracy of our models based on only 9 single point mutations.

Based on combinations of the 9 single point mutations ( $2^{9}$ ), the enantioselectivity of these 512 variants were predicted, and candidates were experimentally checked: better mutants with higher enantioselectivity were indeed found.
With only 37 mutants, our model was able to predict the potential outcome of 512, which means that on the basis of only 7% of the mutants, a whole fitness landscape was predicted.

constraints: No 3D structure required
Only sequence and fitness data
input_data: 9 single point mutants of ANEH with experimental E-values
initial_request.known_context: Epistasis impairs predictions in protein engineering; machine learning has not been applied to evolve enzyme mutants with enhanced or inverted stereoselectivity.
quantifiable_goals: Predict improved enantioselective variants beyond LW202 (E=115)
initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution of enantioselective enzymes is limited by epistasis, where non-additive effects of mutations impair prediction of improved variants. The goal is to develop a machine learning method that predicts amino acid interactions from sequence and fitness data alone, without requiring 3D structure, to guide the engineering of enantioselective enzymes.

source.title: A machine learning approach for reliable prediction of amino acid interactions and its application in the directed evolution of enantioselective enzymes

success_verification.final_conclusion: innov'SAR with FFT successfully predicted improved enantioselective variants of ANEH, with experimental E-values up to 253, surpassing previous best LW202 (E=115). The method captured epistasis and required only 7% of the fitness landscape for training.
limitations: Application limited to recombining mutations present in the training set; predicting non-learned mutations remains future work.
validation_methods: Experimental testing of top predicted mutants

evidence.quote_or_span: The lack of rapid and generalized high-throughput screening (HTS) systems has proven to be a major bottleneck when it comes to creating biocatalysts via directed evolution and protein engineering approaches.
most of the enzymes screened to date are expressed within the cytoplasm of Escherichia coli cells, which means that a lysis step must take place inside the droplets for enzyme activity to be assayed.
we take advantage of the excellent secretion abilities of the yeast Yarrowia lipolytica to describe a highly efficient expression system particularly suitable for the droplet-based microfluidic HTS.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Encapsulate libraries, apply heat shock (90 °C, 30 s), and assay xylanase activity before and after.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using the droplet-based microfluidic HTS system described above, both libraries were analyzed before and after a heat shock of 90 °C for 30 s
The JMY4510 (xlnc) wild-type, library A, and library B were encapsulated in 20 pl droplets as described above.
To generate the heat shock, the droplets were run through PTFE tubing (0.56 mm × 1.07 mm) immersed in a 90 °C water bath. The flow rate was set such that residence time within the tubing was 30 s.
The droplets were then collected in a glass vial and reloaded into the integrated screening device to assay post-heat-shock levels of xylanase activity.

After exposure to heat shock, 1% of the clones (8 clones) in library A were found to have higher activities than the wild-type xylanase (>mean wild-type activity + 3σ]), while this percentage was 0.17% (1 clone) for library B.

Before exposure to heat shock, 27% of the clones (216 clones) in library A and 9% of the clones (54 clones) in library B displayed xylanase activity. None of these clones were found to have greater activity than wild-type xylanase (>mean wild-type activity + 3σ).

evolution_trajectory.decision: Select 129 single point mutants from 8,455 possible using three approaches: alanine scanning of active-site residues, random selection near active site, and Foldit-based student designs.

evidence.quote_or_span: To select a subset of the 8,455 possible single point mutations to the native BglB sequence that could be experimentally characterized, three approaches were taken.
First, all residues within 12 Å of the modeled pNPG were individually mutated to alanine.
Second, a subset of mutants was chosen at random by selecting a random mutation to residues within 12 Å of the active site.

Third, a model of the BglB-pNPG complex was loaded into Foldit, a graphical user interface to Rosetta, point mutations to the protein were modeled, and a subset were chosen by students learning about molecular modeling.

evidence.quote_or_span: Three molecular modeling approaches were taken in this study.
Third, the FoldX position-specific scoring matrix (PSSM) algorithm was used.

To evaluate the ability of molecular modeling software to predict thermal stability of mutants in our data set, we modeled each of the 129 point mutations to the BglB sequence using three approaches: 1) an enzyme-specific algorithm termed RosettaDesign [11], 2) an algorithm for predicting $\Delta \Delta G$ of point mutations to proteins termed Rosetta $\Delta \Delta G$ [12], and 3) an algorithm for predicting $\Delta \Delta G$ of mutations to proteins using FoldX [9].

Second, mutations were generated and scored using the Rosetta ddg\_monomer application, with recommended settings previously validated on experimental data [20], and the results were averaged across all 50 iterations.
Of 79 mutants for which $T_{m}$ was determined, 43 mutants have a $T_{m}$ that falls within $1^{\circ}C$ of the wild type $T_{m}$ .

source.title: Thermal stability and kinetic constants for 129 variants of a family 1 glycoside hydrolase reveal that enzyme activity and stability can be separately designed

success_verification.final_conclusion: Protein stability and enzyme kinetic constants can be separately designed in BglB, as evidenced by weak correlations and low trade-off percentages. Current computational methods show weak correlation with experimental stability, highlighting the need for improved algorithms.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design SNAP2 as a neural network classifier to predict molecular function change, not disease.

evidence.quote_or_span: Elucidating the effects of naturally occurring genetic variation is one of the major challenges for personalized health and personalized medicine.

In contrast, the current version of our SNAP (Screening for Non-Acceptable Polymorphisms) method, SNAP2, does not predict the variant effect as “disease or not” but rather as “change of molecular function or not”.

Here, we introduce SNAP2, a novel neural network based classifier that improves over the state-of-the-art in distinguishing between effect and neutral variants. Our method's improved performance results from screening many potentially relevant protein features and from refining our development data sets.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing methods do not predict molecular function change directly; SNAP predecessor may be improved.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A neural network classifier trained on diverse variant data and many features can better distinguish effect from neutral variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to compile training data for the classifier.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2 designed to improve over SNAP by screening many features and refining datasets.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Need to distinguish functional from neutral sequence variants for personalized health; existing methods focus on disease, not molecular function change.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Compile training data from PMD, EC, OMIM, HumVar, LacI, and HIV-1 protease; assign effect/neutral classes.

evidence.quote_or_span: PMD annotations with ‘no change’ (‘=’) qualification (function equivalent to wild-type) were assigned to the ‘neutral’ class, while variants with any level of increase (‘+’, ‘++’, ‘+++’) or decrease (‘-’, ‘- -’, ‘- - -’) in function were assigned to the ‘effect’ class.
First, $PMD + EC + Disease$ were compiled into one comprehensive set termed ALL with 101,515 variants (40,478 neutral, 61,037 effect) in 9,744 proteins.
We extracted 22,858 human disease-associated variants in 3,537 proteins from OMIM [24] and HumVar [25].
This approach identified 51,817 variants (neutral: 13,638, effect: 38,179) in 4,061 proteins.
We also included data from comprehensive studies of particular proteins, namely LacI repressor from Escherichia coli [31] (4,041 variants) and the HIV-1 protease [32] (336 variants).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need diverse and balanced training data with careful class assignment.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need cross-validation strategy to avoid overfitting due to sequence similarity.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt SPM strategy with microchip-synthesized degenerate oligonucleotides and select codons NWG, NWC, NSG to avoid stop/cysteine and reduce redundancy.

evidence.quote_or_span: The limited library sequence diversity together with excessive redundancy and labour-consuming procedure for candidate identification are two major obstacles to widespread adoption of this technology.

We hereby describe a novel library generation and screening approach to address the problems. The approach started with the targeted diversification of multiple complementarity determining regions (CDRs) of a humanized anti-ErbB2 antibody, HuA21, with a small perturbation mutagenesis strategy.
A combination of three degenerate codons, NWG, NWC, and NSG, were chosen for amino acid saturation mutagenesis without introducing cysteine and stop residues.
we demonstrated that a combination of NWG, NWC and NSG codons can be used to diversify a single position into nineteen different amino acids.

More recently, a variety of commercial microarrays that can simultaneously synthesize a massive number of non-degenerate oligonucleotides have been successfully used for cost-efficient gene assembly $[14–16]$ as well as library construction for nucleic acids $[17]$ , peptides $[18]$ , or antibodies $[19]$ .

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to design a library that maximizes functional diversity while minimizing redundancy and avoiding deleterious mutations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Targeted diversification of multiple CDRs using small perturbation mutagenesis (SPM) with carefully chosen degenerate codons can create a high-quality library.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design specific libraries based on this strategy.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SPM allows systematic introduction of mutations into up to three random candidate positions per CDR, and the chosen codons encode 19 amino acids without stop/cysteine, reducing redundancy.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Traditional phage display libraries have limited diversity and high redundancy, hindering efficient affinity maturation.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design five SPM libraries targeting 40 positions, using microchip-synthesized oligos.

evidence.quote_or_span: In total, 7,749 degenerate oligonucleotides were synthesized on two microchips and released to construct five single-chain antibody fragment (scFv) gene libraries with $4 \times 10^{6}$ DNA sequences.
forty candidate positions from the L1, L3, H1, H2, and H3 CDRs were selected for randomization.
The use of three degenerate codons, NWG, NWC and NSG, led to the translation of nineteen different amino acids with the least distribution variation and codon redundancy.
In summary, all forty scFv-Fc mutants were expressed in normal or high levels, and a very large proportion of them (39/40) displayed decreased EC50s compared to HuA21.
Furthermore, almost half of the highly abundant mutants (17/40) were missed in target-binding screening.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design an 8-variant 5'-UTR library for gapA in a hydrogen-producing E. coli strain and measure hydrogen production.

evidence.quote_or_span: The designed 5'-UTR library (8 variants) was predicted to yield more than a 100-fold range of expression levels
The one variant (H5) showed a 2.5-fold increase in the yield of hydrogen production.
The total amount of hydrogen evolved in the H5 strain was also about 4-fold higher than that of the control strain (H0)

evidence.quote_or_span: UTR Library Designer generated sequence libraries that achieved $10^{5}$ - and $10^{2}$ -fold expression changes for ppc and gapA, respectively.
a random approach that generated 10,000 different sets of library pools was largely unable to achieve such expression changes from in silico analysis (P-value $< 10^{-4}$

evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate the probability from the in silico random library pools.

evidence.quote_or_span: the probability of the random library including a variant with an expression level similar (95%–105%) to that of the optimal value obtained was approximately 10% (11.31% for ppc and 5.25% for gapA out of 10,000 library pools)

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the probability that a random library contains a variant with expression similar to the optimal?

evolution_trajectory.observation: The probability of the random library including a variant with an expression level similar (95%–105%) to that of the optimal value obtained was approximately 10% (11.31% for ppc and 5.25% for gapA out of 10,000 library pools).
evolution_trajectory.state_before: The method outperforms random, but the practical likelihood of random success is not quantified.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose a grid search strategy where the first screening round identifies a sub-optimal expression level, and subsequent rounds refine the search by narrowing the expression range.

evidence.quote_or_span: our method could be used for a grid search with a broad range of expression levels to find a sub-optimal expression level in the first round of the screening process, while providing an opportunity for additional search by further narrowing the range of expression levels to be explored in subsequent screening rounds.

success_verification.final_conclusion: UTR Library Designer efficiently generates small 5'-UTR libraries that cover broad expression ranges, outperforming random mutagenesis. It successfully optimized lysine and hydrogen production in E. coli, demonstrating its utility for metabolic engineering.

Codon optimization recommended if designed variants fail to cover desired range

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test hypothesis by saturation mutagenesis at W373.

evidence.quote_or_span: On the basis of our previous work we expected to find one or a few functional variants of the weakly catalyzing mutant W373K [7,8].

Thus, although their catalytic activity was significantly impaired, we have demonstrated that variation at position 373 is possible, without complete loss of activity, and that variants with mutations at the position could be used in efforts to modulate and explain $\beta$ -glucosidases' specificity.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown whether other amino acids at position 373 can retain activity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: W373 is critical for activity, so saturation mutagenesis will yield few active variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design saturation mutagenesis library to test hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Expected one or a few functional variants based on previous work.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: W373 is highly conserved; previous W373K mutant has severely impaired activity.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use NNM degeneracy primers for saturation mutagenesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: A library of Zm-p60.1 mutants with variations at the Trp373 position was created using the following set of primers introducing NNM degeneracy:
Mutagenesis by NNM codon degeneration yields 32 codon variants, encoding 18 amino acids and 2 stop codons.

Thus, although their catalytic activity was significantly impaired, we have demonstrated that variation at position 373 is possible, without complete loss of activity, and that variants with mutations at the position could be used in efforts to modulate and explain $\beta$ -glucosidases' specificity.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to create a library covering all possible amino acids at position 373 except Met and Trp.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: NNM codon degeneration will generate a diverse library excluding Met and Trp.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Construct library and screen for activity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Library designed with 32 codon variants encoding 18 amino acids and 2 stop codons.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that W373 is critical.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with screening of obtained colonies.

evidence.quote_or_span: The number of colonies was low due to the low transformation efficiency of the engineered E. coli strain.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Low transformation efficiency may limit library size.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Despite low efficiency, some clones will be obtained for screening.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen available clones for activity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Number of colonies was low due to low transformation efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library designed, transformation into E. coli.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen 21 clones on XGlc plates.

evidence.quote_or_span: The clone with no glucosidase activity proved to be a proline variant with an additional stop codon introduced at position 206 by an unknown error in the mutagenesis procedure.
However, most clones proved to be active, for example 20 of 21 clones initially obtained displayed various levels of $\beta$ -glucosidase activity.

Therefore, we applied saturation mutagenesis followed by activity screening to identify other amino acid residues at the W373 position that could be compatible with the composition of $\beta$ -glucosidase Zm-p60.1 and catalytic effects of the substitutions.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Activity of clones unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Most clones will be inactive if W373 is critical.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Sequence active clones to identify variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 20 of 21 clones were active; one inactive clone had an unintended stop codon at position 206.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Low number of colonies obtained.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence the 20 active clones.

evidence.quote_or_span: We identified 10 variants at the 373 position: Leu (five copies); Pro and Arg (each in three copies); Ser, Ala and Gly (each in two copies); His, Thr, Asp and Phe (all in one copy).

The cost-effectiveness of the mutagenesis strategies was compared using estimates for (a) SDM and sequencing based on costs of commercial oligonucleotide synthesis + mutagenesis (by a QuikChange kit) + DNA isolation (by a Qiagen kit) + commercial sequencing (ca. 100 USD per variant in total), and b) screening only, based on DNA isolation (by a Qiagen kit) + commercial sequencing (ca. 20 USD per variant in total).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which amino acid variants are present and at what frequencies?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sequencing will reveal multiple variants with frequencies reflecting codon usage.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess completeness of library and need for further screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 10 unique amino acid variants identified: Leu (5), Pro (3), Arg (3), Ser (2), Ala (2), Gly (2), His (1), Thr (1), Asp (1), Phe (1).
evolution_trajectory.state_before: 20 active clones identified.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Run Monte Carlo simulation with 1,000,000 iterations.

evidence.quote_or_span: we determined theoretical average numbers of clones needed to find new variants and complete the library in computer simulations (Figure 2).
The simulations show that there is a sharp increase in numbers of clones needed for the last quarter of library construction

The simulation used to calculate theoretical average numbers of clones that needed to be screened for a new hit using each codon degeneration strategy was based on Monte Carlo method (scripts are enclosed as a File S6) implemented in Python 2.7.3 with 1 000 000 iterations simulating random clone picking.
File S6 The simulation used to calculate theoretical average numbers of clones that needed to be screened for a new hit based on Monte Carlo method.

evidence.quote_or_span: The cost-effectiveness of the mutagenesis strategies was compared using estimates for (a) SDM and sequencing based on costs of commercial oligonucleotide synthesis + mutagenesis (by a QuikChange kit) + DNA isolation (by a Qiagen kit) + commercial sequencing (ca. 100 USD per variant in total), and b) screening only, based on DNA isolation (by a Qiagen kit) + commercial sequencing (ca. 20 USD per variant in total).
