evidence.quote_or_span: In surprising contrast to the high conservation at this position all enzyme variants were able to hydrolyze the substrates, albeit at reduced levels (Figure 4).
All purified enzymes were tested for their activity towards the natural substrate trans-zeatin-O-β-D-glucoside (tZOG, OlChemIm, Olomouc, Czech Republic) at a concentration of 1 mM.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze pH profiles of most (W373H) and least (W373K) active variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: File S1 The pH optimum analysis of the most and least active variants (W373H and W373K).
We found no significant difference between the W373K mutant and WT pH profiles, but the pH optimum of the W373H mutant shifted from 5.5 to 6.

Thus, although their catalytic activity was significantly impaired, we have demonstrated that variation at position 373 is possible, without complete loss of activity, and that variants with mutations at the position could be used in efforts to modulate and explain $\beta$ -glucosidases' specificity.
coli expression strain, all 20 variants at position 373 were expressed, purified to homogeneity and tested for catalytic effect.
We identified 10 variants at the 373 position: Leu (five copies); Pro and Arg (each in three copies); Ser, Ala and Gly (each in two copies); His, Thr, Asp and Phe (all in one copy).
These results suggest that mutagenesis at the 373 position can influence the optimum pH of Zm-p60.1, but this effect alone is not the cause of the activity reductions.

Assess activity of all variants on natural and artificial substrates

source.title: Combining Rational and Random Strategies in β-Glucosidase Zm-p60.1 Protein Library Construction

success_verification.final_conclusion: All 18 amino acid variants at position 373 are active but with significantly reduced activity, demonstrating that variation is tolerated but not optimal. The combined random and rational strategy efficiently completed the library.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose a novel method that uses evolutionary information, in the form of sequence profiles from structure families, to guide sequence design.

evidence.quote_or_span: the inaccuracy of the force-field design, which cannot accurately describe atomic interactions or correctly recognize protein folds
We propose a novel method which uses evolutionary information, in the form of sequence profiles from structure families, to guide the sequence design.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to design protein sequences that reliably fold into target structures without relying solely on inaccurate physics-based force fields.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using evolutionary information from structure families can guide sequence design more robustly than physics-based methods.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Develop a computational method that implements this hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Evolutionary information from structure families may provide more robust guidance for sequence design.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Physics-based force fields are inaccurate for protein fold recognition, limiting design of medium-to-large proteins.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop the Evolution-Based Method (EBM) consisting of structural profile construction and profile-guided Monte Carlo sequence space searching simulation.

evidence.quote_or_span: The outline of our protein design procedure is shown in Figure 1, which consists of structural profile construction and the profile-guided Monte Carlo sequence space searching simulation.
The energy function of the MC search consists of two parts. The first part counts for the alignment match of the sequence decoy with the target structural profile
The second part of the energy function contains a physics-based force field from FoldX V3.0b5
For each target, ten independent Monte Carlo runs, each starting from a different random sequence, are performed.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a concrete computational method that combines evolutionary profiles with physics-based packing.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A structural profile-guided Monte Carlo search with a combined energy function (profile match + FoldX) can design sequences that fold to the target.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that I-TASSER can discriminate folded from unfolded designs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Method developed: structural profiles from analogs, Monte Carlo search with combined energy, clustering for final sequence.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that evolutionary information can guide sequence design.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test I-TASSER on previously designed sequences with known experimental folding outcomes.

evidence.quote_or_span: the estimated TM-score and RMSD of the I-TASSER predictions for the designed proteins are highly correlated with the actual TM-score and RMSD, with a correlation coefficient of 0.91 and 0.80, respectively.
the average TM-scores (RMSDs) are 0.718 (3.9 Å) and 0.551 (6.8 Å), respectively

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether I-TASSER folding simulations can reliably distinguish folded from unfolded designs.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: I-TASSER estimated TM-score and RMSD correlate with actual values and can separate folded and unfolded designs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use validated I-TASSER to compare EBM and PBM foldability.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Estimated TM-score and RMSD highly correlated with actual values (correlation 0.91 and 0.80). Average TM-score 0.718 (folded) vs 0.551 (unfolded); RMSD 3.9 Å vs 6.8 Å.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: EBM method developed; need to validate I-TASSER as a foldability discriminator.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design sequences for 87 test proteins using EBM and PBM, then fold with I-TASSER.

evidence.quote_or_span: the RMSD and TM-score for the EBM designed sequences are 2.12 Å and 0.87, respectively, which demonstrated an improved foldability by EBM
The method is tested on the design experiment of 87 non-homologous single-domain proteins covering different fold classes.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether EBM-designed sequences show improved foldability over PBM.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze TM-score distribution to understand PBM unreliability.
evolution_trajectory.observation: EBM: RMSD 2.12 Å, TM-score 0.87. EBM shows improved foldability.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: I-TASSER validated as foldability discriminator.

evidence.quote_or_span: the distribution of TM-scores of the PBM designed sequences is more divergent, i.e. the TM-scores are either very high (>0.85) or very low (<0.35)

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why PBM sometimes fails dramatically.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate NRE for secondary structure, Φ/Ψ angles, and solvent accessibility.

evidence.quote_or_span: The NRE of designed sequence by the EBM is 0.33, which is seven times lower than that by the PBM method

The reduction in the secondary structure error for the EBM method is mainly due to the smoothening effects introduced by the structural profiles and the single-sequence based SS energy terms, which significantly increase the short-range residue cooperation that are usually missed in the physics-based force fields.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How EBM improves secondary structure, torsion angles, and solvation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: EBM reduces normalized relative errors (NRE) in secondary structure, backbone torsion, and solvent accessibility compared to PBM.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Group test proteins by TM-score cutoff of structural analogs and compare RMSD.

evidence.quote_or_span: the average RMSD of the I-TASSER models for the first group of 46 proteins is 1.46 Å, which is lower than the two other groups based on the lower TM-score cutoffs (2.57 Å and 2.84 Å).

the designs for the thioredoxin, CISK-PX and TIF1 domains showed strongly shielded methyl shifts between 0.5 and -1.0 ppm and had well-resolved peaks in the amide region, features that are indicative of proteins possessing stable folds.

In reality, recent studies have demonstrated that the current PDB library is approaching to completeness and almost all the proteins, including random homopolypeptides, can have protein analogs with similar folds (TM-score>0.5) in the PDB [62–64].

We found that 32% of residues in the conserved positions in the EBM sequences are identical to that in the target and 44% of residues in the conserved regions are highly homologous (with a BLOSUM62 mutation score>0.5) to the corresponding residues in the target.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Select five designs (hnRNPK, thioredoxin, CISK-PX, LOV2, TIF1) for expression, purification, and characterization by CD and NMR.

evidence.quote_or_span: five sequences were randomly selected from the design pool and subjected to experimental validation. The results showed that all the designed proteins are soluble with distinct secondary structure and three have well ordered tertiary structure
all the designed domains successfully expressed and were soluble

the hnRNPK and Lov2 designs lacked upfield methyl chemical shifts (-1.0-0.5 ppm) and had sparse features in the protein amide range (5.5–10.0 ppm) (Figures 7F and 7I), which suggest that they do not possess a stable fold.

the designs for the thioredoxin, CISK-PX and TIF1 domains showed strongly shielded methyl shifts between 0.5 and -1.0 ppm and had well-resolved peaks in the amide region, features that are indicative of proteins possessing stable folds.

source.title: An Evolution-Based Approach to De Novo Protein Design and Case Study on Mycobacterium tuberculosis

success_verification.final_conclusion: EBM significantly improves foldability over PBM, with average RMSD 2.12 Å and TM-score 0.87 on test set. Evolution terms are major driver. Large-scale application to 243 M. tuberculosis proteins successful. Experimental validation: 3/5 designs have stable folds. Limitations include high computational cost with FoldX (16.5 hours) and failure of two designs to fold stably.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a custom NimbleGen solid-phase capture array targeting 3382 exons of 193 genes, with sample barcoding for multiplexing.

evidence.quote_or_span: When multiple genes need to be analyzed for mutations simultaneously, the gathering of information becomes laborious, expensive and time consuming using the Sanger method.
Although NGS technology can be used to analyze gene variants with high throughput screening, its high cost still prevents its application in whole human genome resequencing projects.
we have designed a custom high-density, solid-phase array (Roche NimbleGen, Inc., Madison, WI, USA) to capture 193 distinct genes involved in 103 different genetic diseases.
the DNA adapter-ligated and indexed fragments from 10 libraries were pooled and hybridized to the capture array for 72 h.

To discover DNA variants of disease-causing genes involved in genetic disorders, we designed a unique high-density capture array to capture the 3382 exons and adjacent intron sequences of 193 genes causing distinct hereditary diseases (approximately 1.54 Mb, Table S1) that have a higher incidence in the Chinese population.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for a cost-effective, high-throughput method to sequence multiple disease genes simultaneously.

evidence.quote_or_span: we selected 7 samples from seven patients with six different genetic diseases resulting from six disease-causing genes and 100 samples from normal human adults as controls.
99.54% of the targeted region was covered in at least a 30-fold depth in each sample

74% of the yielded clean reads could be uniquely matched to target regions, and 99.54% of the targeted region was covered in at least a 30-fold depth in each sample (Figure 2).

evidence.quote_or_span: We finally identified 6317 variants, including 583 indels and 5734 substitutes.

we sequenced 193 genes in DNA from 100 blood samples from 100 human adults and constructed our own polymorphism database for these 193 genes. We then considered the variants that did not overlap with our annotated SNPs (with an allele frequency more than 5%) for the 193 genes as candidate novel mutations for further analysis
Out of the 79 total candidate mutations, 7 mutations occurred in 6 disease-causing genes

We then considered the variants that did not overlap with our annotated SNPs (with an allele frequency more than 5%) for the 193 genes as candidate novel mutations for further analysis, including 29 known substitutions, 41 novel substitutions and 9 novel indels.
Among these variants, 84.4% (4841/5734) were termed as “known SNVs”, which were well known SNPs that had been deposited in the dbSNP database, HGMD, LSMD, and 1000 human genome dataset.

our annotated genetic polymorphism database does not present the highest possible level of representativeness and comprehensiveness

ike behavior and decreased whenever the GC content of the exon was too high or low.

evidence.quote_or_span: Hybridization to a solid-phase array takes almost three days (65–72 hours) and is a time-consuming process, making array-based hybridization technology slightly slower for clinical diagnoses compared with solution-based approaches, which require approximately 24 hours.
array-based methods are hampered with regard to further optimization due to their technological complexity. If a probe has to be added or changed, the whole array must be synthesized de novo.

limitations: Incomplete polymorphism database leads to false positives (72 additional candidates)
GC content bias reduces coverage in extreme GC exons (36% with depth <30×)
Array-based hybridization is time-consuming (65-72h) and less flexible

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop an ML-guided cell-free platform to address the limitations of traditional directed evolution.

evidence.quote_or_span: Enzyme engineering is limited by the challenge of rapidly generating and using large datasets of sequence-function relationships for predictive design.
screening strategies are generally low throughput, which constrains resampling mutations in iterative site saturation mutagenesis campaigns and can miss epistatic interactions
selection methods for directed evolution focus on “winning” enzymes for a single transformation, which limits the ability to collect positive and negative sequence-function relationships

we develop a machine learning (ML)-guided platform that integrates cell-free DNA assembly, cell-free gene expression, and functional assays to rapidly map fitness landscapes across protein sequence space and optimize enzymes for multiple, distinct chemical reactions.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Run traditional iterative saturation mutagenesis (ISM) campaigns for moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine.

evidence.quote_or_span: In parallel, we conducted a more traditional directed evolution campaign on each amide product (moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine) via iterative saturation mutagenesis (ISM).
This would provide valuable higher order mutations to validate and benchmark model performance given our objective of extrapolating from single to higher order mutations.
The campaign for cinchocaine was more difficult to navigate and we failed to observe beneficial mutations beyond a double mutant, despite taking multiple ISM paths (Fig. S14).
This result (i.e., running into dead ends during ISM) supported the need to include ML models in our framework that might capture epistatic interactions.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design augmented ridge regression ML models with variant features concatenating site-specific amino acid encodings and zero-shot fitness predictions.

evidence.quote_or_span: The key idea was to use single mutant data from the HSS to extrapolate higher order mutants with increased activity (Fig. 3A).

To achieve this goal, we chose to fit augmented ridge regression models with our data—as such models are simple and have been previously shown to outperform more sophisticated models for protein engineering
McbA variant feature representations consisted of site-specific amino acid encodings concatenated with a zero-shot fitness prediction
Ridge regression models were augmented following the code accompanying the elegant work of Hsu et al.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose an AF3-assisted multi-task topological Laplacian (MT-TopLap) strategy to bypass experimental structure determination.

evidence.quote_or_span: the DMS experiments are typically a bottleneck in response to fast evolving viruses
the earliest experimental DMS on the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein RBD mutations was not available until September 2020, nine months after the outbreak
We propose an AlphaFold 3 (AF3)-assisted multi-task topological Laplacian (MT-TopLap) strategy to address this need.

we leverage AF3's predicted structures to extract persistent Laplacian features. As such, we can speed up in-silico DMS predictions by bypassing the experimental determination of PPI complex structures.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can AF3-predicted structures substitute experimental structures for TDL-based DMS prediction without significant performance loss?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AF3-predicted structures can substitute experimental structures for TDL-based DMS prediction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the MT-TopLap model integrating features from AF3 structures.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AF3 can predict 3D structures of RBD-ACE2 complexes, enabling feature extraction without experimental structures.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental DMS and 3D structures are bottlenecks for rapid response to viral evolution.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design MT-TopLap with persistent Laplacian features from AF3 structures, auxiliary features, and ESM-2 transformer embeddings, trained on SKEMPI 2.0 with multi-task learning.

evidence.quote_or_span: MT-TopLap integrates persistent Laplacian, auxiliary, and pre-trained transformer features to predict BFE changes upon RBD DMS mutations.

SKEMPI 2.0 is used as training data with a multi-task deep learning approach that simultaneously learns single-site mutational patterns with experimental BFE changes and DMS profiles with experimental enrichment ratios.

the AF3 generated 3D SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 complex is used to generate persistent Laplacian features and auxiliary features. Persistent Laplacian features provide a topological representation, using element-and site-specific atom sets to simplify the structural complexity of protein–protein complexes and encode vital biological information into topological invariants.

The ESM-2 pre-trained transformer is used to convert amino acid sequence of the PPI into a pre-trained transformer embedding. All these features are concatenated and fed into MT-TopLap to predict the BFE change upon mutation.

we perform dataset-level 10-fold cross-validation on four experimental SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 datasets. The learning rate is set to 0.0001 and 500 epochs are used for the pre-training while 200 epochs are used for the validation step.

MT-TopLap requires the 3D structures of new viral variants to achieve accurate predictions. Such structures are typically not available until many months later during pandemics.

evidence.quote_or_span: After 36 h of cultivation, a total of 23,118 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were identified

Through a comprehensive comparison of the transcriptome results of the two strains at two time points, we found that the transcriptionally active period of the wild-type strain did not end at 48 h of fermentation, and the difference in transcriptional activity was small compared with that at 36 h, which was mainly reflected in the small number of DEGs (Table 2).

These results indicate that the main mechanism for increasing the yield of TFA and DHA in the optimal ALE strain at 48 h of fermentation was the increase of acetyl-CoA, NADPH and PKS synthesis pathway fluxes.

Importantly, the ALE\_AC\_11 strain exhibited significantly lower expression of genes encoding RNA polymerase (RNAP) at 48 h than 36 h, indicating a potential decline in transcriptional activity during this metabolic transition period.

In addition to thraustochytrids, Crypthecodinium cohnii, produces high-purity DHA but has a lower biomass (20–30 g/L) and DHA yield (1.4–3 g/L), and is highly sensitive to dissolved oxygen (DO), limiting its industrial scale production $[10, 11]$ .

evidence.quote_or_span: Although excessive metabolic modification increases the production of tyrosol, it also causes a decrease in the growth rate of yeast.
this study attempted to restore the growth of S. cerevisiae through adaptive evolution and further improve tyrosol production.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement ALE with automatic passaging at OD600=15 and stepwise glucose increase (20→40→80→125 g/L).

evidence.quote_or_span: The initial glucose concentration of the evolution medium was 20 g/L, and the amount of added glucose gradually increased during the process of evolution.

In stage 2, the glucose concentration was increased from 40 to 80 g/L. A total of 20 passages were conducted in this stage, and strain S26-AE2 was obtained. The strain was passaged once every 11.75 h on average
In stage 3, the glucose content increased from 80 to 125 g/L, and the strain was passaged once every 11.95 h on average. The evolutionary strain in stage 3 was named S26-AE3.
In stage 1, the glucose concentration was increased to 40 g/L, and the strain was named S26-AE1. At this stage, the average passage time was 16 h.

SNZ3 contained mutations at amino acid residues 118, 122, and 125; synonymous mutations were identified at amino acids 118 and 122, and a nonsynonymous mutation was identified at amino acid residues 125.

we speculated that after adaptive evolution, the decreased expression of HXK2 in strain S26-AE2 reduced the transcriptional regulation of glucose repression in cells

evidence.quote_or_span: After SNZ3 mutation, the Vina score for the interaction with the small-molecule D-ribose 5-phosphate decreased to -5.7
the interaction of the PRO51, PRO363, MET365, and GLY366 residues with small molecules was reduced, and the binding of the ALA348, ILE398, and CYS408 residues was increased

The docking results showed that after the introduction of the $\mathrm{PHO3}^{\mathrm{Asn134Asp}}$ mutation, its Vina score for the interaction with the small-molecule substance thiamine phosphate decreased to $-6.6$ , which indicated that the affinity between the receptor and the ligand improved (Table S3).
Molecular docking and reverse engineering verified the effectiveness of the SNZ3 $^{Val125Ile}$ mutation in increasing tyrosol production.

initial_request.research_problem: Excessive metabolic engineering in S. cerevisiae S26 increased tyrosol but impaired growth, limiting industrial applicability. The goal was to restore growth and further improve tyrosol yield via adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE), then identify and validate the underlying genetic and transcriptomic mechanisms.

source.title: Transcriptome analysis and reverse engineering verification of SNZ3Val125Ile and Pho3Asn134Asp revealed the mechanism of adaptive laboratory evolution to increase the yield of tyrosol in Saccharomyces cerevisiae strain S26-AE2

success_verification.final_conclusion: ALE successfully restored growth and increased tyrosol production in S26-AE2. SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation was validated to increase tyrosol by 17.01%, but double mutant with PHO3 did not show additive effect. Transcriptome and SNP analyses provided mechanistic insights, but direct enzyme activity measurements are lacking.
limitations: PHO3 single mutant effect not clearly reported
Other SNP genes (MAL32, DOT6, HXT3) not explored
Mechanism remains hypothetical without direct enzyme activity measurements

source.title: Scanning the active center of formolase to identify key residues for enhanced C1 to C3 bioconversion

success_verification.final_conclusion: Scanning saturation mutagenesis of 25 active-center residues identified S236F as the top variant with 7.6-fold improved DHA formation and 2.6-fold higher DHA/GA ratio. MD simulations revealed increased local hydrophobicity as the mechanism. However, S26F single mutation was ineffective, and discrepancies between whole-cell and purified enzyme activities were noted. Only single-site mutations were tested, and combinatorial effects remain unexplored.
limitations: Only single-site mutations tested; combinatorial effects unknown
Discrepancy between whole-cell and purified enzyme DHA activities
Kinetic constants of S236F not significantly higher than wild-type
Potential trade-offs in enzyme stability or expression not addressed
validation_methods: Biological replicate re-testing of top variants

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ALE on B. subtilis J46 in galactose-containing medium.

evidence.quote_or_span: Efficient utilization of galactose by microorganisms can lead to the production of valuable bio-products and improved metabolic processes.
While Bacillus subtilis has inherent pathways for galactose metabolism, there is potential for enhancement via evolutionary strategies.
This study aimed to boost galactose utilization in B. subtilis using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) and to elucidate the genetic and metabolic changes underlying the observed enhancements.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use M9 minimal medium with 2% galactose, initially supplemented with yeast extract (0.11% or 1% at different stages), then phased out; transfer every 24 h.

initial_request.research_problem: Bacillus subtilis J46 grows poorly on galactose (specific growth rate ~0.03 h⁻¹), limiting its use on soybean biomass.

source.title: Engineering Bacillus subtilis J46 for efficient utilization of galactose through adaptive laboratory evolution

success_verification.final_conclusion: ALE successfully enhanced galactose utilization in B. subtilis J46, yielding strain BSGA14 with tenfold growth increase. Key mutations araR H226R and glcR frameshift account for >80% of fitness gain, validated by reverse engineering. Evolved and engineered strains show improved enzyme production in soybean medium.
limitations: Other mutations beyond araR and glcR may contribute to fitness
Scale-up and industrial application not tested
Mechanism of glcR contribution not fully elucidated
validation_methods: Reverse engineering of single and double mutants
Growth profiling in minimal and soybean media

When tested using attNanno x attNannoL substrates, recombination by Int-h/218 + N99D and Int C3 + N99D was potentially improved over Int-h/218 and Int C3 (Fig 5).
Conversely, the D99N reversion in Int C4 and Int C6 potentially led to reduced efficiency of recombination.
We therefore generated a panel of constructs to interrogate the N99D mutation.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design Int C7 comprising N99D, H329R, and core C3 mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: we generated a construct termed Int C7 (Table 1) comprising the expected minimal set of mutations required for improved recombination of the novel attNanno1 and attHNanno substrates (N99D and H329R) in addition to the core C3 mutations. Expressed and purified C7 showed 345-fold improvement over C3 for recombination of attNanno1 substrates and 1097-fold improvement for recombination of HTN substrates

Based on results highlighting a potential key role for the N99D mutation in observed phenotype, we generated a construct termed Int C7 (Table 1) comprising the expected minimal set of mutations required for improved recombination of the novel attNanno1 and attHNanno substrates (N99D and H329R) in addition to the core C3 mutations.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Express and purify Int C7 and test recombination on attNanno1 and attHNanno substrates.

evidence.quote_or_span: Expressed and purified C7 showed 345-fold improvement over C3 for recombination of attNanno1 substrates and 1097-fold improvement for recombination of HTN substrates (Fig 8A).
The results showed that with 750ng and 1000ng of transfected target vector, both junctions can be generated by nested PCR, which was verified by PCR product sequencing

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform MD simulations on Int C7 and related complexes.

evidence.quote_or_span: This large network of interactions was observed to be stable for >75% of the simulation time and is likely a key determinant of the observed specificity switch.

MD simulations provide additional insight into the orthogonal phenotypes observed in this study. A favourable network of interactions promotes binding and activity of Int variants comprising N99D in the DNA core binding domain to substrates with the cytosine to guanine transversion.

H329 in the Int catalytic domain is distal to bound DNA. Mutation to R329 potentially introduces up to 4 intra-subunit salt bridges that could stabilize tetramer binding to DNA and favour recombination of non-cognate sequences.
The combined H329R and N99D mutations present in C7 therefore result in loss of the N99-DG8 interaction but improved R329-D351 interaction (attB-Int $^{C7}$ complex, Fig 10, S4 and S6A Figs).
However, the H329R mutation resulted in an R329 –D351 salt bridge interaction that was stable for 60% of the simulation time for the attB-Int $^{C3\_H329R}$ complex (S4 and S5B Figs).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the mechanistic basis for the specificity switch conferred by N99D and H329R?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: N99D enables interaction with cytosine in variant sites, and H329R forms salt bridge with D351 stabilizing tetramer.

constraints: Must target exogenous sequences in Phaeodactylum tricornutum and Nannochloropsis oceanica

initial_request.known_context: Intrinsic sequence specificities of recombinases curtail their use to DNA sequences with sufficient homology to endogenous target motifs.
quantifiable_goals: Improve recombination efficiency on non-cognate substrates
Achieve >1000-fold specificity switch
initial_request.research_problem: Lambda integrase is efficient for large DNA integration but limited by intrinsic sequence specificity to cognate attB sites. The goal is to engineer variants targeting pseudosites in microalgae and human genomes to expand the DNA editing toolbox.

source.title: Expanding the DNA editing toolbox: Novel lambda integrase variants targeting microalgal and human genome sequences

success_verification.final_conclusion: Directed evolution yielded Int C7 with N99D and H329R mutations, achieving >1000-fold specificity switch for attNanno1 and attHNanno substrates. Int C7 enabled single-copy genomic integration at a safe harbor locus in human cells with sustained transgene expression. Microalgae targeting failed likely due to DNA uptake/expression issues.
limitations: Microalgae targeting failed likely due to DNA uptake/expression issues.
RMCE limited by availability of pseudo sites and cognate enzymes.
validation_methods: PCR and sequencing of recombination junctions

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform phylogenetic and recombination analyses to determine origin and estimate Re.

evidence.quote_or_span: Our phylogenetic analyses suggested that XBB emerged through the recombination of two cocirculating BA.2 lineages, BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1 (a progeny of BA.2.75), during the summer of 2022.

Our analysis suggests that the XBB clade's most recent common ancestor (tMRCA) existed at the start of July 2022 (median posterior date: July 7, 2022; 95% HPD confidence intervals: from June 10, 2022, to July 29, 2022).
we first split the alignment used for the recombination analysis above at genome position 22,920 (the breakpoint inferred by RDP5)

XBB.1 exhibited profound (13-fold) resistance to breakthrough BA.5 infection sera (P<0.0001, Fig. 2b).
10 out of 14 breakthrough BA.2 infection sera and 9 out of 20 breakthrough BA.5 infection sera fail to neutralize XBB.1.
XBB.1 significantly escaped from monovalent vaccine sera (23.3-fold, P<0.0001), BA.1 bivalent vaccine sera (19-fold, P<0.0001), and BA.5 bivalent vaccine sera (41-fold, P<0.0001) compared with B.1.1.

our data suggest that multiple substitutions in the XBB.1S cooperatively contribute to the resistance against humoral immunity induced by breakthrough BA.2 infection.

initial_request.known_context: XBB emerged through recombination of BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1, with a breakpoint in the RBD. It shows higher effective reproduction number (Re) than parental lineages, indicating increased fitness through recombination.
initial_request.research_problem: Investigate the virological characteristics of the SARS-CoV-2 XBB variant, a recombinant lineage from two Omicron subvariants (BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1), to understand how recombination impacts viral fitness, immune evasion, receptor binding, fusogenicity, and pathogenicity, and to explain XBB's rapid global spread.

source.title: Virological characteristics of the SARS-CoV-2 XBB variant derived from recombination of two Omicron subvariants

success_verification.final_conclusion: XBB variant emerged through recombination of BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1, increasing fitness via recombination. XBB.1 exhibits profound immune resistance, enhanced ACE2 binding, increased infectivity and fusogenicity, but lower intrinsic pathogenicity in hamsters compared to Delta, comparable to BA.2.75. Discrepancy between fusogenicity and pathogenicity may be due to non-S mutations or model limitations.
limitations: Discrepancy between fusogenicity and intrinsic pathogenicity may be due to non-S mutations, theoretical limits, or hamster model differences.
Hamster model may not reproduce human condition due to potential differences in ACE2 tropism and affinity.
validation_methods: In vitro growth kinetics and airway-on-a-chip

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a supervised deep-learning model (SESNet) that leverages local evolutionary context, global sequence features, and structure information.

evidence.quote_or_span: Deep learning has been widely used for protein engineering. However, it is limited by the lack of sufficient experimental data to train an accurate model for predicting the functional fitness of high-order mutants.
we develop SESNet, a supervised deep-learning model to predict the fitness for protein mutants by leveraging both sequence and structure information, and exploiting attention mechanism.

Our model integrates local evolutionary context from homologous sequences, the global evolutionary context encoding rich semantic from the universal protein sequence space and the structure information accounting for the microenvironment around each residue in a protein.
we use Potts model to establish the local encoder

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to accurately predict fitness of high-order mutants with limited experimental data?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating sequence and structure information with an attention mechanism can improve prediction accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to benchmark the designed model against existing methods.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Model architecture defined to integrate multiple information sources.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Deep learning models for protein fitness prediction require large experimental datasets, which are scarce for high-order mutants.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Benchmark SESNet against supervised (ECNet) and unsupervised (ESM-1b, ESM-1v, MSA transformer) models on 20 DMS datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: in 19 out of 20 datasets, the supervised models generally outperform the unsupervised ones as expected, and our model (SESNet) achieves the best performance among all the models.

we further explored the ability of our model to predict the fitness of higher-order variants by training it using the experimental results of the low-order variants on 6 datasets of DMS. As shown in Fig. 2B and C, our model outperforms all the other models.
we randomly split a given dataset into five folds by randomized shuffling and splitting.
We evaluated the performance for predicting the fitness of high-order mutants by the model trained on low-order mutants.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does SESNet outperform existing models on DMS datasets?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SESNet will achieve state-of-the-art performance on fitness prediction across multiple DMS datasets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze contribution of each model component via ablation study.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SESNet achieves best performance in 19 out of 20 datasets and outperforms all models on high-order mutant prediction.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: SESNet architecture designed but not yet evaluated.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ablation study by removing each component and measuring average Spearman correlation.

evidence.quote_or_span: the average spearman correlation of the original model is 0.672, much higher than that without local encoder (0.639), that without global encoder (0.247) and that without structure module (0.630).

The ablation study reveals that all three components contribute to the improvement of model performance, and the contribution from the global encoder, which captures the sequence feature in global protein sequence universe, is the most significant.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which components of SESNet are most critical for performance?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: All three components (local, global, structure) contribute positively, with global encoder being most significant.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate attention weights to understand structure module's role.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Average Spearman: original 0.672, no local 0.639, no global 0.247, no structure 0.630. Global encoder most significant.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose data augmentation: pre-train SESNet on ESM-IF1 predicted fitness for all single and 30,000 random double mutants, then fine-tune on limited experimental data.

evidence.quote_or_span: we propose a data augmentation strategy by leveraging the data from unsupervised models to pre-train our model.

our model can achieve strikingly high accuracy in prediction of the fitness of protein mutants, especially for the higher order variants (>4 mutation sites), when finetuned by using only a small number of experimental mutation data (<50).

we propose a simple strategy of data augmentation by using the result generated by one unsupervised model to pre-train our model on a given protein, and then fine-tuning it using a limited number of experimental results on the same protein.
We used the fitness results of low-order mutants predicted by the unsupervised model, ESM-IF1, to pre-train our model.
we first built a mutant library, which contains all the single-site mutants and 30,000 double-site mutants randomly selected from tens of millions of saturated double-site mutants.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to achieve high accuracy with very limited experimental data (<50 points)?

evolution_trajectory.observation: Strategy designed to leverage unsupervised predictions for initialization.

source.title: SESNet: sequence-structure feature-integrated deep learning method for data-efficient protein engineering

success_verification.final_conclusion: SESNet integrates sequence and structure information with attention, achieving state-of-the-art performance on 19/20 DMS datasets. A data augmentation strategy using unsupervised model pre-training enables high accuracy with very limited experimental data, especially for high-order mutants.
limitations: Structure module insufficient for capturing precise structural information for optimizing fluorescence intensity in GFP.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Pursue computational design of affinity-enhanced ACE2 decoys.

evidence.quote_or_span: SARS-CoV-2, especially B.1.1.529/omicron and its sublineages, continues to mutate to evade monoclonal antibodies and antibodies elicited by vaccination.
there is an urgent need to develop broad-spectrum pan-coronavirus therapeutics that can protect against both future SARS-CoV-2 variants and developing SARS-associated viruses
Any mutation in the RBD that limits binding to the sACE2 derivative will likely have reduced binding towards native ACE2 receptors potentially making the virus unfit to propagate.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for broad-spectrum pan-coronavirus therapeutics that resist mutational escape.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Affinity-enhanced soluble ACE2 decoys can outcompete native ACE2 and resist mutational escape because mutations reducing decoy binding would also reduce native receptor binding.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesis motivates design of first-generation decoy.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Any mutation in the RBD that limits binding to the sACE2 derivative will likely have reduced binding towards native ACE2 receptors potentially making the virus unfit to propagate.

The second-generation ACE2 decoy with 4 mutations (T27F, Q42L, L79I, and N330F) is referred to as FLIF throughout the manuscript.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use CL-FEP instead of MM/GBSA for binding affinity predictions.

evidence.quote_or_span: the MM/GBSA method overestimates the binding energy due to the disregard of entropy which is a major source of error and computational expense in MM/GBSA calculations

The conformational entropy change is usually computed by normal-mode analysis on a set of conformational snapshots taken from MD simulations. In this case, contribution from entropy is neglected because of its large computational cost and low prediction accuracy.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: MM/GBSA overestimates binding energy due to neglect of entropy.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: CL-FEP provides more accurate absolute binding free energies.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Accurate predictions guide experimental validation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MM/GBSA overestimates binding energy; CL-FEP accounts for entropy and provides more accurate predictions.

initial_request.known_context: De novo enzymes have been successfully designed but exhibit low catalytic efficiencies. Directed evolution can improve activity, but the structural basis is not fully understood.
initial_request.research_problem: De novo enzymes have low catalytic efficiencies, requiring directed evolution to improve activity. The study aims to understand how directed evolution alters conformational ensembles to enhance catalysis, and to use these insights to improve computational enzyme design by incorporating ensemble modeling of crystallographic data.

source.title: Ensemble-based enzyme design can recapitulate the effects of laboratory directed evolution in silico

success_verification.final_conclusion: Ensemble-based computational design using crystallographic data from a low-activity enzyme can recapitulate the effects of directed evolution, potentially obviating the need for extensive experimental evolution.
limitations: Subtle ensemble changes are difficult to predict computationally.
The approach requires experimental structural data of the low-activity enzyme.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a UMI-linked consensus sequencing (UMIC-seq) method.

evidence.quote_or_span: An erroneous raw third-generation sequencing read cannot reliably be assigned to its template, when multiple template candidates differing only in few point mutations are possible. This inability to assign reads to any one parental molecule therefore renders consensus-based “polishing” from unlinked reads impossible.

we leverage UMI-tags to assign erroneous nanopore reads to their molecule of origin, facilitating clustering for accurate consensus formation even when starting with a pool of highly similar sequences

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to assign erroneous nanopore reads to their original template molecules in a pool of highly similar sequences.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: UMI-tagging can link nanopore reads to their original template molecules, enabling accurate consensus generation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design a method to reduce UMI-variant complexity to make the approach practical.
evolution_trajectory.observation: UMI-tags can be used to assign erroneous nanopore reads to their molecule of origin, facilitating clustering for accurate consensus formation.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: In directed evolution libraries, closely related variants cannot be distinguished by raw nanopore reads, making consensus generation impossible.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Incorporate a transformation step into the UMIC-seq workflow.

evidence.quote_or_span: Uniquely, the UMI-variant complexity is reduced by a transformation step into cells, because an exact number of molecules is represented in the number of transformant colonies, directly combining selective amplification and control over molecule count in a straightforward molecular biology step.

source.title: UMI-linked consensus sequencing enables phylogenetic analysis of directed evolution

success_verification.final_conclusion: UMIC-seq enabled accurate full-length sequencing of directed evolution libraries, revealing long-range sign epistasis between A64E, R102S, and E323V. The founder variant A64E R102S E323V showed improved catalytic performance, and the method provides a cost-effective, high-throughput alternative to Sanger sequencing.
limitations: Synthetic long-read methods suffer from uneven coverage and high chimeric reads.
Current epistasis studies are limited to short domains, missing complete interactions in entire proteins.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a DNA computing module that achieves this transformation.

evidence.quote_or_span: detection signals increase monotonically with increases in concentrations of both correct and spurious targets
a detection window exists between the target concentrations that lead to the same level of detection signals
the success of current strategies is at the cost of shifting detection windows towards the higher concentration end, inevitably sacrificing sensitivities at lower concentration range

DEG offers a paradigm to expand the detection window without compromising sensitivity at lower concentration range by transforming the quantitative relationship between the detection signal and target concentrations from a monotonic sigmoidal function to an asymmetric unimodal function

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to expand the detection window without compromising sensitivity at lower concentration range?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Transforming the quantitative relationship between detection signal and target concentrations from a monotonic sigmoidal function to an asymmetric unimodal function can expand the detection window without compromising low-concentration sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The hypothesis provides a clear direction for designing a DNA computing module to achieve the desired transformation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DEG offers a paradigm to expand the detection window without compromising sensitivity at lower concentration range by transforming the quantitative relationship between the detection signal and target concentrations from a monotonic sigmoidal function to an asymmetric unimodal function.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Current hybridization probes exhibit monotonic signal increase for both correct and spurious targets, leading to narrow detection windows. Existing frustrating probes shift detection windows to higher concentrations, sacrificing sensitivity at lower ranges.

tool_or_method.name: Conceptual design of DNA Equalizer Gate (DEG)

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design DEG with DEPs that are identical in sequence to strand A, forming a three-stranded complex BCD to consume B.

evidence.quote_or_span: DEG is a DNA computing module that acts on dsDNA and allows user-definable transformation of the quantitative relationship between detection signals and target concentrations
B is then consumed by DEPs that are of the identical sequences with A forming three-stranded complex BCD
a maximum yield exists when the concentration of AB equals to those of DEPs
DEG transforms the quantitative relationship between the detection signal and target concentrations from a typical sigmoidal function to a unimodal function.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to implement the transformation in a DNA-based system?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A DNA Equalizer Gate (DEG) using DNA Equalizer Probes (DEPs) can consume complementary strand B, producing a maximum yield of strand A when target concentration equals DEP concentration.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The design provides a concrete mechanism to achieve the desired unimodal response; next step is to model it computationally.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DEG transforms the quantitative relationship between detection signal and target concentrations from a typical sigmoidal function to a unimodal function. A maximum yield exists when the concentration of AB equals that of DEPs.

parameters.reaction: B consumed by DEPs forming BCD complex

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that transforming signal-concentration relationship to asymmetric unimodal can expand detection window.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct a mathematical model with a stoichiometric matrix RM to reduce the number of independent equilibrium equations.

evidence.quote_or_span: A thermodynamic-driven mathematical model was constructed, where the detection window, reaction yield, and sequence specificity can be precisely simulated and predicted in silico
a stoichiometric matrix RM was introduced to help simplify the calculation
The rank of RM was determined to be 4, indicating that four independent equilibrium equations need to be solved.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to simulate DEG behavior to predict detection window, reaction yield, and sequence specificity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A thermodynamic-driven mathematical model using a stoichiometric matrix can simplify the coupled equilibrium equations and simulate DEG behavior.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The model initially failed for [AB] ≤ [DEPs], requiring a correction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The rank of RM was determined to be 4, indicating that four independent equilibrium equations need to be solved. The model can simulate detection window, reaction yield, and sequence specificity.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: DEG design proposed but its behavior needs to be quantitatively predicted.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Incorporate a probability correction into the model.

evidence.quote_or_span: a probability function that took the possible distributions of DEPs on B was necessary to correctly reflect the final equilibrium distribution

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The model does not correctly reflect the final equilibrium distribution for low target concentrations.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A probability function that accounts for the possible distributions of DEPs on B is necessary to correctly reflect the final equilibrium distribution.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: With the corrected model, in silico predictions of discrimination and robustness factors can be performed.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The probability function was necessary to correctly reflect the final equilibrium distribution.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Initial thermodynamic model failed to simulate DEG behavior when [AB] ≤ [DEPs].

evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate dual-channel DEG-PCR using clinical parasitic worm samples from Honduras, targeting the β-tubulin gene codon 200 A to T mutation.

evidence.quote_or_span: We finally employed DEG-PCR for the diagnosis of soil-transmitted helminth (STH) infections with clinical samples collected from school-age children living in highly endemic rural areas in Honduras.
Simultaneous detection of parasitic infection and screening for drug-resistance was achieved using a dual-channel design (FAM- and Cy5-Reporter).
A single nucleotide A to T mutation of this codon is a hotspot for drug resistance screening.

Experimental tests of the dual-channel DEG-PCR using synthetic DNA standards (blue and red dots) and 13 (D.R.-) clinical samples (green circles) as a training set (c) and 8 unknown clinical parasitic worm samples (d).
Error eclipses with 99% confidence interval and 2-degrees of freedom (two fluorescence channels) were used to define D.R. $(+)$ and D.R. $(-)$
Error eclipses with 99% confidence interval and 2-degrees of freedom (two fluorescence channels) were used to define D.R.+ and D.R.-.

initial_request.research_problem: Discriminating single nucleotide variants (SNVs) in dsDNA using hybridization probes suffers from a trade-off between sensitivity and specificity, resulting in narrow detection windows where signals from correct and spurious targets overlap.

source.title: Expanding detection windows for discriminating single nucleotide variants using rationally designed DNA equalizer probes

limitations: Quantification ambiguity: a given yield can correspond to two different target concentrations

evidence.quote_or_span: L377M, the only residue that exhibited decreased activity relative to WT, appears to partially seal this proposed cavity by its increased size and enhanced interaction with Val89
Leu377 from the substrate-binding domain and Val89 from the catalytic domain in WT SaGGR form van der Waals interactions between methylene groups (grey) that comprise the tunnel's native contacts.
This tunnel appears to be occluded in the L377M mutant, which is the only mutant shown to decrease reductase activity toward farnesol.

In the remaining mutants enhancing reductase activity, they either expand the substrate-binding tunnel (L377G or L377T) or provide charges that could enhance protein–solvent interactions via hydrogen-bonding oxygens (red) or nitrogens (blue) (L377N, L377R, L377D, L377H)

evidence.quote_or_span: In an attempt to enhance the $H_{2}O_{2}$ tolerance of DyP4, we created 4 methionine-substituted variants (M43L M77L M253L and M253F).

success_verification.final_conclusion: Directed evolution using BENNY yielded variant 4D4 with 2.7-fold improved kcat/Km and 7-fold improved H2O2 tolerance (IC50 7.03 mM vs WT 0.97 mM), along with higher protein yield. BENNY system enabled high-throughput screening but has a secretion bottleneck.

evidence.quote_or_span: K778F, D377L/G422L, D377L/K778F) and antagonistic (T114F/S597W, D336L/S597W, D377L/S597W) interactions between aa residues constituting the double mutant (Table 4, Fig.

The next four double mutants (T114F/D377L, D336L/K778F, D377L/G422L, D377L/K778F) showed a synergistic effect on protein $\Delta\Delta G_{FOLD}$ , suggesting the presence of anti-cooperatively interacting residues.

In addition, the double mutant cycle analysis for $\Delta\Delta G_{FOLD}$ indicated the presence of antagonistic (T114F/S597W, D336L/S597W, D377L/S597W) interactions between aa residues constituting the double mutant.

As the antagonistic effect was very weak, it is possible that both double mutants (T114F/S597W, D336L/S597W) are in fact formed from single mutants, suggesting an additive effect takes place rather than an antagonistic one.

source.title: Computer-Aided Saturation Mutagenesis of Arabidopsis thaliana Ent-Copalyl Diphosphate Synthase

success_verification.final_conclusion: A computational pipeline identified 6 single mutants (T114F, D336L, D377L, G422L, S597W, K778F) and 7 double mutants with predicted improved stability and binding affinity for GGPS. The pipeline was validated by correctly rejecting 11 known deleterious mutants. However, no experimental validation was performed, and the D336L mutation may impair reprotonation of D379, potentially reducing catalytic activity.

rare copy number variations in pharmacogenes, accounting for up to 1% of all loss-of-function alleles, are an additional source of genetic variability with relevance for drug metabolism phenotypes that are commonly not considered by computational functionality prediction algorithms
For many drugs, genetic variability cannot be directly translated into effectiveness or ADR susceptibility due to the interdependency of different metabolic pathways

data regarding the effects of such gene-gene or gene-drug interactions is sparse, which complicates predictions of drug effectiveness or safety even when rare genetic variants are incorporated into the analyses

initial_request.known_context: Over 90% of genetic variability in pharmacogenes is rare, but its impact on drug pharmacokinetics has not been systematically evaluated.
quantifiable_goals: Quantify the contribution of rare variants to functional variability in pharmacogenes
Assess the impact of rare variants on drug response predictions for five drugs
initial_request.research_problem: Current pharmacogenetic predictions rely on common variants and miss rare variants, leaving substantial unexplained inter-individual variability in drug response. The study aims to systematically evaluate the contribution of rare genetic variants in pharmacogenes to functional variability and drug response predictions.

source.title: Integrating rare genetic variants into pharmacogenetic drug response predictions

success_verification.final_conclusion: Rare genetic variants have the potential to explain a substantial part of unexplained genetic variability in drug metabolism phenotypes, with gene- and drug-specific contributions. For some drugs (e.g., warfarin, irinotecan), rare variants are major contributors; for others (e.g., simvastatin, voriconazole, olanzapine), their impact is smaller but still notable.
limitations: Exclusion of synonymous, UTR variants, and rare CNVs
Inability to directly translate genetic variability to effectiveness due to pathway interdependency
Sparse gene-gene and gene-drug interaction data
metrics: Percentage of functional variability explained by rare variants
Aggregated frequency of rare deleterious alleles
validation_methods: Computational prediction framework with 92% sensitivity and 95% specificity

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a dual-channel microfluidic droplet screening (DMDS) platform with two working modes: cooperative mode for activity and biased mode for enantioselectivity.

evidence.quote_or_span: Screening such huge mutagenesis libraries for the directed evolution of enzymatic enantioselectivity has been especially challenging, owing to the requirement of performing chiral chromatography, a high-cost and labor-intensive analytical process.
existing droplet technologies lose some of this promise when one considers that they lack the ability to screen for enzymatic enantioselectivity.
Our system is capable of evaluating two reaction channels simultaneously, through the use of a dual-fluorescence detection/sorting microfluidic device.
we define this two selection substrate screening mode as the “DMDS cooperative mode” (sorting the double-positive population in Fig. 1g).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No high-throughput method exists to directly screen for enantioselectivity in enzyme libraries.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A dual-channel microfluidic droplet screening platform can simultaneously evaluate two substrates, enabling high-throughput screening for enantioselectivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Platform design enables high-throughput screening; next, generate and screen a mutant library for improved activity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DMDS platform designed with dual-fluorescence detection/sorting to evaluate two reactions simultaneously. Cooperative mode uses two substrates with same reactive group but different fluorophores to select for catalytic activity. Biased mode uses two substrates differing in reactive groups and fluorophores for positive/negative selection to screen enantioselectivity.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Directed evolution of enantioselective enzymes is limited by screening throughput (<10^4 colonies/day) and costly chiral chromatography. Existing droplet microfluidic systems (FADS) use only one substrate and cannot screen for enantioselectivity.

source.title: A synthetic microbial biosensor for high-throughput screening of lactam biocatalysts

success_verification.final_conclusion: A synthetic microbial biosensor (CL-GESS) was developed and optimized through promoter engineering and directed evolution of NitR, enabling high-throughput screening of metagenomic libraries. This led to the discovery of CF3HBD, a novel cyclase that converts 6-ACA to ε-caprolactam without cofactors. Structural and mutagenesis studies identified key catalytic residues and yielded a Y152A mutant with 3-fold improved activity.
limitations: Low background signal persists in CL-GESS, possibly due to leaky expression or transcriptional noise, limiting sensitivity.

evidence.quote_or_span: To apply RAPID, several conditions must be met: (i) single cells must be cultivated in a metabolically active and productive state in picoliter droplets; (ii) RNA aptamers must allow target concentration measurements in picoliter droplets; (iii) the aptamer must remain sensitive and specific even in the presence of the producer cell and growth medium for the several day production phase; and (iv) the microfluidic system must perform all requisite operations efficiently and allow recovery of live cells for downstream cultivation and sequencing analysis of responsible proteins or pathways.

evidence.quote_or_span: Here we introduce the algorithm RedLibs (Reduced Libraries) that identifies globally optimal degenerate RBSs that uniformly span the entire TIR space in a linear manner.

RedLibs compares the TIR distributions of all possible degenerate RBSs to a desired target distribution, leading to truly uniform predicted distributions that can be implemented by simple one-pot cloning protocols.

We applied two different RBS diversification strategies: an unstructured randomization in which six or eight nucleotides were fully randomized ( $N_{6}$ or $N_{8}$ ) as well as diversification using smart RBS libraries with target sizes of 4, 12 and 24 members produced by RedLibs to resemble a uniform distribution.

evidence.quote_or_span: Opposing views on increased or decreased structural rigidity of the folded state have been put forward in this context.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Target the prone EGFR tetramer interface for virtual screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Overexpression or mutation of the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) potently enhances the growth of many solid tumors.
Tumor cells frequently display resistance to mechanistically-distinct EGFR-directed therapeutic agents, making it valuable to develop therapeutics that work by additional mechanisms.
Recent studies have identified a novel prone extracellular tetrameric EGFR configuration, which we identify as a potential target for drug discovery.

Our focus is on the prone EGFR tetramer, which contains a novel protein-protein interface involving extracellular domain III. This EGFR tetramer is computationally targeted for stabilization by small molecule ligand binding.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for therapeutics that work by additional mechanisms beyond existing EGFR inhibitors.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Stabilizing the prone EGFR tetramer via small molecules targeting its novel protein-protein interface can modulate EGFR signaling and internalization.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesis leads to design of virtual screening against the tetramer interface.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The prone EGFR tetramer contains a novel protein-protein interface involving extracellular domain III, suitable for small molecule targeting.

constraints: Compounds must be drug-like (LogS > -6.0)
Compounds should target the tetramer-specific interface
input_data: Molecular dynamics simulation of the head-to-head prone tetramer
Life Chemicals library of 345,232 drug-like compounds
initial_request.known_context: Recent studies identified a prone extracellular tetrameric EGFR configuration with a novel protein-protein interface involving domain III. Tumor cells frequently display resistance to mechanistically-distinct EGFR-directed therapeutic agents.
quantifiable_goals: Identify small molecules that modulate EGFR function via tetramer stabilization
Achieve a hit rate above random selection
initial_request.research_problem: EGFR overexpression/mutation drives many solid tumors, but resistance to current therapies (antibodies targeting extracellular domain, kinase inhibitors) necessitates new mechanisms.
initial_request.target_object: EGFR prone tetramer extracellular domain interface

risk_notes: All coverage requirements met with exact evidence quotes.

source.title: Compounds identified by virtual docking to a tetrameric EGFR extracellular domain can modulate Grb2 internalization

success_verification.final_conclusion: Virtual screening against the EGFR tetramer interface identified 14 hit compounds that modulate Grb2 internalization. Hits showed minimal effects on phosphorylation, and docking analysis revealed common interaction with Arg470. The study demonstrates a novel approach to target EGFR quaternary structure, but direct binding and mechanism remain unconfirmed.
limitations: Grb2 screening assay does not diagnose mechanism (stabilization, destabilization, or other oligomeric changes)
No direct binding evidence that active compounds bind to EGFR tetramer as predicted by docking
Modest viability effects and lack of strong signaling modulation
Future medicinal chemistry and target engagement studies needed
metrics: Oligomeric state distribution (% monomer, dimer, trimer, tetramer)
validation_methods: Biophysical validation of Grb2-tetramer interaction from literature

evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with multi-faceted SNP discovery and PSV exclusion strategies.

evidence.quote_or_span: In Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar), these goals are currently hampered by the lack of a high-density SNP genotyping platform.

a multi-faceted approach to SNP discovery was applied using a combination of RR-Seq, RAD-Seq and RNA-Seq alongside several strategies for exclusion of paralogous sequence variants (PSV) including RR-Seq of haploid material.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to discover a large set of reliable SNPs given the salmonid-specific whole genome duplication that generates paralogous sequence variants (PSVs).
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A multi-faceted SNP discovery approach using RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, and RNA-Seq, combined with strategies for PSV exclusion including RR-Seq of haploid material, can yield a dense, reliable SNP array.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design and execute SNP discovery experiments using RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, and RNA-Seq on diverse samples.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known challenges of salmonid genome duplication and previous SNP discovery efforts.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-density SNP genotyping platform exists for Atlantic salmon, hampering genetic studies and breeding programs.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform RR-Seq on 56 fish (including haploid embryo), RAD-Seq on 160 fish, and RNA-Seq on 72 fish.

evidence.quote_or_span: three main Illumina – based sequencing strategies were applied; RR-Seq (56 fish), RAD-Seq (160 fish) and RNA-Seq (72 fish).
the RR-Seq also included a pool of wild fish from diverse geographical sources and a single haploid Atlantic salmon embryo for the purpose of PSV exclusion

Libraries were checked for quality and quantified using the Bioanalyzer 2100 (Agilent, USA), before being sequenced in barcoded pools of 12 individual fish on the Illumina Hiseq 2000 instrument (100 base paired-end sequencing, v3 chemistry) and all sequence data were deposited in the ENA under accession number ERP003968.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which samples and sequencing strategies will maximize SNP discovery while enabling PSV exclusion?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using RR-Seq on farmed and wild fish plus a haploid embryo, RAD-Seq on families, and RNA-Seq on whole fry will provide a comprehensive SNP set.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Align reads to reference genome and call putative SNPs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sequencing libraries prepared and sequenced on Illumina HiSeq 2000.

parameters.RR-Seq samples: 56 fish (farmed and wild, plus haploid embryo)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to generate sequencing data from diverse Atlantic salmon samples.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Align reads to reference genome (GCA_000233375.1), call variants, and apply filters (allele frequency, read depth, Mendelian errors, repeat masking).

evidence.quote_or_span: Results: SNP discovery was performed using extensive deep sequencing of Reduced Representation (RR-Seq), Restriction site-Associated DNA (RAD-Seq) and mRNA (RNA-Seq) libraries derived from farmed and wild Atlantic salmon samples (n = 283) resulting in the discovery of > 400 K putative SNPs.
Following the quality-control filtering of these putative SNPs (described in 'Methods'), 99,097 (RR-Seq), 83,151 (RAD-Seq) and 229,754 (RNA-Seq) candidate SNPs remained
Of the putative SNPs remaining, those with an allele frequency of ≤0.1 or a read depth of ≤10 (n = 172,501) were removed.
Duplicated reads originating from PCR were marked using Picard and subsequently ignored.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How many putative SNPs can be discovered and how to filter them for array design?

evolution_trajectory.observation: Initial putative SNPs: >400K. After QC: 99,097 (RR-Seq), 83,151 (RAD-Seq), 229,754 (RNA-Seq).

parameters.filters: allele frequency ≤0.1, read depth ≤10, Mendelian errors, repeat masking

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sequencing data generated; need to identify candidate SNPs.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Submit 411,308 candidate SNPs to Affymetrix for in silico p-convert prediction; tile probes for 266,105 SNPs with recommended or neutral scores.

evidence.quote_or_span: All candidate SNPs (total of 411,308; 4,139 of which were detected in more than one SNP discovery category) were submitted to Affymetrix for in silico prediction of their probability of conversion to a reliable assay on the Axiom array (p-convert score).

All 'recommended' probes were included and 'neutral' probes were included if paired with a 'recommended' or a 'neutral' probe, resulting in the tiling of probes for 266,105 putative SNPs, this being 93% of the capacity of the array.
the final array contained 286,021 putative SNPs assayed by 443,627 probes.
The candidate SNPs were provided to Affymetrix as 71-mer nucleotide sequences from the forward strand with the alleles at the target SNP highlighted at position 36.
The ssalar01 array was used to genotype 96 Atlantic salmon samples of diverse origin

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which candidate SNPs will convert to reliable assays on the Affymetrix Axiom platform?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using Affymetrix's p-convert score to select SNPs will maximize the number of working assays.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Genotype a diverse panel of 96 Atlantic salmon samples to validate array performance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Final array (ssalar01) contains 286,021 putative SNPs assayed by 443,627 probes.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Filtered candidate SNPs available; need to design the physical array.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Genotype 96 samples on the ssalar01 array and classify SNPs using Axiom GT1 algorithm.

evidence.quote_or_span: The ssalar01 array was used to genotype 96 Atlantic salmon samples of diverse origin

a total of 135,682 SNPs were designated as high quality and polymorphic. The main reason for discarding SNPs at this stage was the high rate of monomorphism, with 110,910 SNPs designated as monomorphic

The SNPs were split into categories according to their clustering performance with respect to various Axiom-generated quality-control criteria; (i) ‘polymorphic high resolution’ where the SNP passes all QC, (ii) ‘monomorphic high resolution’ where the SNP passes all QC except the presence of a minor allele in two or more samples, (iii) ‘call rate below threshold’ where genotype call rate is under 97%, (iv) ‘no minor homozygote’ where the SNP passes all QC but only two clusters are observed, (v) ‘off-target variant’ (OTV) where atypical cluster properties arise from variants in the SNP flanking region, and (vi) ‘other’ where the SNP does not fall into any of the previous categories.
In this study, only SNPs from categories (i) and (iv) were included in further analyses.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How many of the tiled SNPs will be polymorphic and high quality?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Genotyping 96 diverse Atlantic salmon samples will identify a large set of high-quality polymorphic SNPs.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform linkage mapping in SalMap families Br5 and Br6 using 10 offspring per family; assign SNPs to linkage groups at LOD 2.5.

evidence.quote_or_span: This analysis relied on the lack of male recombination in centromeric regions of the male salmonid genome, and this feature facilitated mapping of markers to linkage groups according to identical or near-identical sire-based inheritance patterns.

Secondly, a 'two-point' linkage analysis was performed to determine the LOD scores between all pairs of markers in randomly selected pools of \~5,000 SNPs including anchor markers from each of the 29 pairs of Atlantic salmon chromosomes (Additional file 1: Table S2).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the SNPs be placed on the 29 Atlantic salmon chromosomes?

evolution_trajectory.observation: 40,214 SNPs mapped across all 29 chromosomes.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate correlation between SNP counts per chromosome and Lien et al. map.

evidence.quote_or_span: The number of SNPs per chromosome showed a high positive correlation (r = 0.84, Figure 3B) with the SNP linkage map of Lien et al.
These results are indicative of the validity of the chromosome assignment for the SNPs, and suggestive of their genome-wide distribution.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the chromosome assignment agree with existing maps?

constraints: Must exclude paralogous sequence variants (PSVs) arising from salmonid-specific whole genome duplication
Must achieve high conversion rate from candidate SNPs to working assays on the array

initial_request.known_context: Existing low-density SNP panels and microsatellite markers are insufficient for genome-wide association studies and genomic selection in Atlantic salmon.
quantifiable_goals: Develop an array with >100,000 high-quality polymorphic SNPs
Achieve genome-wide coverage with average spacing <20 kb
initial_request.research_problem: Atlantic salmon lacks a high-density SNP genotyping array, limiting genetic studies and breeding programs. The genome's recent duplication complicates SNP discovery due to paralogous variants.
initial_request.target_object: High-density SNP genotyping array for Atlantic salmon

source.title: Development and validation of a high density SNP genotyping array for Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar)

success_verification.final_conclusion: The ssalar01 array provides a high-density SNP genotyping platform for Atlantic salmon with 132,033 QC-filtered SNPs, validated by linkage mapping and sex prediction, though population structure analysis requires larger sample sizes.
limitations: Small sample sizes for population structure analysis, especially wild samples
High dropout rate for RNA-Seq-derived SNPs (68%)
Ascertainment bias due to SNP discovery in farmed populations
validation_methods: Linkage mapping to assign SNPs to chromosomes

evidence.quote_or_span: In order to handle all these different expression constructs, an efficient parallel cloning method was required.
ccdB introduced into the vector series was designed for strong constitutive expression in order to suppress growth in non-resistant cells at 100% efficiency.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use competitive growth under hydrocarbon stress to enrich for superior AcrB mutants.

evidence.quote_or_span: n-octane has no chromophore, thus efflux rate of n-octane from E. coli cannot be conveniently determined spectroscopically in real time.

Based on the hypothesis that solvent resistance correlates to increased growth and efflux rate, subjecting E. coli cells expressing a mixture of AcrB variants to the toxicity of n-octane would enable the superior mutants to dominate the population

initial_request.research_problem: Biofuel toxicity limits microbial production yield; engineering native E. coli efflux pump AcrB via directed evolution to improve efflux of biofuel molecules like n-octane.

source.title: Directed evolution of an E. coli inner membrane transporter for improved efflux of biofuel molecules

success_verification.final_conclusion: Directed evolution of AcrB yielded variants 1G1 and 1G2 with over 40% increased n-octane efflux. Beneficial mutations N189H, T678S, Q737L, M844L were identified, with N189H and T678S showing synergy. Mutations are distant from the substrate path, validating the directed evolution approach. No increased antibiotic resistance was observed.
limitations: Crystal structures would be required to validate mechanistic hypotheses (EV_C003_E07_01).

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform a screen for high-fidelity variants by selecting for resistance to mutagenic base analogs.

evidence.quote_or_span: we performed a screen for high fidelity variants of CVB3 by selecting for resistance to the mutagenic base analogs, ribavirin and 5-azacytidine.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence passaged virus populations and confirm resistance by reverse genetics.

evidence.quote_or_span: Wild type CVB3 acquired a new CtoU mutation resulting in an Alanine to Valine change at position 372 of the RdRp (A372V) after passage in both ribavirin (by passage 10) and AZC (by passage 20)
The role of A372V in resistance was confirmed by introducing the mutation back into the CVB3 wild type cDNA infectious clone

Wild type CVB3 acquired a new CtoU mutation resulting in an Alanine to Valine change at position 372 of the RdRp (A372V) after passage in both ribavirin (by passage 10) and AZC (by passage 20), that did not arise in untreated control passages.

The role of A372V in resistance was confirmed by introducing the mutation back into the CVB3 wild type cDNA infectious clone and comparing the growth of A372V to wild type virus treated with 3 RNA mutagens with different nucleotide structures: ribavirin (300 $\mu$ M), AZC (300 $\mu$ M) and 5-fluorouracil (FU, 150 $\mu$ M).
