evidence.quote_or_span: Unlike A372V, the mutation frequency of S299T did not suggest higher fidelity compared to wild type (Figure 5F).
As shown in Figure 2, S299T RdRp incorporated more GMP over time than wild type RdRp, consistent with the S299T mutation decreasing RdRp fidelity (Table 1).
To confirm the lower fidelity phenotype conferred by the S299T mutation, an in vitro biochemical assay examining the relative levels of misincorporation with the purified S299T enzyme was performed.

Finally, to examine the potential effects of the S299T mutation on RdRp fidelity, infections were performed in the presence of ribavirin, amiloride, EIPA, or under mock-treatment and mutation frequencies were determined for each population.

The S299T variant seems to resist amiloride on both fronts: RNA synthesis is less inhibited (Figure 5E) and its mutation frequency is unchanged (Figure 5F).

Growth experiments were performed using a high-throughput Bioscreen C plate reader and are the averages of at least 30 independent growth curves each for three independent batches of fungal conidia (>90 cultures).
Both variants displayed robust growth at 36.5°C, whereas only one was able to grow at 37°C.

Due to the reduced sporulation of EVG017, not enough spores could be directly harvested for insect bioassays. Therefore, the strain was passaged once through M. sanguinipes by rubbing the abdomen of host insects on an agar culture of EVG017.

Remarkably, the resulting variant, EVG017g, maintained thermotolerance after passage through the insect and showed increased virulence compared to the non-insect passaged parent strain as measured by $ST_{50}$ .
Similar phenomena, where cycle duration needed to be increased and temperature stabilized to allow fast growing variants to take over, were also seen in cycles 16 (34.6°C) and 20 (38°C).

evidence.quote_or_span: None of the strains were pathogenic or able to cause mortality in hosts at 36°C.

However, when insects infected at 36°C were subsequently placed at 28°C, the hosts were rapidly killed by all three fungal strains, indicating that the wild type and adapted strains remained viable at 36°C, but could not cause pathogenicity and death.

Despite their confirmed thermotolerance, the adapted variants did not show increased virulence at 36–37°C, indicating that the ability to grow in vitro at 36–37°C does not necessarily mean that in vivo growth and pathogenesis will occur.

Assays confirmed that thermotolerant variants were, indeed, entomopathogenic, albeit with complex alterations in virulence parameters such as lethal dose responses ( $LD_{50}$ ) and median survival times ( $ST_{50}$ ).

evolution_trajectory.observation: None of the strains caused mortality at 36°C. At 28°C, variants retained pathogenicity but with higher LD50 and lower ST50 compared to wild type. Infected insects moved to 28°C after 36°C exposure were rapidly killed, indicating viability at 36°C.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze cycle duration and temperature data to identify barriers.

at certain temperatures (32°C, 36.5°C and 37.5°C), it took longer for favorable variants to take over, hence these temperatures were considered as thermal barriers, perhaps requiring multiple or complex mutations to arise in the population.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Conclude that directed adaptation was successful and that pathogenicity can be retained or recovered.

evidence.quote_or_span: We report the successful directed adaption of a eukaryotic organism, namely the filamentous fungus, M. anisopliae, for increased thermotolerance using a novel continuous culture machine whose operation is fundamentally different from chemostats and other current methods.

Selected mutants displayed complex phenotypes with respect to non-selected attributes such as conidiation and virulence, although both parameters could be retained or recovered while maintaining the thermotolerant phenotype.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test TFAM binding using EMSA with oligonucleotide probes containing variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: we employed Electrophoresis Mobility Shift Analysis (EMSA). Specifically, we tested for the effect of transition variants defining Caucasian haplogroup J (C295T) and a sub-haplogroup of J, J1b2
EMSA analysis of double-stranded oligonucleotides spanning each of the two different TFAM binding sites, revealed increased TFAM binding to the C295T variant
this increase in binding was observed at multiple different TFAM to DNA ratios (10/1 and 25/1 molar ratios are shown in Figure 2)
no significant difference with C242T variant (data not shown)

evidence.quote_or_span: The variant defining Caucasian haplogroup J (C295T) increased the binding of TFAM (Electro Mobility Shift Assay) and the capacity of in vitro L-strand transcription, especially of a shorter transcript that maps immediately upstream of conserved sequence block 1 (CSB1)

Both mutant templates showed a trend toward increased LSP transcription activity when either the full-length or shorter product was analyzed, but only in the case of shorter, $\sim$ 160 transcript from the haplogroup J template (C295T) was this difference statistically significant (p-value 0.

Both mutant templates showed a trend toward increased LSP transcription activity when either the full-length or shorter product was analyzed, but only in the case of shorter, $\sim$ 160 transcript from the haplogroup J template (C295T) was this difference statistically significant (p-value 0.034).

Notably, the J1b2 construct showed increased transcription activity, although its additional mutation (C242T) did not significantly affect TFAM binding (data not shown), suggesting that the more functional variant is C295T that defines haplogroup J as a whole.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate cybrids with haplogroup J and H mtDNA backgrounds and measure mtDNA copy number and transcript levels.

evidence.quote_or_span: cybrids (i.e., cells sharing the same nuclear genetic background but differing in their mtDNA backgrounds) harboring haplogroup J mtDNA had a >2 fold increase in mtDNA copy number, as compared to cybrids containing haplogroup H, with no apparent differences in steady state levels of mtDNA-encoded transcripts.

Two genes (i.e. ND1 and ND4L) exhibited reduced steady state levels in only one of the haplogroup J cybrids (designated 3861), whereas the other H- and L-strand transcripts showed no significance difference among the tested cybrids
both haplogroup J cybrids had more than twice as many mtDNAs than did any of the haplogroup H cybrids

Cybrids haplogroup assignment was verified by PCR RFLP of selected polymorphic sites and sequencing and HVR1 and HVR2: cybrids 3861 and 1106 belong to haplogroup J1 and J1b2, respectively, while cybrids CB, LR, NN, SB and TL belong to haplogroup H.

evidence.quote_or_span: it is quite possible that, in addition to its effects on transcription documented herein, the C295T variant also could impact mtDNA replication efficiency
The elevated mtDNA copy number in haplogroups J versus H cybrids support the possibly increased replication capacity

Since the region surrounding CSB1 and CSB2 has been implicated in various aspects of transcriptional priming of mtDNA replication [24,25,26], and given the involvement of TFAM in mitochondrial transcription and replication [16], it is quite possible that, in addition to its effects on transcription documented herein, the C295T variant also could impact mtDNA replication efficiency.
The elevated mtDNA copy number in haplogroups J versus H cybrids support the possibly increased replication capacity of haplogroups J.

evidence.quote_or_span: Since the C295T variant defines haplogroup J and was not fixed in any other human mtDNA lineage, it is possible that the observed functional effect can be partially attributed to its phenotypic effect.

Nevertheless, the C295T nucleotide position underwent only a single fixation event during human phylogeny in the branch leading to haplogroup J, suggesting that, although poorly conserved, the low human variability at this nucleotide position is in line with its functional potential.

source.title: Ancient mtDNA Genetic Variants Modulate mtDNA Transcription and Replication

success_verification.final_conclusion: The C295T variant defining haplogroup J increases TFAM binding and in vitro transcription, and is associated with elevated mtDNA copy number in cybrids, suggesting a functional impact on mtDNA replication. However, steady-state transcript levels are not consistently altered, possibly due to post-transcriptional regulation. Direct mutagenesis is needed for confirmation.
limitations: Inability to perform site-directed mutagenesis in mtDNA
Small sample size: only 2 haplogroup J cybrid lines vs 5 haplogroup H lines
Inconsistent gene-specific transcript variations

source.title: SPACIER: on-demand polymer design with fully automated all-atom classical molecular dynamics integrated into machine learning pipelines

success_verification.final_conclusion: SPACIER successfully integrates automated MD with BO to design polymers with desired optical properties, autonomously learning design principles like sulfonyl groups. Experimental validation confirmed good agreement for P1, but synthesis of P2 failed due to insolubility. Limitations include restricted structural diversity and unclear generalizability of RadonPy predictions.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose to use MolBERT embeddings in a semi-supervised active learning framework.

evidence.quote_or_span: Active learning typically trains models on labeled examples alone, while unlabeled data is only used for acquisition. This fully supervised approach neglects valuable information present in unlabeled molecular data, impairing both predictive performance and the molecule selection process.

We address this limitation by integrating a transformer-based BERT model, pretrained on 1.26 million compounds, into the active learning pipeline. This effectively disentangles representation learning and uncertainty estimation, leading to more reliable molecule selection.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to leverage unlabeled data to improve active learning for molecular property prediction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating a pretrained BERT model (MolBERT) into active learning will disentangle representation learning and uncertainty estimation, leading to more reliable molecule selection.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design semi-supervised active learning using MolBERT embeddings and an MLP head.

evidence.quote_or_span: we propose to use molecular representations from a pretrained self-supervised learning model. Specifically, we encoded the molecular SMILES sequences into corresponding embeddings, utilizing a large transformer model MolBERT, pretrained on 1.6 million SMILES via masking, alongside physicochemical properties
The embedding of each SMILES sequence is a pooled output from the pretrained MolBERT with dimension 764.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare active learning performance using BERT vs ECFP features and EPIG, BALD, Random acquisition functions on Tox21 and ClinTox datasets with scaffold splitting and balanced initial set of 100 molecules.

evidence.quote_or_span: Experiments on Tox21 and ClinTox datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves equivalent toxic compound identification with 50% fewer iterations compared to conventional active learning.
We evaluated the impact of molecular representations on active learning performance using three acquisition strategies (EPIG, BALD, and Random sampling) on two datasets (Tox21 and ClinTox).
Train and test sets are created using an 80/20 scaffold split, with the training set further divided into initial set, validation set, and pool set through random splitting.

BERT-based models efficiently extracted 70% of positive samples in approximately 100 iterations, while ECFP-based models required around 180 iterations—nearly a twofold increase in sample acquisition effort.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze feature representations using UMAP, PCA, and quantitative metrics (Davies-Bouldin index, class purity, Fisher's ratio).

evidence.quote_or_span: The BERT features exhibit more structured organization, where positive samples (red points, 6.8% of dataset) are distributed in distinct clusters
ECFP representations show a more scattered distribution with significant overlap between positive and negative regions
These metrics collectively demonstrate that BERT's structural advantages are fundamental rather than artifacts of visualization choices.

The Davies-Bouldin index, where lower values indicate better cluster separation, shows that BERT (6.046) provides 36.5% better separation between toxic and non-toxic compounds compared to ECFP (9.529).
Fisher's ratio-a direct measure of class separability that quantifies the ratio of between-class to within-class variance-is 2.5 times higher for BERT (0.054) compared to ECFP (0.021).
For the minority positive class (toxic compounds), BERT embeddings demonstrate 69.5% higher neighborhood purity (0.154 vs. 0.091)

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze the cause of performance decline on Pgp inhibition.

evidence.quote_or_span: Both EPIG and BALD with BERT features show decline in average precision after peaking at iterations 150-200.
This decline stems from a distribution mismatch-the pool set has more positives (ratio 1.56) while the test set has more negatives (ratio 0.35).
Figure 10 shows uncertainty-based methods preferentially select minority negative samples from the pool, with BERT-BALD's positive-to-negative ratio dropping from 1.0 to 0.55 by iteration 300.
This sampling initially improves performance but eventually creates harmful distribution shift.

initial_request.research_problem: Data-efficient molecular property prediction in drug discovery using active learning, where traditional methods with handcrafted features (ECFP) struggle with limited labeled data, leading to poor uncertainty estimation and suboptimal sample selection.

source.title: Molecular property prediction using pretrained-BERT and Bayesian active learning: a data-efficient approach to drug design

success_verification.final_conclusion: Integrating pretrained MolBERT embeddings into active learning with EPIG acquisition significantly improves data efficiency for molecular property prediction. BERT-EPIG achieves steeper learning curves, earlier convergence, better calibration, and 2-fold improvement in toxic compound identification over random sampling. However, BALD underperforms random on ClinTox, and distribution mismatch can cause performance decline on some datasets.
limitations: Binary classification only
Limited datasets (Tox21, ClinTox, PAMPA, Pgp)
One pretrained model (MolBERT)
Performance decline on Pgp inhibition due to distribution mismatch
validation_methods: Representation analysis (UMAP, PCA, Davies-Bouldin index, class purity, Fisher's ratio)

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose Hierarchical Grad-CAM (HGE) to obtain local explanations at atom, ring, and entire molecule level.

evidence.quote_or_span: the integration of explainable methods to elucidate the specific contributions of molecular substructures to biological activity remains a significant challenge

Even if the graph representation of a molecule is self-explainable, the precise relation between the relevant molecular properties and the network prediction is not, and it suffers from the same issues observed in classical Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) devoted to images.
SHAP was used to determine the significance of individual features; however, it did not provide insight into their pharmacophoric characteristics.
HGE leverages Grad-CAM to obtain local explanations at atom, ring, and the entire molecule level

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing XAI methods do not provide pharmacophoric-level explanations for GNN predictions.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Reject GNNExplainer; adapt Grad-CAM by removing ReLU and placing explainers at GC layers 1, 3, and 7.

evidence.quote_or_span: We chose Grad-CAM as the best performing technique for labeling pointwise contributions to positive predictions
the ReLU activation function has been removed to capture not only positive contributions to the prediction, but also negative ones.

we placed also a Grad-CAM explainer at the very first GC layer to consider the atoms' impact in a 1-neighborhood that is at a bond level. Another explainer was placed after the third layer to catch information related to 3-neighborhoods.

initial_request.known_context: GNN predictions for molecular bioactivity are not self-explainable; existing XAI methods (SHAP, GNNExplainer) fail to provide pharmacophoric-level explanations.
initial_request.research_problem: Explaining GNN predictions in virtual screening for drug discovery by identifying molecular substructures (moieties) driving bioactivity, to guide medicinal chemists.

source.title: Unveiling molecular moieties through hierarchical Grad-CAM graph explainability

success_verification.final_conclusion: HGE successfully provides hierarchical explanations of GNN predictions, identifying relevant molecular moieties. GCNN outperforms baselines in sensitivity, and HGE offers richer explanations than GNNExplainer. However, multi-target classification remains challenging, and pharmacological effect is not captured.
limitations: HGE identifies binding-relevant structures but does not account for pharmacological effect or potency magnitude.
Single-target focus limits multi-target applicability.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose AI as a solution to replace traditional methods.

evidence.quote_or_span: Traditional drug discovery methods are not able to compete with rapidly occurring viral mutations, since these are often time-consuming and labor-intensive.

the use of AI, which includes a variety of techniques such as machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), natural language processing (NLP), and reinforcement learning (RL), has shown impressive success
AI techniques, such as machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), and natural language processing (NLP), can speed up and enhance this process by analyzing vast datasets efficiently.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a sequence-space diffusion model (ProteinGenerator) based on RoseTTAFold.

evidence.quote_or_span: Although powerful, structure-based approaches, such as RFdiffusion $^{11}$ and Chroma $^{12}$ , provide limited opportunities to guide protein generation using sequence-based features and to identify sequences with more than one fold and/or function.

We reasoned that carrying out diffusion in sequence space rather than structure space could enable guidance of design using sequence-based features and explicit design of sequences populating multiple states.

evidence.quote_or_span: We used PG to address this challenge by scaffolding bioactive peptide sequences within an inert protein cage (Fig. 4a) by designating a region of the protein chain (usually at the N or C terminus) to be fixed at the bioactive peptide sequence and freely diffusing the remainder of the sequence.
Proteins of 155 amino acids in length were generated in 25 steps with spans of helical DSSP conditioning to encourage the model to generate helical bundles and cleavage loops.

source.title: Computational design and engineering of self-assembling multivalent microproteins with therapeutic potential against SARS-CoV-2

success_verification.final_conclusion: Computationally designed trivalent microprotein MP-5ff and its nanobody derivative Tr67 achieved sub-nanomolar binding, broad neutralization of Omicron variants including cluster 2, and induced 3-RBD-up conformation, validating the multivalent design strategy.
limitations: No in vivo therapeutic efficacy data
Pharmacokinetics, immunogenicity, and biosafety not evaluated
Cryo-EM resolution limited to 9 Å
MM/GBSA has limitations in absolute binding free energy prediction

evolution_trajectory.decision: Explore deep learning methods for PLA prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: Determination of protein–ligand binding affinity (PLA) is a key technological tool in hit discovery and lead optimization, which is critical to the drug development process. PLA can be determined directly by experimental methods, but it is time-consuming and costly.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to leverage deep learning to improve PLA prediction efficiency and accuracy.

evolution_trajectory.observation: PLA determination is time-consuming and costly; deep learning has potential to help.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: PLA determination is time-consuming and costly experimentally.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose DeepLIP, a novel DL architecture that employs an early fusion strategy for PLA prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: we proposed DeepLIP, a novel DL architecture that employs an early fusion strategy for PLA prediction.
DeepLIP represents protein binding pockets and ligands as descriptors, thereby mitigating the redundancy of sequence encoding.
this architecture departs from the conventional voxelization representation by introducing spatial graphs.
By fusing these three disparate levels of heterogeneous information, DeepLIP is able to capture multiple representations and combine them for complementary purposes

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to integrate complementary information from ligand, pocket, and interactions to improve PLA prediction accuracy and efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating ligand descriptors, pocket descriptors, and interaction graphs via early fusion can capture complementary information and improve prediction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesis leads to the design of the DeepLIP architecture.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DeepLIP represents protein binding pockets and ligands as descriptors, mitigating redundancy of sequence encoding; departs from conventional voxelization by introducing spatial graphs; fuses three disparate levels of heterogeneous information.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing DL methods for PLA prediction have limitations: sequence-based methods ignore receptor-ligand interactions, structure-based methods lack explicit physicochemical descriptions and are computationally expensive due to 3D voxelization.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design DeepLIP with descriptor-based ligand and pocket representations and spatial graph for interactions, fused early with self-attention.

evidence.quote_or_span: This resulted in seven scFv libraries per chain: three libraries from optimizing the ensemble-based fitness function (namely, En-HC, En-GA and En-Gibbs), three libraries from optimizing the GP-based fitness function (namely, GP-HC, GP-GA, GP-Gibbs), and one PSSM library
For each scFv chain (Ab-14-H variants and Ab-14-L variants), we constructed two fitness functions extrapolated from the ensemble and GP model, respectively.
For each fitness function, we performed optimization using three sampling strategies.

This resulted in 6 libraries per chain: 3 libraries from optimizing the ensemble-based fitness function (namely, En-HC, En-GA and En-Gibbs), and 3 libraries from optimizing the GP-based fitness function (namely, GP-HC, GP-GA, GP-Gibbs).
We then rank-ordered the generated sequences based on their fitness score per library and selected the top 6000 sequences per library for experimental validation.

initial_request.known_context: Candidate antibodies from phage display are often weak binders; the vast search space of antibody sequences makes exhaustive evaluation infeasible.
quantifiable_goals: Engineer scFvs with significantly stronger binding than directed evolution.
Achieve high diversity and success rate in designed libraries.
initial_request.research_problem: Developing a machine learning approach to optimize antibody single-chain variable fragments (scFvs) for high affinity and diversity, outperforming conventional directed evolution.

source.title: Machine learning optimization of candidate antibody yields highly diverse sub-nanomolar affinity antibody libraries

success_verification.final_conclusion: The ML-driven approach, particularly using ensemble models with hill climb or genetic algorithm sampling, successfully engineers highly diverse scFv libraries with sub-nanomolar affinity, significantly outperforming directed evolution (PSSM). The best design achieved a 28.7-fold improvement over PSSM with 94.3% success rate. However, a diversity-affinity trade-off exists, and GP models fail to extrapolate to high-order mutants.
limitations: Cost limitations restricted training data to k=1,2,3 random mutations.
Diversity-affinity trade-off: high diversity (e.g., En-Gibbs) can reduce affinity.
GP model shows poor extrapolation for sequences with ≥6 (heavy) or ≥10 (light) mutations.
metrics: Percent success (fraction of designs with improved binding over Ab-14)
Fold improvement over best PSSM binder
Spearman correlation between predicted and measured affinities
validation_methods: Experimental validation via yeast mating assay
In silico performance metric correlation with actual success (rank correlation 0.8)

R-iLACCO0.2, was subjected to an iterative process of library generation and screening in E. coli. Libraries were generated by site-directed random mutagenesis or error-prone PCR of the whole gene. For each round, approximately 200–400 fluorescent colonies were picked, cultured, and tested on 96-well plates under a plate reader. There were 11 rounds of screening before R-iLACCO1, R-iLACCO1.1, and R-iLACCO1.2 were identified.

Ultimately, we developed the R-iLACCO1 variant with high molecular brightness (11.3 in L-lactate-bound state, Table 2) and $\Delta F/F$ of 20 that is one of the highest fluorescence responses for red fluorescent biosensor protein, as measured using purified protein (Supplementary Table 2) $^{18,31}$ .

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop METIS, a versatile active machine learning workflow with a simple online interface.

evidence.quote_or_span: Optimization of biological networks is often limited by wet lab labor and cost, and the lack of convenient computational tools.
Here, we describe METIS, a versatile active machine learning workflow with a simple online interface for the data-driven optimization of biological targets with minimal experiments.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use a variational autoencoder (VAE) to model sequence co-evolution and sample near-human OTC variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: sequence space is incomprehensibly vast, and methods to map sequence to function (computationally or experimentally) are inaccurate or time-/labor-intensive.

the most effective sequence design methods may need to consider not just position- or pair-wise conservation when making mutations, but also the interactions between all positions in a given sequence alignment.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing methods like consensus design only consider position- or pair-wise conservation, missing higher-order interactions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Capturing higher-order co-evolutionary relationships with a generative model (VAE) will yield more functional variants than consensus approaches.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design and train a VAE on OTC homolog sequences to test the hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The most effective sequence design methods may need to consider interactions between all positions in a sequence alignment.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Vast sequence space and inaccurate/labor-intensive mapping methods hinder therapeutic enzyme engineering.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Build VAE with two convolutional neural networks (encoder and decoder), latent dimension 64, and train with weighted sampling based on similarity to hOTC.

evidence.quote_or_span: we applied variational autoencoders (VAEs), a recent advance from the deep learning literature
we built a large multi-sequence alignment of OTC homologs obtained by BLAST search. After filtering these sequences to remove those that caused the most gaps, we were left with 3818 sequences
Weighted sampling with replacement was used to preferentially select training data for each batch based on the similarity to hOTC.
Our VAE consists of two convolutional neural networks (an encoder and a decoder).
The number of filters in the encoder blocks were 128, 96, and 64 respectively, and the outputs were pooled two-fold in the length dimension only.
We used a latent space of dimension 64.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to construct a VAE that can learn from OTC homolog sequences and generate near-human variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A convolutional VAE trained on 3818 OTC homologs with weighted sampling favoring human-like sequences will generate functional near-human variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that the trained VAE accurately captures sequence statistics.
evolution_trajectory.observation: VAE designed and trained; weighted sampling addresses class imbalance from microbial bias in databases.

parameters.weighted_sampling: inversely proportional to Hamming distance to hOTC

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that VAE can capture higher-order interactions; need to build and train model.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Encode hOTC, sample repeatedly from its encoded distribution, and remove duplicates to create a library of 87 unique near-hOTC variants.

The variants on average contained 8 amino acid substitutions relative to hOTC (Fig. 2C)
To sample near-human variants from the VAE for testing, we first encoded the hOTC sequence, resulting in a set of means and variances.
After removing duplicates, this resulted in the 87 variants tested in this work.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Recombinantly produce all OTC variants, measure specific activity and melting temperature (Tm) via differential scanning fluorimetry.

evidence.quote_or_span: all OTC variants (i.e., both VAE and consensus variants) were recombinantly produced, and activity and thermostability were characterized

comparison of the mean activity between the two libraries (solid black lines) indicates a statistically significant difference (p value <0.0001); the consensus library on average has a mean activity that is 0.96-fold relative to hOTC while the VAE library shows a significant improvement with a mean activity of 1.4-fold relative to hOTC.
all VAE variants exhibited improved thermostability with respect to hOTC

With respect to thermal stability, both consensus and VAE approaches yielded libraries with substantially elevated average melting temperatures: a 8 C° improvement for the consensus library and a 12 C° improvement for the VAE library.
The average melting temperature ( $T_{m}$ ) of the VAE library was 73 °C (solid black line), a 12 °C improvement over hOTC.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform regularized linear regression on 182 variants with 48 predictors; compare activity and stability variances between libraries.

evidence.quote_or_span: We used regularized least squares linear regression to estimate the effects of individual mutations on specific activity and thermostability.

Taken as a whole, however, specific activity and melting temperature do not appear to be globally anti-correlated, as might have been expected if the sequences were close to optimal with respect to both high specific activity and high melting temperature.
The enhanced mean activity observed for the VAE library suggests that the epistatic interactions captured in this library contribute to increased catalytic rate.

However, the observation that the VAE and consensus libraries exhibit comparable variance in activity suggests that the residues involved in epistatic interactions do not dominate in their contributions to catalytic activity.
Our observations include all 87 sampled VAE sequences and 85 of 90 consensus library sequences after some initial filtering to remove entries with null data $n = 182$ .

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering therapeutic enzymes for mRNA therapeutics is challenging due to vast sequence space and inaccurate or labor-intensive mapping methods. The goal is to improve expression, half-life, and catalytic efficiency of ornithine transcarbamylase (OTC) while minimizing mutations to reduce immunogenicity.

source.title: Therapeutic enzyme engineering using a generative neural network

success_verification.final_conclusion: VAE-designed OTC variants show significantly improved specific activity (1.4-fold mean increase) and thermal stability (12°C Tm improvement) over hOTC, with a higher hit rate for combined improvements compared to consensus design. Top variants maintain improved specific activity in mammalian cells despite lower expression. Epistatic interactions captured by VAE contribute to activity, but individual residue effects dominate stability.
limitations: Limited number of variants tested (87 VAE, 91 consensus)
Mechanistic basis of mutations not fully investigated
Lower expression of VAE variants in mammalian cells
validation_methods: Differential scanning fluorimetry for thermal stability
Mammalian cell expression and activity assay

evidence.quote_or_span: In this work, we explore the usage of pharmacophore modeling to identify metal-free dyes for DSSCs by means of virtual screening.

we hypothesized that using the standard pharmacophoric features would account for key properties of active dyes in particular in what pertains to their aromaticity (via the AR feature), hydrophobicity (via the HI feature), and charge distribution (via the NIC, PIC, HBA, and HBA features).

entries with light simulator conditions different from AM 1.5 G 100 Mw cm $^{-2}$ (sun simulator conditions), semiconductors different from $TiO_{2}$ or electrolytes different from iodide/triiodide were also excluded.

initial_request.research_problem: Develop pharmacophore models ('photovoltaphores') to identify metal-free dyes for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) via virtual screening, addressing the need for cost-effective, sustainable sensitizers.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design TRIDENT platform with cytosine deaminase (PmCDA1), engineered adenosine deaminase (yeTadA1.0), and DNA repair factors (Apn2p, Msh6p) targeted via T7 RNAP.

evidence.quote_or_span: mutations are limited to an approximately 40 bp window adjacent to the gRNA target site, mutation of the 20 bp gRNA binding site is inhibited, and mutagenesis is focused to just two or three base pairs

effective diversification of a 1000 bp gene can require more than 20 gRNAs, requiring redesign for each new target and inhibiting mutation at over 400 bp of sequence corresponding to gRNA binding sites

Moore et al. and Álvarez et al. also developed a cytidine deaminase T7 RNA Polymerase targeting system in E. coli, but did not utilize DNA-modifiers to increase mutation diversity and both systems, while more targeted than chemical mutagenesis, still retained significant off-target mutation frequency

TRIDENT consists of up to three components targeted to a locus of interest via T7 RNA polymerase: (1) a cytosine deaminase enzyme that introduces C>T and G>A substitutions, (2) an engineered adenosine deaminase enzyme that introduces A>G and T>C substitutions, and (3) DNA-repair factors that further enhance A/T/G mutational diversity

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to achieve targeted, continual, inducible in vivo mutagenesis with high mutation rates, broad windows, tunable spectra, and minimal off-target effects.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A system mimicking B-cell somatic hypermutation (SHM) by targeting mutagenic enzymes via T7 RNAP could overcome these limitations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test initial implementation of TRIDENT with PmCDA1-T7 RNAP fusion.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TRIDENT is designed to consist of up to three components: cytosine deaminase, adenosine deaminase, and DNA-repair factors, targeted to a locus via T7 RNAP, inspired by SHM.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing in vivo mutagenesis systems (CRISPR-X, EvolvR, TRACE) have limitations: narrow mutation windows (~40 bp), inhibition at gRNA binding sites, fixed mutation bias, lack of tunability, and significant off-target effects.

The improved system further enabled titratable control of on-target mutation rate over a 100-fold range from a LOF rate of $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ to $2.

constraints: Targeted mutagenesis
Inducible control
Minimal off-target effects
Broad mutation window
Tunable mutation spectrum
initial_request.known_context: CRISPR-based targeting systems (CRISPR-X, EvolvR) have narrow mutation windows (~40 bp), inhibit mutation at gRNA binding sites, and have fixed mutation bias. TRACE lacks chemically inducible tunability and has low diversity biased to C/G transitions; Moore et al. and Álvarez et al. systems have significant off-target mutation frequency.
quantifiable_goals: High on:off-target mutation ratio
Broad mutation window (up to 2 kb)
Tunable mutation rate over 100-fold range
Increased mutational diversity beyond C>T and G>A
initial_request.research_problem: Existing in vivo mutagenesis systems have limited mutation windows, require multiple gRNAs, inhibit mutation at binding sites, and have fixed mutation biases. The goal is to develop a targeted, continual, inducible in vivo mutagenesis platform with high mutation rates, broad windows, tunable spectra, and minimal off-target effects.

source.title: Polymerase-guided base editing enables in vivo mutagenesis and rapid protein engineering

success_verification.final_conclusion: TRIDENT is a targeted, continual, inducible in vivo mutagenesis platform with high mutation rates, broad windows (up to 2 kb), tunable spectra, and minimal off-target effects. It successfully evolved a red-shifted mCherry variant and pyrimethamine-resistant PfDHFR, with D54N as the key resistance mutation.
limitations: Bias towards transition mutations
Single T7 terminator may not fully prevent downstream mutagenesis

The binding profiles of h11B11 to five hACE2 variants (S19P, I21T, K26R, N33D, and D38E) in the N-terminal helix (NTH), which may greatly affect the stability of receptors and MAb binding, were further analyzed.
However, the S19P variant exhibited complete resistance to the MAb, revealing the importance of S19 on NTH for h11B11 binding to hACE2.
Meanwhile, we speculate two reasons for the S19P variant eliminating the interaction between hACE2 and MAb h11B11.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a multi-task neural network (TSplice) to predict tissue-specific splicing.

evidence.quote_or_span: MMSplice has been trained in a tissue-agnostic fashion and therefore does not capture effects of variants affecting tissue-specific regulatory elements.
the publicly available SPANR does not allow performing tissue-specific predictions.

A common denominator of these models is that they are trained by multi-task learning, i.e., the models make joint predictions for all tissues or cell types using a common set of underlying predictive features. This strategy allows models to efficiently pool information about regulatory elements that are shared across cell types or tissues.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No model predicts tissue-specific variant effects on splicing.

skeletal muscle and the two heart tissues (left ventrial and atrial appendage) also clustered together with shared splicing patterns.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Train TSplice on ASCOT (excluding chr2,3,5) with KL divergence loss, Adam optimizer, and ensemble of 8 models.

evidence.quote_or_span: TSplice was trained on the ASCOT dataset using all chromosomes except for chromosome 2, 3, and 5.
Missing values, which typically correspond to tissues in which the gene is not expressed, were masked out in the loss function.
After finding the best hyperparameter combination, 20 models were trained with the best hyperparameters but different random initialization.
A forward model selection strategy was used to select a set of models whose average predictions gives the smallest loss on the validation set.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a dual-reporter system with a translation sensor and a folding sensor.

evidence.quote_or_span: heterologous expression of recombinant proteins in bacteria such as E. coli, a valued host for industrial expression of a wide range of peptides and proteins, often results in misfolding, aggregation and degradation.
no system enables simultaneous monitoring of translation and folding at the single cell level.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No system enables simultaneous monitoring of translation and folding at the single cell level.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A dual-reporter system can be developed to simultaneously monitor protein translation and folding at the single-cell level.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design the dual-reporter system.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The problem of low-throughput screening and lack of simultaneous monitoring motivates the design of a dual-reporter system.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Heterologous protein expression in E. coli often results in misfolding, aggregation, and degradation. Existing screening methods are low-throughput and cannot simultaneously monitor translation and folding at the single-cell level.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a dual-reporter system consisting of a translation sensor (translation-coupling cassette with mCherry) and a folding sensor (IbpA promoter driving GFP).

evidence.quote_or_span: we have developed a dual-reporter system that simultaneously monitors protein translation and protein folding at the single cell level (Fig. 1a).

The translation sensor consists of a translation-coupling cassette comprised of a strong secondary mRNA structure formed by a C-terminal hexa-histidine tag, a stop codon for the gene of interest and a ribosome binding site (RBS) for a downstream fluorescent reporter protein, mCherry.
The protein folding sensor is based on the naturally occurring heat shock response system in E. coli.
In our protein folding sensor, the RpoH inducible IbpA promoter is inserted upstream of a GFP reporter gene

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design sensors that can report on translation and folding independently.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A translation-coupling cassette with mCherry can report translation, and an IbpA promoter driving GFP can report folding stress.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize the folding sensor components for better sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The designed system should allow simultaneous monitoring of translation and folding at the single-cell level.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: No system exists for simultaneous monitoring of translation and folding at single-cell level.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test plasmid backbones (pBBR1 vs ColE1) and GFP variants (GFP-mut3 vs GFP-ASV) using heat shock induction.

evidence.quote_or_span: To test whether the copy number affects the lbpAp-GFP activity, we have analyzed the heat shock response from two vector backbones with the pBBR1 or the ColE1 ORI, respectively.
The two ORIs were further tested in combination with two GFP-variants, GFP-mut3 and GFP-ASV.

Monitoring of the heat shock response signals of protein folding sensors (pSEVA441-IbpAp and pSEVA631(Sp)-IbpAp) with different origin of replications (ColE1 and pBBR1, respectively) and GFP variants (GFP-mut3 and GFP-ASV) after induction of the IbpA promoter.
two sharp well-defined peaks were observed from the heat induced and uninduced pBBR1 plasmids with a 3–5-fold increase in the signal-to-noise ratio.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The effect of plasmid copy number and GFP stability on the folding sensor performance is unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using a low-copy plasmid backbone (pBBR1) and an unstable GFP variant (GFP-ASV) will improve the signal-to-noise ratio of the folding sensor.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate the optimized dual-reporter system with model proteins.
evolution_trajectory.observation: pBBR1 with GFP-ASV gave two sharp well-defined peaks with a 3–5-fold increase in signal-to-noise ratio compared to other combinations.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: The folding sensor design needs optimization for dynamic range and sensitivity.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate the dual-reporter system with five model proteins: PARP1-BRCT (soluble), BRCA1-BRCT (insoluble), p19 (soluble), E6 (insoluble), and a control.

evidence.quote_or_span: Overexpression of the insoluble BRCA1-BRCT, however, promoted binding of RpoH to the IbpA promoter region of the folding sensor, resulting in a 5–10-fold increase in GFP-signal compared to PARP1-BRCT.
Different levels of GFP fluorescence were observed for BRCA1-BRCT and E6 although they both were expressed as insoluble protein.
The results demonstrate that it is possible to combine both biosensors to simultaneously investigate translation and proper folding of proteins in E. coli.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the dual-reporter system can distinguish between soluble and insoluble proteins and report on their translation and folding status.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Soluble proteins will show high mCherry and low GFP, while insoluble proteins will show low mCherry and high GFP.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test solubility tags to improve solubility and further validate the system.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Soluble proteins gave high mCherry/low GFP; insoluble proteins gave low mCherry/high GFP, but GFP levels varied between insoluble proteins (BRCA1-BRCT showed 5–10-fold increase in GFP signal compared to PARP1-BRCT).
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Optimized dual-reporter system ready for validation with model proteins.

evidence.quote_or_span: The N-terminally linked solubility tags did not have a large impact on the translation level of PARP1-BRCT, BRCA1-BRCT, and E6, which all expressed also without the tags, and the SUMO tag even decreased the expression level of BRCA1-BRCT.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether solubility tags can improve solubility and reduce GFP signal for insoluble proteins.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate a random mutant library of PARP1-BRCT (1–3 mutations per protein) and screen by FACS for high mCherry and high GFP.

evidence.quote_or_span: We thus demonstrate the use of the dual-reporter system to screen for variants with either reduced or increased stability in a high-throughput mode.

a randomly generated mutant library of pET22-PARP1-BRCT-mCherry was expressed with the protein folding sensor pSEVA631(Sp)-IbpAp-GFP-ASV to demonstrate the applicability of the dual-reporter system to screen for new variants with correct translation but altered stability.

The mutant library was prepared using the error-prone DNA polymerase Mutazyme II that provides a minimal mutational bias. By adjusting the amount of initial target DNA and the number of gene duplications, a mutation rate of 1–3 amino acid substitutions per protein was achieved.

The cell cultures were sorted using FACS for high mCherry signal (P1) alone and for both high mCherry signal (P1) and high GFP signal (P2). Gate 1 (P1) was defined as a mCherry signal higher than the control plasmid background, to ensure that only cells expressing correctly translated proteins were collected. Gate 2 (P2) was defined as the upper 1% of cells with the highest GFP fluorescence, in order to select for variants expressing proteins with decreased stability.

initial_request.known_context: E. coli is a valued host for industrial expression of peptides and proteins, but heterologous expression often results in misfolding, aggregation, and degradation.

initial_request.research_problem: Heterologous protein expression in E. coli often leads to misfolding, aggregation, and degradation. Existing screening methods are low-throughput and cannot simultaneously monitor translation and folding at the single-cell level.

source.title: A dual-reporter system for investigating and optimizing protein translation and folding in E. coli

success_verification.final_conclusion: The dual-reporter system successfully enables simultaneous monitoring of protein translation and folding at the single-cell level, allowing high-throughput screening of protein variants. It can detect misfolded proteins and differentiate mutation types, but GFP signal is not comparable between unrelated proteins, and a downstream activity assay is needed for enzyme activity.
limitations: GFP fluorescence may not be comparable between unrelated proteins due to varying DnaK binding sites.
A downstream activity assay is needed to ensure enzyme activity.
Suppressor screening for single nucleotide substitutions may be confounded by reversion to wild type.

initial_request.known_context: D2-BGL is a GH3 β-glucosidase from a metagenomic library with high substrate affinity but suffers from substrate inhibition and low recombinant production.
quantifiable_goals: Increase specific activity at high cellobiose concentration
Increase recombinant enzyme production in P. pastoris
Improve glucose release from sugarcane bagasse
initial_request.research_problem: Improve catalytic efficiency, substrate inhibition tolerance, and recombinant production of the fungal GH3 β-glucosidase D2-BGL for biomass saccharification via directed evolution.

source.title: Improvements of the productivity and saccharification efficiency of the cellulolytic β-glucosidase D2-BGL in Pichia pastoris via directed evolution

success_verification.final_conclusion: Triple mutant Mut M (F256M+Y260D+D224G) combines enhanced activity, reduced substrate inhibition, and increased production, leading to up to 57% more glucose release from sugarcane bagasse at high biomass loading, but with increased glucose inhibition.
limitations: Increased glucose inhibition (Ki_glu 1.59 vs 3.23 mM) may limit performance in high-glucose environments
Mechanism of D224G expression enhancement not fully elucidated
Only tested on one biomass type (sugarcane bagasse)

initial_request.research_problem: Identifying and characterizing synergistic stabilizing mutations in CI2 using a bacterial folding sensor, overcoming limited dynamic range for stabilized variants.

source.title: Synergistic stabilization of a double mutant in chymotrypsin inhibitor 2 from a library screen in E. coli

success_verification.final_conclusion: L49I/I57V is a synergistic double mutant that stabilizes CI2 beyond wild-type through relief of unfavorable interactions and entropic effects from alternative Ile49 conformations. Computational methods fail to predict this synergy.
limitations: Error-prone PCR library had limited diversity; most codons one base substitution from wild-type.
Folding sensor dynamic range still limited; I57V nearly minimizes GFP signal, hindering selection of further stabilized variants.
metrics: ΔΔGf (change in stability relative to wild-type)

success_verification.final_conclusion: A scalable P. pastoris whole-cell catalyst for CYP2C9 was developed using bidirectional promoter tuning, optimized bioreactor cultivation at pH 5 with methanol co-feeding, achieving 40% ibuprofen conversion with high selectivity and outperforming fission yeast by 55% higher STY and 21% higher specific production rate.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a multi-stage computational pipeline with increasing complexity.

evidence.quote_or_span: The main drawback resides in its random nature and in the limited sequence exploration; both require screening of thousands (if not millions) of variants to achieve a target function.

The cornerstone of our technology is the combination of bioinformatics with protein design algorithms and empirical/physics-based simulations that model the interactions between the substrate and the enzyme. Such simulations follow a funnel like scheme with increasing complexity to gradually filter enzyme candidates (Fig. 1).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Random mutagenesis and limited sequence exploration lead to high screening burden.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A computational funnel-like pipeline combining bioinformatics, protein design, and molecular modeling can gradually filter enzyme candidates and reduce the number of variants needing experimental testing.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Implement the first stage: bioinformatics and molecular modeling.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The pipeline is designed to combine bioinformatics, protein design algorithms, and empirical/physics-based simulations in a funnel-like scheme with increasing complexity: implicit solvent, explicit solvent MD, and QM.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Directed evolution requires screening thousands to millions of variants.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply bioinformatics diagnosis (sequence analysis, dynamics, docking, migration) and mutagenesis screening with Rosetta/FoldX.

evidence.quote_or_span: In the second step, mutagenesis, millions of enzyme variants are modeled and screened with an in-house protocol that includes proprietary and third-party software like Rosetta $[21]$ and FoldX $[22]$ .
In such a way, only residues with enough entropy are retained for the final library and consensus mutations are suggested to stabilize the protein $[12]$ .

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to diagnose enzyme and screen millions of variants for binding and catalysis.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using Rosetta and FoldX with binding energy and catalytic distance metrics can effectively filter variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Variants passing this stage proceed to MD filtering.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Millions of enzyme variants are modeled and screened; only residues with enough entropy are retained for the final library and consensus mutations are suggested to stabilize the protein.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Pipeline design established; first stage is bioinformatics and molecular modeling.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply HTMD to filter variants based on NAC populations.

evidence.quote_or_span: This technique proved to be successful in tuning selectivity with high-throughput MD (HTMD) $[24]$ .

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to account for conformational dynamics and near attack conformations (NACs).
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: High-throughput MD (HTMD) with many short simulations can filter variants by population of NACs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: MD stage has a limitation: requires a crystal structure; explore alternatives. Also, for reactions involving electron rearrangements, proceed to QM.
evolution_trajectory.observation: HTMD proved successful in tuning selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Variants filtered by bioinformatics stage.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate Monte Carlo simulations as an alternative to MD for NAC assessment.

evidence.quote_or_span: MD is often not a viable solution in industrial applications because of the lack of a suitable crystal structure. Currently, we are evaluating alternative ways to account for NACs, based on Monte Carlo simulations.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: MD is often not viable without a suitable crystal structure.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Alternative Monte Carlo-based methods can account for NACs without requiring a crystal structure.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Continue to QM stage for reactions involving electron rearrangements.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Currently evaluating alternative ways to account for NACs based on Monte Carlo simulations.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: MD stage applied, but requires crystal structure.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation of traditional QM and develop a shortcut method.

evidence.quote_or_span: Unfortunately, traditional techniques are as slow as informative so they are not suited to screen hundreds of variants $[8]$ .

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Traditional QM techniques are not suited for screening hundreds of variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A faster QM-based scoring method is needed to enable screening of hundreds of variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Develop a QM shortcut using spin density scoring.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Traditional QM techniques are as slow as informative, not suited for screening hundreds of variants.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Traditional QM is too slow for screening hundreds of variants.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a QM shortcut using spin density scoring.

evidence.quote_or_span: We developed a scoring method based on the total spin density localized on the substrate after five geometry optimization steps (the electron density roughly converged in that time) and a distance-dependent dielectric potential.
With today's cloud computing facilities and better hardware, $\sim500$ variants can be tested in 1 day for less than €500.
This passes through calculating properties, instead of TS energies, that: (1) correlate with activity; (2) converge much faster than energy during geometry optimization.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a faster QM-based scoring method for activity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Spin density after five geometry optimization steps correlates with activity for laccases and can serve as a fast scoring method.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: QM shortcut addresses speed limitation but may be system-specific; need to analyze generalizability.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The method enables screening of ~500 variants/day for less than €500. It calculates properties that correlate with activity and converge faster than energy.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Traditional QM is too slow; need a faster method.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Conduct ML analysis on two datasets: full mutagenesis study of a small protein and activity data of 96 ligands against 16 esterases.

evidence.quote_or_span: This poses a serious question on the actual knowledge of how enzymes work and whether the key parameters to label activity are actually family and reaction dependent.

These doubts were confirmed by ML analysis that we conducted on two data sets: a full mutagenesis study for a small protein ( $\sim$ 60 residues) $[33]$ and the activity data of 96 ligands against 16 esterases $[34]$ .

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unclear whether key parameters for activity are universal or family/reaction dependent.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ML analysis on diverse datasets can reveal whether molecular features correlate with activity across systems.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Findings motivate gathering family-specific data and developing family-dependent ML models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: No single molecular feature strongly correlates with activity across systems; features are family and reaction dependent.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Need for better predictive models across enzyme families.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Plan to gather extensive data for key enzyme families.

evidence.quote_or_span: Therefore, we aim at gathering thousands of data points for key enzyme families to conduct further research and develop family-dependent models.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Lack of sufficient data for key enzyme families to build predictive models.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Gathering thousands of data points for key enzyme families will enable development of family-dependent ML models.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Also address long-range mutation detection, another limitation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Aim to gather thousands of data points for key enzyme families to conduct further research and develop family-dependent models.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: ML analysis shows family-dependent activity features.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation and initiate development of new tools.

evidence.quote_or_span: no reliable tool seems to be available for their quick detection.
Despite this success of MD in characterizing long range mutation effects $[37–41]$ , no reliable tool seems to be available for their quick detection.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No reliable tool available for quick detection of long-range mutation effects.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Current tools are insufficient; new methods are needed to quickly identify long-range mutation hot spots.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Develop tools to quickly detect long-range mutation hot spots and predict amino acid substitutions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Despite MD success in characterizing long-range mutation effects, no reliable tool exists for quick detection.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Long-range mutations can significantly affect activity but are hard to detect.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop tools that do not involve long MD, aiming to predict hot spots and amino acid substitutions in minutes.

evidence.quote_or_span: In our company we are developing tools that do not involve long and resource intensive MD simulations, shrinking the prediction time for hot spots from weeks to minutes.
Moreover, we aim at not only singling out hot spots but actually predict the correct amino acid substitutions.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to shrink prediction time from weeks to minutes and predict correct amino acid substitutions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: New tools that avoid long MD simulations can quickly detect hot spots and predict substitutions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Future work to validate these tools.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Developing tools that shrink prediction time from weeks to minutes and aim to predict correct amino acid substitutions.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: No quick tool for long-range mutation detection.

source.title: Computational Enzyme Design at Zymvol

success_verification.final_conclusion: Zymvol's computational pipeline reduces the screening burden of directed evolution by combining bioinformatics, MD, and QM. A QM shortcut enables fast activity scoring for laccases. ML analysis reveals family-dependent activity features, motivating future family-specific models. Tools for long-range mutation detection are under development.
limitations: MD requires crystal structure; Monte Carlo alternative under evaluation
QM shortcut may be system-specific (demonstrated for oxidoreductases)
No universal activity features; ML models need family-specific data
Long-range mutation detection tools not yet validated
metrics: Number of variants screened computationally vs. experimentally
Speed and cost of QM shortcut
validation_methods: Experimental testing of top-ranked variants from pipeline

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design MutPred2 to output a general pathogenicity score and a ranked list of molecular alterations.

evidence.quote_or_span: they remain inadequate to the task in exome-scale applications owing to a less than optimal balance of false-positive and true-positive detection rates
they do not generate actionable hypotheses regarding the molecular consequences of these variants
MutPred2 is a machine learning-based method, and software package that integrates genetic and molecular data to reason probabilistically about the pathogenicity of amino acid substitutions.
This is achieved by providing (1) a general pathogenicity prediction, and (2) a ranked list of specific molecular alterations potentially affecting the phenotype.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for a method that balances false-positive and true-positive rates and provides actionable molecular mechanism hypotheses.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A machine learning method integrating genetic and molecular data can improve pathogenicity prediction and provide mechanistic insights.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Develop sequence-based predictors for structural and functional properties to enable mechanistic predictions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MutPred2 is designed to address the shortcomings of existing methods.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing pathogenicity predictors have high false-positive rates and do not provide molecular mechanism hypotheses.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop over 50 predictors with a common feature set, output scores between 0 and 1, and organize into an ontology.

evidence.quote_or_span: we developed sequence-based predictors for over 50 structural and functional protein properties

All predictors, with minor exceptions, were trained with a common feature set, subjected to the same evaluation protocols, designed to output scores between 0 and 1, and implemented within the positive-unlabeled machine learning framework
we constructed a custom ontology of molecular alterations by grouping the properties into broader categories
over 50 protein property predictors were developed within a unified positive-unlabeled learning framework

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to predict a wide range of structural and functional property changes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Over 50 sequence-based property predictors can be developed within a positive-unlabeled learning framework to cover diverse molecular alterations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Train the pathogenicity model using these property predictors and variant data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Predictors were developed and organized into a custom ontology of molecular alterations.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: MutPred2 design requires predictors for molecular alterations.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Train the model on the specified dataset using a bagging approach.

evidence.quote_or_span: The MutPred2 pathogenicity model was trained on a set of 53,180 pathogenic and 206,946 unlabeled (putatively neutral) variants
We found that filtering the training set is beneficial only when the test set is also filtered, using the same criteria.
Furthermore, using the entire unfiltered training set resulted in comparable or better performance in most cases
we chose not to perform any filtering in subsequent steps

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to train a model that distinguishes pathogenic from neutral variants using positive-unlabeled data.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A bagged ensemble of feed-forward neural networks trained on 53,180 pathogenic and 206,946 unlabeled variants can accurately predict pathogenicity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate model performance via cross-validation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Model trained; evaluation of training set filtering criteria showed no filtering was optimal.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Property predictors are ready; need to train pathogenicity model.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform per-protein tenfold cross-validation and estimate label noise.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using a per-protein tenfold cross-validation, the AUC was estimated at 87.7%
We estimate the proportion of noisy positive variants to be 2.8% and the proportion of unlabeled variants that are pathogenic to be 5.8%
These results allow us to provide a corrected estimate of the AUC of 91.3%

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to estimate model performance and account for label noise.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cross-validation will yield an AUC around 87.7%, and label noise estimation will provide a corrected AUC.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate on independent test set to confirm generalizability.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AUC was 87.7%; estimated 2.8% noisy positives and 5.8% pathogenic in unlabeled; corrected AUC 91.3%.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model trained without filtering.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate MutPred2 and other methods on an independent test set.

evidence.quote_or_span: MutPred2 still had the highest AUC (87.1%), largely attributable to its high sensitivities at lower false-positive rates (FPRs).
CADD was more sensitive only when the FPR was high (between 20 and 30%).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to compare MutPred2 with existing methods on an independent dataset.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: MutPred2 will achieve the highest AUC on the independent set, especially at low false-positive rates.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze score distributions on healthy genomes to assess calibration.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MutPred2 had the highest AUC (87.1%), outperforming CADD at lower FPRs; CADD was more sensitive only when the FPR was high (between 20 and 30%).
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cross-validation shows promising performance.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze score distributions on healthy genomes and correlate with minor allele frequencies.

evidence.quote_or_span: MutPred2 outputs a generally decreasing score distribution (0.3% of variants with scores >0.9), that we believe is better suited for the interpretation of personalized genome-scale data
MutPred2 scores showed a stronger negative correlation with MAFs

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to assess score distribution characteristics and correlation with allele frequencies.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: MutPred2 scores will show a decreasing distribution with few high scores and strong negative correlation with MAF, making them suitable for genome-scale interpretation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate enriched molecular mechanisms in Mendelian disease mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MutPred2 outputs a decreasing score distribution (0.3% of variants with scores >0.9) and shows stronger negative correlation with MAFs compared to other methods.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: MutPred2 shows strong discriminative performance.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform enrichment analysis of molecular mechanisms in Mendelian disease mutations versus unlabeled variants.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design replication-incompetent AAV libraries to enable safe in vivo selection.

evidence.quote_or_span: Efficient adeno-associated virus-mediated (AAV-mediated) gene delivery remains a significant obstacle to effective retinal gene therapies.

To mitigate the possibility of replication of the AAV libraries in the primate retina and subsequent spread, a rep in trans strategy was developed in which the library rep sequence is mutated (pRepSafeStop) while leaving intact regulatory elements, including the P40 promoter, which is necessary for cap protein expression.
Stop codons in the rep sequence prevented the expression of the 4 Rep proteins, and the full rep sequence was supplied on pRepIntronHelper.
an intron was inserted in the rep supplied in trans to prevent packaging of recombined genomes

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing AAV variants do not efficiently transduce outer retinal cells from the vitreous in primates.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Safe library design enables construction and packaging for primate screen.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Replication-incompetent libraries prevent replication and spread while allowing cap expression.

Libraries were pooled and injected intravitreally in primates.
After AAV infection had occurred, retinal tissue and RPE cells were collected, and cap gene variants were PCR amplified, recloned, and repackaged for the subsequent round of injection.
Five rounds of selection were performed, and error-prone PCR was performed after the third round to introduce additional diversity into the library.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform deep sequencing and analyze selection dynamics.

evidence.quote_or_span: analysis of results from deep sequencing over the rounds of selection revealed a subset of variants that increased significantly in their representation during rounds of selection for each of the input libraries

Deep sequencing to gain insights into selection dynamics revealed that the variants with the greatest fold increase during selection, rather than the most frequent final variant, is the optimal metric for identifying top-performing variants.

Overrepresentation of variants in the original library significantly influenced the number of clones in the final round of selection, with variants that were highly represented in the original library more likely to constitute a majority of clones in the final round and mask more promising variants that were less abundant at the start but that had a greater fold increase over rounds of selection.
Deep sequencing of libraries revealed convergence of variants over rounds of selection.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which variants were enriched and what metric best identifies top performers?

evolution_trajectory.decision: Select 12 variants plus controls for barcoded screen in a single primate; NHP#9 (AAV2-LALIQDSMRA) and NHP#26 (AAV2 583 LQRGVRIPSVLEVNGQ) chosen for individual validation based on fold increase.

evidence.quote_or_span: Twelve variants from the three successfully amplified libraries were chosen for a secondary round of selection with GFP-barcoded libraries, along with parental serotypes as controls.

Based on their increase in representation relative to the original library, individual variant capsids were chosen and used to package a scCAG-eGFP genome also containing a unique DNA barcode sequence.
These barcoded vectors were then pooled in equal amounts and injected intravitreally.
Retinal cells (photoreceptors or RPE cells) were harvested, GFP barcodes were PCR amplified from the collected tissue, and deep sequencing was used to quantify the relative abundance of barcodes.

The top-ranking variant from the GFP-barcoded screen, LoopSwap variant \~583-LQRGVRIPSV-LEVNGQ (designated NHP#26), was also tested for validation despite the limitation that reduced production of this vector enabled only a low dose.
The top-ranking vector, LoopSwap variant AAV2 583\~LQRGVRIPSVLEVNGQ, outperformed other variants in the GFP-barcoded screen, though it yielded lower viral titers ( $\sim5 \times 10^{11}$ vg/mL).

The top-ranking variant from the GFP-barcoded screen, LoopSwap variant \~583-LQRGVRIPSV-LEVNGQ (designated NHP#26), was also tested for validation despite the limitation that reduced production of this vector enabled only a low dose.

evidence.quote_or_span: Analysis was performed on n = 1 primate.
Further work is required to determine an optimal regimen and the period during which immune suppression may be required, or if immune suppression may be tapered off after an initial critical period.

To prevent any immune response to GFP or viral vectors, which have been previously reported (7), the monkey used for fluorophore expression received daily subcutaneous injections of cyclosporine at a dose of 6 mg/kg for immune suppression, beginning 1 week before AAV injection, and the dosage regimen was adjusted based on blood trough levels to within a 150–200 ng/ml target range.
The variant NHP#26 transduced the outer retina at a titer of $5 \times 10^{10}$ vg, 2 log units lower than the dosage used in a previous study to inject a primate with 7m8 (7).

Intravitreal injection is a preferred route but is limited by anatomical barriers. Previous murine variant 7m8 showed some success but limited translation to primates.

source.title: In vivo–directed evolution of adeno-associated virus in the primate retina

success_verification.final_conclusion: In vivo directed evolution in primates yielded AAV variants NHP#9 and NHP#26 with improved outer retinal transduction. NHP#9 showed 2-fold more foveal cones and ~12-fold higher GFP expression vs 7m8, with reduced RGC transduction. NHP#26 transduced photoreceptors and Müller cells despite low titer. Limitations include small sample size, immune suppression, and low production titers for some variants.
limitations: Secondary screen performed on n=1 primate
Immune suppression used may affect translatability
NHP#26 had low production titer (~5e11 vg/mL)
Long-term expression and safety not assessed

initial_request.research_problem: Screening large strain libraries generated by SCRaMbLE for improved biosynthesis of metabolites like betulinic acid is bottlenecked by the low throughput of standard LC-MS, which takes ~5 min per sample, making it impractical to find rare (~1%) improved strains.

CRISPR validation confirmed that a TIR1 3' UTR deletion in BC11 caused a threefold increase in BA biosynthesis.
limitations: No obvious rearrangement detected for three of the top 12 strains, suggesting natural mutations elsewhere may have caused improvements.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a transposon-based method to generate random in-frame InDel libraries.

evidence.quote_or_span: insertions or deletions (InDels) remain an overlooked source of variation despite their frequent and functionally beneficial occurrence in natural protein evolution
