evolution_trajectory.state_before: Directed evolution relies on point substitutions; InDels are underexplored.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design TransDel for deletions and TransIns for insertions.

evidence.quote_or_span: TransDel carries a selection marker (resistance gene against chloramphenicol; CamR) enabling the recovery of in vitro transposition products after transformation into E. coli.
Mlyl digestion removes TransDel together with 8 bp of the target DNA (4 bp at each end), leaving blunt ends and resulting in the removal of a contiguous 3 bp sequence from the target DNA
blunt-ended cassettes Del2 or Del3 are ligated into the gap left upon TransDel removal for the generation of 6 and 9 bp deletions, respectively.
TransDel is an asymmetric transposon with Mlyl at one end and Notl at the other end.
cloni® 10G cells subsequently plated on LB-agar-amp, yielding libraries of wtPTE variants with +3, +6 or +9 bp random insertions.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to generate deletions of 3, 6, 9 bp and insertions of +3, +6, +9 bp.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Transposon-based cassettes with restriction sites can create precise in-frame InDels.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Generate libraries for wtPTE.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TransDel enables removal of 3 bp; Del2/Del3 enable 6 and 9 bp deletions. TransIns enables +3, +6, +9 bp insertions.

parameters.deletion_cassette: TransDel with MlyI sites, CamR marker; Del2/Del3 with KanR
parameters.insertion_cassette: TransIns with NotI/MlyI ends; Ins1/Ins2/Ins3 with randomized triplets and AcuI sites

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No method for random in-frame InDels of multiple lengths.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate six libraries (-3, -6, -9, +3, +6, +9 bp) and validate by Sanger and deep sequencing.

evidence.quote_or_span: validated by generating libraries of InDel variants of Brevundimonas diminuta phosphotriesterase (wtPTE)
All 121 Sanger-sequenced variants displayed only a single modification from the initial transposon insertion, without any incidental mutations
Good coverage of possible positions in the insertion libraries translates into high diversity at most positions in wtPTE
10 or more distinct DNA insertions were observed between 66% (+3 bp) and 80% (+6 and +9 bp libraries) of positions

To demonstrate that TRIAD libraries allow access to functional innovation via adaptive InDels, all the libraries generated from the full-length wtPTE gene (six libraries in total: -3, -6, -9, +3, +6 and +9 bp) were subjected to two parallel screening campaigns to identify variants with enhanced arylesterase activity against either 4-nitrophenyl butyrate (4-NPB) or 2-naphthyl hexanoate (2-NH) (Fig.
Six independent libraries of wtPTE InDel variants were generated, comprising three deletion (-3, -6 and -9 bp) and three insertion libraries (+3, +6 and +9 bp).

evolution_trajectory.observation: Libraries generated; Sanger sequencing of 121 variants confirmed single modifications without incidental mutations. Deep sequencing showed >85% position coverage and high diversity.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure PTE and AE activities of hundreds of variants from TRIAD and TriNEx libraries.

evidence.quote_or_span: the levels of native phosphotriesterase (PTE; substrate: paraoxon; Fig. 5) and promiscuous arylesterase (AE; substrate: 4-nitrophenyl butyrate, 4-NPB; Fig. 5) activities were determined for several hundred wtPTE variants from each TRIAD library and from a trinucleotide substitution library (TriNEx library; Fig. 6; Supplementary Tables S8-9).
83% of deletions and 77% of insertions are strongly deleterious (<0.1 PTE activity), compared with only 24% in the substitution library
while the average activity of TRIAD variants is more compromised, a larger proportion has successfully adapted for the activity
The effect of InDels is on average more deleterious than substitutions (Fig. 6a), while the fraction of hits is increased in InDel libraries (Fig. 6b)

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose that InDels expand accessible sequence space and evolutionary outcomes.

evidence.quote_or_span: InDel mutagenesis and selection puts us in a position to address the question whether alternative initial mutations would enable access to different evolutionary trajectories leading towards the same functional outcome
Based on the hypothesis that the use of a wider genetic and functional diversification (i.e., by both substitutions and InDels) might lead to a wider diversity of possible evolutionary trajectories
The effect of InDels is on average more deleterious than substitutions (Fig. 6a), while the fraction of hits is increased in InDel libraries (Fig. 6b)

We further observed that four of these adaptive InDels increase arylesterase activity 20- to 35-fold (in $k_{cat}/K_{M}$ ) against 2-NH, which is more than the 2.6-fold difference brought about by the initial H254R mutation from the previous directed evolution $^{21}$ .

initial_request.known_context: InDels are frequent and beneficial in natural protein evolution but overlooked in laboratory evolution.
quantifiable_goals: Generate diverse InDel libraries
Compare fitness effects of InDels vs. point substitutions

initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution typically uses point substitutions, but insertions and deletions (InDels) are common in natural evolution and may access unexplored sequence space. Existing InDel methods have limitations: high frameshift rates, fixed lengths, or only deletions. The goal is to develop a transposon-based method (TRIAD) to generate random in-frame InDel libraries and explore their functional effects on phosphotriesterase (wtPTE), comparing to point mutations.

source.title: Accessing unexplored regions of sequence space in directed enzyme evolution via insertion/deletion mutagenesis

success_verification.final_conclusion: TRIAD generates diverse in-frame InDel libraries. InDels are more deleterious than substitutions but more likely to yield improved arylesterase variants, with distinct mechanisms (kcat vs. KM) and potential for alternative evolutionary trajectories.
limitations: Undersampling of +6 and +9 bp libraries
Adjacent substitutions in 39% of InDels
Limited to in-frame modifications

evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose to remove the cap by mutation to make space for the 5' G-extension.

evidence.quote_or_span: most of the increased fidelity nucleases can routinely be used only with fully matching 20-nucleotide-long spacers (20G-sgRNAs)
they possess diminished activity with 5'-extended sgRNAs
We proposed that it might result from a capping of the 5' end of the sgRNA by Glu1007 and Tyr1013, which are connected via a surface loop
removing the cap by mutation would make space for a 5' G-extension of the sgRNA without clashing with the polypeptide chain

the incorporation of Blackjack mutations into an increased fidelity variant of SpCas9 that is not fully compatible with 5' modified sgRNAs and using it either alone or in combination with a genome- or epigenome-editor would considerable increase its available target space by making them compatible with 21G-sgRNAs.

initial_request.research_problem: SLE is a heterogeneous autoimmune disease with a strong genetic component, but common GWAS variants explain only a modest fraction of risk. The study investigates whether rare coding variants in known SLE-risk genes contribute to disease, focusing on functional consequences in BLK and BANK1.

source.title: Functional rare and low frequency variants in BLK and BANK1 contribute to human lupus

success_verification.final_conclusion: Rare coding variants in BLK and BANK1 contribute to SLE by impairing kinase activity and IFNβ repression, with epistatic interactions. BLK variants from patients, but not controls, fail to repress IFNβ. BANK1 W40C is a loss-of-function variant that promotes type I IFN signaling by altering TRAF6 localization and IRF5 activation.
limitations: Mouse model with Blk R125W did not show increased type I IFN response, possibly due to species differences or environmental factors.
Incomplete penetrance of autoimmunity in heterozygous individuals suggests additional genetic or environmental factors.
Functional assays primarily in overexpression systems (HEK293T) may not fully recapitulate endogenous B-cell context.
metrics: Percentage of cells with inclusion bodies
validation_methods: CRISPR-Cas9 edited Ramos B-cell line homozygous for BLK R131W

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering a more robust 5-hydroxymethylfurfural oxidase (HMFO) for industrial FDCA production by enhancing thermostability while maintaining activity.

source.title: Creating a more robust 5-hydroxymethylfurfural oxidase by combining computational predictions with a novel effective library design

success_verification.final_conclusion: 7xHMFO is a highly thermostable scaffold (Tm_app 60.5 °C) with WT-like activity; 8BxHMFO achieves >95% FDCA conversion in 9 h at 40 °C due to improved operational stability, despite lower catalytic efficiency. Golden Gate gene shuffling efficiently combined stabilizing mutations, advancing the FRESCO protocol.
limitations: Trade-off: activity-enhancing mutations reduce thermostability; 8BxHMFO has lower kcat/Km than the double mutant.

limitations: Some variants (e.g., T243S, S182R, P9S/K27Q/T162I) showed reduced thermostability.

evidence.quote_or_span: To rebuild real scFv clones from the virtual HCDR3 sequences in the clusters, we performed two-step linker PCR
For each cluster, 1–5 HCDR3 sequences newly identified from the fourth round of bio-panning via NGS analysis were selected arbitrarily (Table 4).
These selected sequences were used to synthesize the primers to retrieve the whole scFv gene from the phagemid DNA.
The scFv gene was prepared in two-step linker PCR using the primers and cloned into a phagemid vector (Figure 3).
Of the 40 clones retrieved from the phagemid DNA pool prepared after the fourth round of bio-panning, positive binding reactivity was confirmed in 26 clones.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Simulate directed evolution on GB1 landscape comparing ML, single-mutant walk, and recombination with 570 variants each.

evidence.quote_or_span: we compare the final fitnesses reached by simulated directed evolution with and without machine learning, based on testing the same number of variants

Assuming each amino acid occurs with equal frequency and that the library has complete coverage, applying the 3-fold oversampling rule to obtain roughly 95% library coverage (18, 19) results in a total of 570 variants screened (SI Appendix, Library Coverage).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does ML-assisted evolution outperform traditional methods under equal screening burden?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ML-assisted evolution will reach higher fitness than single-mutant walk or recombination when testing the same number of variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze results: frequency of reaching global optimum.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Simulations compared final fitnesses reached by ML-assisted evolution, single-mutant walk, and recombination with equal screening burden.
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Computational setup ready.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate percentage of simulations reaching global optimum for each method.

evidence.quote_or_span: machine learning-assisted evolution reaches the global optimum fitness value in 8.2% of 600 simulations, compared to 4.9% of all starting sequences reaching the same value through a single-mutant walk and 4.0% of simulated recombination runs.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Quantify advantage of ML over traditional methods in reaching global optimum.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ML-assisted evolution reaches global optimum more often.

evidence.quote_or_span: the machine-learning approach requires about 30% fewer variants to achieve final results similar to the single-mutant walk with this analysis.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Round 1: Train models on 124 sequence-function pairs, predict libraries, screen 90 variants each for (S)- and (R)-enantiomers.

evidence.quote_or_span: From 124 sequence–function relationships sampled randomly, models were trained to predict a restricted set of selective variants.

From the predicted libraries for both enantiomers, two variants, called VCHV (93 % ee) and GSSG (62 % ee) for their amino acids at positions 32, 46, 56, and 97, were identified by screening 90 variants for each.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can ML-assisted evolution improve enzyme enantioselectivity experimentally?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Training on random sequence-function pairs can predict libraries with improved enantioselectivity.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified VCHV (93% ee (S)) and GSSG (62% ee (R)).
evolution_trajectory.state_before: Method validated on GB1 simulations.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Round 2: Train models on new data, predict libraries, screen variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: From those predicted libraries, we obtained two variants with measured enantioselectivities of 93% and 79% ee for the (S)- and (R)-enantiomers, respectively.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can further improvement be achieved by recombining beneficial mutations?

evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation: VCHV less selective in final validation.

evidence.quote_or_span: VCHV was the most selective variant in the initial screen, but was less selective in final validation.
However, the 49P 51V 53I variant from VCHV has higher total activity under screening conditions.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: VCHV's selectivity decreased in final validation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Initial screen may overestimate selectivity due to screening conditions.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze amino acid tolerance at position 49.

evidence.quote_or_span: proline is conserved at residue 49 in two of the most (S)-selective variants.
tyrosine and arginine are also tolerated at position 49 with less than 1% loss in enantioselectivity.
This suggests that there are diverse solutions in protein space for specific properties

evidence.quote_or_span: While predictive accuracy could be improved by more computationally-expensive simulations or by collecting more data for machine learning, improved variants can already be identified by sampling from a space predicted to be dense in higher fitness variants.

constraints: Equal screening burden compared to traditional directed evolution methods
Use of shallow neural networks
Training on random samples of combinatorial libraries
input_data: GB1 fitness landscape (149,361 out of 160,000 variants)
initial_request.known_context: Combinatorial sequence space is expensive to sample experimentally. Machine learning models trained on tested variants can provide a fast method for testing sequence space computationally.
quantifiable_goals: Reach global optimum fitness more often than single-mutant walk or recombination
Reduce number of variants needed for similar results
Achieve high enantioselectivity in enzyme variants
initial_request.research_problem: Directed protein evolution is limited by the high experimental cost of screening large combinatorial libraries. The paper proposes integrating machine learning to predict protein fitness in silico, reducing the number of variants that need to be tested experimentally.
initial_request.target_object: Protein fitness landscape (GB1 binding protein) and enzyme enantioselectivity (Rma NOD)

source.title: Machine learning-assisted directed protein evolution with combinatorial libraries

success_verification.final_conclusion: Machine learning-assisted directed evolution can efficiently explore combinatorial sequence space, reaching higher fitness with fewer variants than traditional methods, and successfully engineered enantioselective enzymes.
limitations: VCHV selectivity decreased in final validation
Predictive accuracy could be improved with more data or simulations
metrics: Percentage of simulations reaching global optimum
validation_methods: Simulated directed evolution on GB1 fitness landscape
Experimental validation with enzyme enantioselectivity assays
